**Mechanism:** dorsal angulation of the distal radius alters the geometry between radius and ulna, tightening the IOM and creating **DRUJ incongruency** → both max pronation and supination are restricted.

**Biomechanics data:**
- **Saito et al.:** significant change in DRUJ stiffness at **10° and 20°** dorsal tilt (in pronation); none at neutral tilt → **correct dorsal angulation to <10°.**
- **Kihara et al.:** DRUJ incongruency worsens with increasing dorsal tilt, **most dramatic beyond ~20°** (≈10° of dorsal tilt of the articular surface from the normal 10–11° volar tilt); increased dorsal angulation → IOM tightness limiting **both** pronation and supination.

**Normal distal radius parameters (know cold):**
- Radial height **13 mm**
- Radial inclination **23°**
- Volar tilt **10–11°**

**Operative criteria after distal radius fracture:** radial shortening **>3 mm**, dorsal tilt **>10°**, intra-articular step-off/displacement **>2 mm** (adjusted for age/function).

**Why finger extension is wrong:** MCP extension depends on the EDC/extensor mechanism, unaffected by an extra-articular dorsal malunion of the radius. Distal radius dorsal malunion is a **DRUJ/forearm-rotation** problem, so the motion lost is **forearm rotation (supination/pronation)**, not finger MCP motion. (A separate classic complication of distal radius fracture is **EPL rupture** → loss of thumb IP extension/retropulsion — but that's tendon rupture, not the malunion-rotation mechanism asked here.)

**Anchor:** dorsal distal radius malunion → IOM tight + DRUJ incongruent → lose **forearm pronosupination**. Correct dorsal tilt to <10°. Normal radius: 13 mm height / 23° inclination / 11° volar tilt.
cards.wrongAnswer: finger extension at the MCP joints

cards.front: DAA THA Anterior Retractor — Superior Trajectory + DEEP to Rectus Femoris
cards.back:
Wrong answer: A; with the retractor tip **superficial** to the indirect head of the rectus femoris. Correct: **A; with the retractor tip DEEP to the indirect head of the rectus femoris**.

Direct anterior approach THA, anterior acetabular retractor placement, lowest femoral nerve risk = **trajectory A (most superior) + tip deep to the rectus femoris indirect head**. I picked the right line (A) but the wrong **depth** — deep, not superficial.

**Two variables, both must be right:**
1. **Trajectory (A vs B vs C) — go superior.** Using the acetabulum as a clock face (ASIS → center = **12 o'clock**), moving **clockwise toward 3 o'clock brings the retractor closer to the femoral nerve**. **A is the most superior/least clockwise → safest.** (Yoshino et al., 84 hips: nerve 33.2 mm medial at 0° but only **16.6 mm at 90°** → avoid the 90°/3-o'clock position.)
2. **Depth — go deep to the rectus femoris indirect head.** The **indirect (reflected) head of rectus femoris** overlies the anterior rim; a retractor tip **deep** to it keeps the muscle interposed as a **cushion protecting the femoral nerve**. A tip **superficial** to the rectus can **bluntly injure** the femoral nerve. (Even a deep tip can cause **traction** injury with prolonged tension — retract only when needed.)

**Why my answer (superficial) is wrong:** superficial placement leaves the femoral nerve exposed to direct/blunt retractor injury. Deep placement uses the rectus femoris as a protective buffer. Same trajectory A, opposite safety profile.

**Anatomy anchor:** the **femoral nerve is the closest major neurovascular structure to the anterior acetabular rim**; it (and the external iliac vessels) get closer as the retractor moves anteriorly/inferiorly along the rim (Sullivan et al. — place the anterior retractor **superiorly**).

**Anchor:** DAA anterior retractor safest = **most superior trajectory (A, toward 12 o'clock) + tip DEEP to the rectus femoris indirect head**. Superior + deep. Avoid clockwise-toward-3-o'clock and avoid superficial-to-rectus.
cards.correctAnswer: A; with the retractor tip DEEP to the indirect head of the rectus femoris
cards.wrongAnswer: A; with the retractor tip superficial to the indirect head of the rectus femoris

cards.front: PTT Transfer for Foot Drop — Identify Tibialis Posterior on Axial CT
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Structure C (flexor digitorum longus). Correct: **Structure A — tibialis posterior**.

17-year-old, missed anterior compartment syndrome (delayed transfer, opioid-masked pain, >75% anterior muscle non-viable at 14 h) → **permanent tibialis anterior loss / foot drop**, other compartments 5/5, supple ankle. Reconstruction = **posterior tibialis tendon (PTT) transfer** re-routed through the interosseous membrane to the dorsum (lateral cuneiform). I knew the answer was PTT — I **misidentified the muscle on the axial CT (Figure B)**. That's the gap.

**The concept (I had this):** for foot drop from irrecoverable TA loss, transfer the **tibialis posterior** through the IOM to the mid-dorsal foot (lateral cuneiform). It's an **out-of-phase** transfer (PTT fires in terminal stance, TA at heel strike) but works via resting tone + it's **stronger and a better donor than FDL**; **peroneus longus** is the next-best but anatomically less ideal (Cho et al.: dorsiflexion strength ≈33% of normal but function/gait satisfactorily improved).

**The actual fix — deep posterior compartment on axial cross-section, medial → lateral behind the tibia (mnemonic "Tom, Dick, and Harry"):**
- **Tibialis posterior** — **most medial, sits directly behind/against the tibia** (its resting position right behind the tibia is exactly why it's the ideal donor). = **Structure A**
- flexor **D**igitorum longus — just lateral to tib post = the FDL I wrongly picked (Structure C)
- posterior tibial **A**rtery + tibial **N**erve
- flexor **H**allucis longus — most lateral of the deep group, behind the fibula

**Leg compartments quick map (axial):**
| Compartment | Muscles | Nerve |
|---|---|---|
| Anterior | TA, EHL, EDL, peroneus tertius | deep peroneal |
| Lateral | peroneus longus & brevis | superficial peroneal |
| Superficial posterior | gastroc, soleus, plantaris | tibial |
| Deep posterior | **Tib post (medial, on tibia)**, FDL, FHL (lateral, on fibula) | tibial |

**The PT–FDL crossover (level-dependent — this is why the axial answer changes):**
The tibialis posterior tendon crosses **deep (anterior) to** the FDL tendon at the **crural chiasm (chiasma cruris)**, in the **distal third of the leg, ~5–7 cm above the medial malleolus.**
- **Mid-leg / muscle belly (above the chiasm):** tib post is the **deepest** muscle, on the interosseous membrane; **FDL sits medial and superficial** to it. (This is the level of Figure B here — tib post = Structure A, on the tibia.)
- **At/below the chiasm → medial malleolus → tarsal tunnel:** relationship flips — **tib post is the most ANTERIOR and MEDIAL** structure (the "Tom" of Tom-Dick-Harry), with **FDL now posterior** to it.

Don't confuse with the **chiasma plantare (master knot of Henry)** in the foot — that's **FDL crossing FHL** on the plantar surface, a different tendon pair. The crural chiasm is also the region the surgeon works through to harvest/re-route the PTT, so the crossover is practically relevant, not just trivia.

**Anchor:** on axial imaging, **tibialis posterior = most medial deep-posterior muscle, hugging the back of the tibia** (proximal/mid-leg). It crosses **anterior to FDL at the crural chiasm** (~5–7 cm above the medial malleolus) and becomes the most anterior/medial tendon at the ankle. Concept and anatomy both have to be right to get the point.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Radial Club Hand / Congenital Syndromes
cards.front: Radial Club Hand — Rule Out Fanconi Anemia with CBC, Peripheral Smear, and Chromosomal Breakage Analysis
cards.back:
Wrong answer: LFTs, CBC, and cardiac ultrasound. Correct: **CBC, peripheral blood smear, and chromosomal breakage analysis**.

**Radial club hand (radial longitudinal deficiency) — associated syndromes:**
- **Fanconi anemia (FA)** — autosomal recessive, the most life-threatening of the group
- TAR syndrome (thrombocytopenia absent radius)
- Holt-Oram syndrome
- VACTERL association
- VATER association

**Why FA drives the workup:** FA causes progressive aplastic anemia and is the most common inherited cause of aplastic anemia. Typical presentation is age 6-9. It is the only one of these conditions that can require bone marrow transplant for survival, which is why it takes priority in the evaluation.

**Diagnostic workup for FA:**
- CBC — shows decreased leukocytes, RBCs, and platelets
- Peripheral blood smear
- **Chromosomal breakage analysis** — the genetic test that confirms the diagnosis; shows increased chromosomal breakage

Cardiac ultrasound and renal ultrasound screen for associated anomalies but do not rule out FA. LFTs are not part of the FA workup.

De Kerviler et al. reviewed clinical and radiologic features of FA, noting congenital malformations affect the radial forearm and urinary system and recommending ultrasound for early detection. Alter described the hematologic disorders (FA, Diamond-Blackfan anemia, TAR) that can present first in the pediatric upper extremity.
cards.correctAnswer: CBC, peripheral blood smear, and chromosomal breakage analysis

cards.front: Scapholunate Instability — Dorsal Intercarpal Ligaments Are the Key Secondary Stabilizer Against DISI
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Volar lunotriquetral ligament. Correct: **Dorsal intercarpal ligaments**.

**Scapholunate instability — the most common carpal instability:**
- Primary stabilizer of the scaphoid and lunate: the **scapholunate ligament**, commonly injured with a fall onto an outstretched hand
- Secondary stabilizers: **dorsal intercarpal ligaments** and **dorsal radiocarpal ligaments**
- Failure to recognize injury to the secondary stabilizers can cause persistent **dorsal intercalated segment instability (DISI)**, which predisposes to SLAC wrist and early wrist osteoarthritis

**Clinical presentation:** positive Watson's scaphoid shift test after FOOSH, dorsal wrist pain.

**Supporting anatomy/biomechanics:**
- Mitsuyasu et al. showed the dorsal intercarpal ligament (DIC) stabilizes the scaphoid and lunate during static and dynamic wrist motion, and recommended intraoperative assessment of the DIC with consideration for repair/reconstruction during scapholunate ligament surgery.
- Viegas et al. showed the dorsal intercarpal and dorsal radiocarpal ligaments form a lateral V configuration over the dorsal wrist, producing an indirect dorsal stabilizing effect.

The volar lunotriquetral ligament and transverse carpal ligament are not the relevant secondary stabilizers here; TFCC is unrelated to scapholunate instability.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Flexor Tendon Repair / WALANT
cards.front: Wide-Awake Flexor Tendon Repair — Epinephrine Is Safe in Digits, Not Contraindicated
cards.back:
EXCEPT question — the false statement is the correct answer. Wrong answer: "Facilitates postoperative early active motion" (this is TRUE, not the exception). Correct: **"Epinephrine is contraindicated due to the risk of digital necrosis"** — this is the false statement.

**Modern teaching overturns the old digital-epinephrine dogma:** epinephrine is safe in finger and hand surgery at an appropriate dose. The "no epinephrine in digits" rule is outdated.

**Wide-awake flexor tendon repair (zones 1-2), under tumescent local anesthesia (lidocaine + epinephrine, no tourniquet, no sedation):**
- Advantages: bloodless field, reduced sedation, cost savings
- Intraoperative active motion allows identification of repair gaps and confidence in the repair
- Allows inspection of tendon gliding through the A2 pulley and assessment of active excursion
- Facilitates postoperative early active motion, which reduces rupture and tenolysis rates

**Dosing:** typically 7 mg/kg lidocaine with 1:100,000 epinephrine if <50cc needed; lidocaine/epinephrine ratio and bupivacaine addition can be adjusted case to case. **Phentolamine** (an alpha antagonist) is the antidote to epinephrine-induced vasoconstriction — inject 1 mg diluted in 5 mL saline at the same site if needed.

**Supporting evidence:**
- Lalonde et al. reviewed wide-awake flexor tendon repair with early mobilization in zones 1-2 using local lidocaine with epinephrine. Discontinuing tourniquet, sedation, peripheral nerve blocks, and general anesthesia decreased rupture and tenolysis rates while allowing protected midrange active flexion postoperatively.
- McKee et al. ran a prospective, randomized, triple-blind study on 12 volunteers comparing 1% lidocaine with epinephrine to 1% plain lidocaine, measuring relative hemoglobin concentration of skin/soft tissue by spectroscopy. They concluded 25 minutes is the ideal wait time between epinephrine injection and incision to minimize bleeding.
cards.correctAnswer: "Epinephrine is contraindicated due to the risk of digital necrosis" — this is the false statement
cards.wrongAnswer: Facilitates postoperative early active motion" (this is TRUE, not the exception)

cards.front: Camptodactyly — Splinting and Stretching First; Genetic Workup Is Not Indicated for Isolated Cases
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Nighttime extension splinting and stretching regimen with full genetic workup. Correct: **Nighttime extension splinting and stretching regimen** (no genetic workup).

**Camptodactyly:** a nontraumatic flexion deformity isolated to the PIP joint, typically of the small finger. Often bilateral and sporadic, though some congenital disorders are associated. Multiple anatomic structures have been implicated in its pathogenesis, and multiple surgical techniques have been described — but treatment is nonsurgical first.

**Treatment sequence:**
1. **Intrinsic-plus splinting with passive stretching** — first-line, should be initiated as soon as the diagnosis is made
2. Surgery — reserved for failed splinting or significant contractures approaching 60 degrees

**Why the genetic workup answer is wrong:** this case describes an isolated, sporadic deformity in an otherwise healthy child — a full genetic workup is not indicated. Genetic evaluation is reserved for syndromic or clearly associated presentations, not routine isolated camptodactyly.

**Natural history if untreated:** adjacent joint involvement can develop, most commonly **MCP hyperextension**.

**Supporting evidence:**
- Comer et al. reviewed complications of camptodactyly. Most common: progression or failed improvement of PIP contracture and MP hyperextension, isolated PIP postoperative residual stiffness, and bony remodeling of the proximal phalanx head preventing full extension. Inconsistent surgical results support early detection and conservative treatment (stretching and night splinting) as the mainstay.
- Rhee et al. reviewed outcomes of passive stretching for isolated camptodactyly flexion contractures in children under age three, showing marked improvement in contracture with conservative management.
cards.correctAnswer: Nighttime extension splinting and stretching regimen (no genetic workup)
cards.wrongAnswer: Nighttime extension splinting and stretching regimen with full genetic workup

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Pediatric Hand / Carpal Ossification
cards.front: Carpal Ossification Order — The Capitate Ossifies First (1-3 Months), Not the Lunate
cards.back:
Wrong answer: The bone affected by AVN in Kienbock's disease (lunate). Correct: **The largest carpal bone** — the capitate.

**The capitate is the first carpal bone to ossify**, with its ossification center appearing around **1-3 months of age**, occasionally simultaneous with the hamate. It is also the **largest of the 8 carpal bones**.

**Ossification order:** proceeds in a counterclockwise pattern (right wrist) — capitate → hamate → triquetrum → lunate → scaphoid → trapezium → trapezoid → **pisiform last, around age 9-10**. This predictable sequence is used clinically to evaluate bone age in children with suspected growth disorders.

**Distractor bones in this question:**
- Preisser's disease — AVN of the **scaphoid**
- Kienbock's disease — AVN of the **lunate**
- Trapezium — articulates with the base of the thumb metacarpal
- Pisiform — the only sesamoid carpal bone

**Supporting evidence:**
- Goodell et al. reviewed pediatric hand and finger fractures and outlined the ossification sequence: capitate first (1-3 months), hamate shortly after, continuing circularly to the pisiform (age 9-10).
- Daneff et al. studied ultrasound assessment of carpal ossification centers as a way to estimate bone age without the radiation of serial hand radiographs, confirming the capitate is typically first to appear (1-3 months), often simultaneous with the hamate.
cards.correctAnswer: The largest carpal bone — the capitate
cards.wrongAnswer: The bone affected by AVN in Kienbock's disease (lunate)

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Thumb CMC / Ligamentous Injury
cards.front: Thumb CMC Dorsal Dislocation — The Dorsoradial Ligament (Not the AOL) Is the Primary Restraint
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Dorsal dislocation, L2 (anterior oblique ligament) is torn. Correct: **Dorsal dislocation, L3 (dorsoradial ligament) is torn.**

**Thumb CMC dislocation is rare; dorsal dislocations are more common than volar.**

**Mechanism:** axial loading with the thumb metacarpal flexed (classically axial load onto the thumb; also described from a force driven into the first web, e.g., handlebar into the thumb on impact).

**Key ligament distinction:**
- **Dorsoradial ligament (DRL)** — the **primary restraint to dorsal dislocation**; this is what tears
- **Anterior oblique ligament (AOL)** — remains **continuous/intact** but peels off the base of the 1st metacarpal; it does not tear

**Contrast with Bennett/Rolando fracture:** in these fracture-dislocations, the AOL stays attached to a bone fragment at the metacarpal base, and the metacarpal translates because of the fracture through the base — not because of ligament failure.

**Supporting evidence:**
- Strauch et al. reviewed acute CMC dislocations in cadavers, applying a dorsal dislocating force while progressively sectioning ligaments. The DRL was the primary restraint; the AOL allowed dislocation only after peeling off the metacarpal base. The joint was more stable in pronation and extension, which tightens the AOL.
- Bettinger and Berger reviewed the functional anatomy of the trapezium and TMCJ, describing 16 stabilizing ligaments using geometric and mathematical models. They identify the DRL as an important stabilizer against dorsal forces, with the trapeziotrapezoid, trapezio-II metacarpal, and trapezio-III metacarpal ligaments functioning as tension elements.
cards.correctAnswer: Dorsal dislocation, L3 (dorsoradial ligament) is torn
cards.wrongAnswer: Dorsal dislocation, L2 (anterior oblique ligament) is torn

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Kienbock's Disease / Lichtman Classification
cards.front: Kienbock's Disease — Lichtman Stage IIIB Requires a Radioscaphoid Angle >60° (Fixed Scaphoid Flexion), Not Just Lunate Collapse
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Stage IIIA — lunate collapse. Correct: **Stage IIIB — lunate collapse with fixed scaphoid flexion.**

**The IIIA vs. IIIB distinction is the radioscaphoid angle:** a radioscaphoid angle **>60 degrees** signals fixed scaphoid flexion and moves the diagnosis from IIIA to IIIB, regardless of the degree of lunate collapse alone.

**Kienbock's disease pathophysiology:** avascular necrosis of the lunate, thought to be precipitated by a combination of anatomic and biomechanical factors — repetitive trauma, ulnar negative variance, decreased radial inclination, and vascular congestion.

**Lichtman classification and management:**
| Stage | Findings | Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| I | Lunate normal or edema only (MRI signal change, may have linear fracture) | Immobilization |
| II | Lunate sclerosis on plain films, no collapse | Radial or capitate shortening, STT fusion, or revascularization procedure |
| IIIA | Lunate collapse, scaphoid in appropriate position | Same as Stage II |
| IIIB | Lunate collapse **with fixed scaphoid flexion** (radioscaphoid angle >60°) | PRC, lunate excision with limited intercarpal fusion (STT or scaphocapitate) |
| IV | Degeneration of the remainder of the carpus (pancarpal arthritis) | PRC, intercarpal fusion, or total wrist fusion |

**Clinical correlate in this case:** the surgeon recommends PRC but states total wrist arthrodesis is not warranted given the limited extent of carpal involvement — consistent with Stage IIIB (isolated lunate/scaphoid-level disease), not Stage IV (which would involve the rest of the carpus and push toward total wrist fusion).
cards.correctAnswer: Stage IIIB — lunate collapse with fixed scaphoid flexion

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Carpal Tunnel Syndrome / Electrodiagnostics
cards.front: EMG vs. NCV in Carpal Tunnel Syndrome — Distal Sensory Latency Is an NCV Finding, Not an EMG Finding
cards.back:
EXCEPT question. Wrong answer: Increased insertional activity (this IS an EMG finding, not the exception). Correct: **Increased distal sensory latency** — this is measured on NCV, not EMG.

**EMG vs. NCV — different halves of the electrodiagnostic study:**
- **EMG** detects the electrical potential generated by muscle cells on activation. It evaluates the **motor unit** and can show: **fibrillations at rest, positive sharp waves, decreased motor recruitment, and increased insertional activity.**
- **NCV** measures the speed of nerve impulse conduction down the axon. Only large, myelinated fibers are evaluated on NCV. It measures **distal latencies and conduction velocities.**

**NCV diagnostic parameters for carpal tunnel syndrome:**
- Distal motor latency **>4.5 msec**
- Distal sensory latency **>3.5 msec**
- Conduction velocity **<52 m/sec**

**Take-home:** if a finding is described in terms of "latency" or "conduction velocity," it belongs to NCV. If it's described in terms of spontaneous muscle electrical activity (fibrillations, sharp waves, recruitment, insertional activity), it belongs to EMG.

**Source:** Brumback et al. and Gooch et al. reviewed electrodiagnostic studies for compression neuropathies (Brumback et al., *Hand Clin*, 1992).
cards.correctAnswer: Increased distal sensory latency — this is measured on NCV, not EMG
cards.wrongAnswer: Increased insertional activity (this IS an EMG finding, not the exception)

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / ECU Instability / Tendon Subluxation
cards.front: ECU Subluxation — Immobilize in Pronation, Extension, and Radial Deviation (Not Ulnar Deviation)
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Pronation, extension, ulnar deviation. Correct: **Pronation, extension, and radial deviation.**

**Dynamic ECU subluxation:** the ECU passes through a fibro-osseous subsheath forming the 6th dorsal extensor compartment and attaches to the base of the 5th metacarpal. Subluxation results from attenuation or traumatic rupture of the ECU subsheath. More common in tennis players and typically worsened by overuse.

**Presentation:** clicking on the ulnar side of the wrist with **supination and flexion** (this is when the tendon subluxates — the position that provokes symptoms). A click reproduced with the pathognomonic motion is diagnostic; MRI can help evaluate ECU position or assess for tendinosis.

**Treatment — the position that confers stability is the opposite of the provocative position:** immobilize in **pronation, extension, and radial deviation**, which is the most stable position for the ECU. Supination and flexion is the least stable position and should be avoided in symptomatic ECU instability.

**Escalation if conservative management fails:** direct subsheath repair (acute setting) or extensor retinaculum flap reconstruction (chronic cases).

**Supporting evidence:** Ghatan et al. tested ECU stability in a cadaveric model and found pronation/extension was the most stable position, supination/flexion the least stable, and recommended against the latter position in patients with symptomatic ECU instability.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Dupuytren's Disease / Nonoperative Treatment
cards.front: Dupuytren's — Pruritic Rash and Axillary Lymphadenopathy Are Specific to Collagenase Injections, Not Percutaneous Needle Fasciotomy
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Collagenase injections are more expensive but require fewer treatments than PNF to achieve the same results. Correct: **Pruritic rash and axillary lymphadenopathy may be observed after collagenase injections but are not typically seen after percutaneous needle fasciotomy.**

**Background:** regional subtotal fasciectomy is the gold standard for Dupuytren's contracture, but its complication rate runs up to 40%, which makes nonoperative options attractive.

**Percutaneous needle fasciotomy (PNF):** a 22G or 25G needle releases palpable/visible cords in the office, followed by manipulation and night orthosis wear.

**Collagenase injection (CI):** 0.25 mL for MCP contracture, 0.20 mL for PIP contracture. Manipulation is performed the following day under local anesthesia; repeated at 4 weeks if the desired result isn't achieved.

**Complication profile — this is the tested distinction:**
- CI-specific: **pruritic rash and axillary lymphadenopathy** (not seen after PNF). Most common CI complications overall: edema, contusion, pain, lymphadenopathy, and skin laceration. Major complications (CRPS, flexor tendon rupture) are rare with CI.
- Skin tears/lacerations can occur with **both** PNF and CI — not exclusive to PNF as one distractor answer implied.

**Recurrence and outcomes:**
- Black et al.: recurrence higher with PNF (85%) vs. fasciectomy (24%); PNF is acceptable for early disease with less severe contracture, and in elderly or medically frail patients.
- Nydick et al. retrospectively compared PNF (30 patients) to CI (29 patients): similar satisfaction, complication rates, and success (contracture reduced to 0-5 degrees) between the two — both are good alternatives to surgery. Cost/treatment-burden comparisons between CI and PNF were not what distinguished the correct answer here — the specific side-effect profile was.
cards.wrongAnswer: Collagenase injections are more expensive but require fewer treatments than PNF to achieve the same results

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Carpal Instability / Scapholunate Ligament Anatomy
cards.front: Scapholunate Dissociation — Requires Disruption of the Dorsal (Not Volar) Scapholunate Interosseous Ligament
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Volar scapholunate interosseous ligament. Correct: **Dorsal scapholunate interosseous ligament.**

**Scapholunate ligament complex — three components, listed in decreasing yield strength:**
1. **Dorsal scapholunate ligament** — the stoutest of the three
2. Volar (palmar) scapholunate ligament
3. Proximal fibrocartilaginous membrane

**Key point:** because the dorsal component is the strongest, **disruption of the dorsal scapholunate interosseous ligament is required for true scapholunate dissociation to occur** — injury to the volar ligament or the proximal membrane alone is not sufficient.

**Clinical correlate:** a widened scapholunate gap on radiograph indicates disruption of this complex, with the dorsal ligament as the critical structure. (Compare with [[hand_wrist_incorrects]] entry [2]: the dorsal intercarpal and dorsal radiocarpal ligaments are the *secondary* stabilizers preventing DISI once the primary scapholunate ligament complex is already injured.)

**Source:** Berger reviewed wrist ligament anatomy and function (*Hand Clin*, 1997), describing the scapholunate ligament complex and its three components by relative strength.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Pediatric Hand / Radial Longitudinal Deficiency
cards.front: Radial Longitudinal Deficiency — Centralization Is Not Offered Before 12 Months; Use Carpal Ossification to Estimate Age When Age Isn't Given
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Unstable carpus. Correct: **Current patient age** — this specific patient is younger than 12 months, and centralization is not generally offered before 12 months because of anesthesia safety.

**How to determine age from the radiograph when age isn't given:** use the carpal ossification timeline (see [[hand_wrist_incorrects]] entry [5] — capitate ossifies first). The capitate should show some calcification by 1-2 months and the lunate by 2-4 months, both prominent by 12 months. Absence of any carpal ossification on this radiograph indicates the child is well under 12 months.

**Radial longitudinal deficiency (RLD, radial clubhand):**
- Incidence 1:30,000–100,000 live births; slight male predilection (3:2); bilateral in 38-58% of cases
- Associated syndromes: VATER/VACTERL, TAR, Fanconi anemia, Holt-Oram (see [[hand_wrist_incorrects]] entry [1])

**RLD spectrum (severity):**
1. Type 1 — short radius, distal epiphysis present
2. Type 2 — hypoplastic radius, defective distal epiphysis
3. Type 3 — partial absence of radius, proximal defect
4. Type 4 — completely absent radius (most severe, but also most common)

**Treatment:** associated syndromes must be ruled out first. Mild forms: observation, splinting, and stretching. Complete absence (type 4) may warrant **hand centralization** unless contraindicated.

**Centralization procedure:** resection of a portion of the carpus, shortening of the ECU, and potentially an angular osteotomy of the ulna (sparing the distal ulna physis).

**General contraindications to centralization:**
- Older patients who have already adapted with good function
- Limited elbow flexion (<90 degrees) — the child relies on wrist motion to compensate, so centralization (which stiffens the wrist) would worsen function
- Severe soft tissue contracture
- Age under 12 months (anesthesia risk) — the answer in this specific case

**Supporting evidence:** Geck et al. evaluated outcomes of 29 limbs with RLD treated with radialization or modified centralization; all patients showed improvement in hand-forearm angle and hand-forearm stability.

cards.front: Congenital Cleft Hand — Autosomal Dominant Inheritance; Concept Was Right, Pedigree Reading Was the Miss
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Figure D. Correct: **Figure E** — the pedigree showing an autosomal dominant pattern. The inheritance pattern (autosomal dominant) was identified correctly; the error was misreading which pedigree diagram actually depicts that pattern.

**Congenital cleft hand** (split-hand malformation, "lobster claw" deformity): absence of one or more central digits. Inheritance is primarily **autosomal dominant**, with inherited forms tending to become more severe each generation. Sporadic cases without family history also occur, from de novo mutation.

**How to read an autosomal dominant pedigree — the features that must all be present:**
- Affected individuals appear in **every generation** (vertical transmission) — no skipping generations the way recessive conditions can
- Affected males and females in roughly equal numbers (autosomal, not sex-linked)
- Every affected individual has **at least one affected parent** — unaffected parents do not produce affected offspring
- Roughly **50%** of the offspring of an affected parent (mated with an unaffected partner) are affected

**Contrast for pedigree differentiation:**
- Autosomal recessive: can skip generations; affected children often born to two unaffected (carrier) parents; consanguinity raises risk
- X-linked recessive: affects males far more than females; no male-to-male transmission
- X-linked dominant: affects both sexes but affected fathers pass the trait to **all daughters, no sons**

**Clinical takeaway:** because most cases are inherited as autosomal dominant, patients with this condition should receive genetic counseling on the likelihood of passing it to offspring.

**Supporting evidence:** Guero et al. reviewed congenital cleft hand, describing a phenotypic spectrum from minor central digit shortening to complete absence of the third ray (or two-to-four rays in severe cases). Inheritance is mostly autosomal dominant, though sporadic de novo cases occur.
cards.correctAnswer: Figure E — the pedigree showing an autosomal dominant pattern

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Pediatric Hand / Fingertip Amputation
cards.front: Pediatric Fingertip Amputation — Ointment and Dressing Changes Remain First-Line Even With Bone Exposed
cards.back:
Wrong answer: V-Y advancement flap. Correct: **No change from the initial plan** — ointment and dressing changes.

**Key point — this is a pediatric-specific answer:** in young children, ointment and dressing changes remain the most appropriate treatment for a fingertip amputation **even when bone is exposed.** Children have a markedly greater capacity for distal fingertip regeneration than adults, and conservative management (secondary intention healing) does not need to be abandoned just because bone is visible in the wound.

**Reconstruction ladder** (least to most invasive — useful framework, but not indicated here): primary closure → healing by secondary intention → split-thickness skin graft → full-thickness skin graft → random pattern local flap → axial pattern local flap → island pattern local flap → distant random pattern flap → distant axial pattern flap → free tissue transfer. Flaps (V-Y advancement, homodigital island, thenar, volar advancement) are reserved for cases where conservative management fails or is not appropriate — not the default for pediatric fingertip injuries with exposed bone.

**Supporting evidence:**
- Lamon et al. reviewed 25 patients (average age 30) with fingertip injuries treated with dressings and warm soaks starting 2 days post-injury; no healing complications. Only one patient in this adult cohort had bone exposed.
- Soderberg et al. ran a Level 3 study of 36 operative vs. 34 conservatively treated fingertip amputations with bone exposure and found **no benefit to surgery** over conservative management.
cards.correctAnswer: No change from the initial plan — ointment and dressing changes

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Skeletal Dysplasia / Madelung Deformity
cards.front: Leri-Weill Dyschondrosteosis — Sex-Linked SHOX Gene Mutation Causing Madelung Deformity
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Pseudoachondroplasia. Correct: **Leri-Weill dyschondrosteosis.**

**Leri-Weill dyschondrosteosis:** short stature plus **bilateral Madelung deformities** of the wrist. Caused by mutation of the **SHOX gene**, located on both the X and Y chromosomes — a mutation on either sex chromosome causes the dysplasia (**sex-linked dominant**, not autosomal).

**Madelung deformity mechanism:** disruption of the **volar-ulnar physis of the distal radius**, producing increased radial inclination and radiopalmar (volar) tilt. Clinical consequences: ulnocarpal impaction, restricted forearm rotation, and median nerve compression.

**Genetics of the distractors — useful to keep straight:**
| Condition | Gene / Inheritance |
|---|---|
| Leri-Weill dyschondrosteosis | SHOX (X and Y chromosomes) — sex-linked |
| Cleidocranial dysplasia | CBFA-1 (transcription factor activating osteoblast differentiation) — autosomal dominant |
| Pseudoachondroplasia | COMP, chromosome 19 — autosomal dominant |
| Ellis-van Creveld syndrome | EVC gene — autosomal recessive; acromesomelic shortening, postaxial polydactyly |
| Achondroplasia | FGFR-3 — autosomal dominant |

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Pediatric Hand / Trigger Thumb
cards.front: Pediatric Trigger Thumb — Delaying Surgery to Age 2 Has No Sequelae
cards.back:
Wrong answer: A slight delay in motor milestones that will catch up after surgical release. Correct: **No sequelae.**

**Pediatric ("congenital") trigger thumb:** typically progresses from triggering at the thumb IP joint to a fixed flexion contracture.

**Treatment timeline:**
- Non-operative management (passive extension exercises or extension splinting) may be attempted; full spontaneous resolution has been reported **during the first two years of life**
- After age 2, spontaneous resolution of the contracture is **rare**, and surgical intervention is recommended
- **Delaying surgical release until age 2, even in the presence of a fixed contracture, is not associated with any known sequelae** — no permanent contracture, no hand dominance change, no motor milestone delay
- Surgery = release of the A1 pulley of the thumb, with **95% resolution across all age groups**

**Supporting evidence:**
- Shah et al. reviewed pediatric trigger thumb and trigger finger. Non-operative treatments (passive exercises, extension splinting) are of questionable efficacy but may be tried, with resolution possible up to age 2. After age 2, they recommend surgery since spontaneous resolution is unlikely.
- Baek et al. followed a prospective cohort of pediatric trigger thumb patients treated non-operatively (no stretching or splinting) for 48 months; 60% resolved spontaneously.
cards.wrongAnswer: A slight delay in motor milestones that will catch up after surgical release

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Tendon Injury / Lumbrical Plus Finger
cards.front: Lumbrical Plus Finger — Paradoxical PIP/DIP Extension on Attempted Flexion After DIP Amputation
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Boutonniere deformity. Correct: **Lumbrical plus finger.**

**Lumbrical plus finger:** paradoxical extension of the IP joints when attempting to flex the fingers. After a DIP amputation, attempted finger flexion causes the **PIP to extend** instead.

**Mechanism:** the lumbricals originate from the FDP. When the FDP is lacerated or amputated, contraction of the proximal FDP stump pulls on the lumbrical origin instead of advancing the tendon, which shortens the lateral bands and produces paradoxical PIP and DIP extension.

**Causes:**
1. FDP laceration or rupture distal to the lumbrical origin
2. Amputation of the DIP distal to the central slip insertion (the scenario in this question)
3. Excessively long flexor tendon graft

**Treatment:** lumbrical release at the level of the flexor sheath in the palm, which eliminates the paradoxical PIP extension.

**Distinguish from quadrigia effect:** quadrigia occurs when the FDP is advanced **more than 1 cm** during repair. Because the four FDP tendons share a common muscle belly, over-advancing one tendon reduces flexion strength/excursion in the adjacent digits — a different mechanism from lumbrical plus finger.

**Source:** Parkes reviewed the lumbrical plus finger, describing the FDP-lumbrical mechanism and the three causes above.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Congenital Hand / Amniotic Band Syndrome
cards.front: Amniotic Band Syndrome (Streeter's Dysplasia) — Associated with Congenital Talipes Equinovarus
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Unremarkable gestational history. Correct: **Congenital talipes equinovarus (clubfoot).**

**Amniotic band syndrome** (constriction ring syndrome, Streeter's dysplasia): occurs in about 1:15,000 live births, affects both sexes equally. Sporadic, **not hereditary**. Leading theory: premature amnion rupture forms membranous strands that circumferentially wrap and constrict the developing fetus in utero.

**Distribution of involvement:** appendages are most commonly affected; constriction of the torso or head can cause severe cardiac, pulmonary, GI, or cranial abnormalities. Of the limbs, the **hands** are most commonly affected, with a spectrum from amputation to syndactylization.

**Key association tested here:** Streeter's dysplasia is associated with **congenital talipes equinovarus** — not an unremarkable pregnancy history. Prognosis correlates with the severity of the constricting bands; severe cases may need constriction band release, Z-plasties for contracture, or reconstruction of involved digits.

**Supporting evidence:** Foulkes and Reinker reviewed 71 cases of congenital constriction band syndrome. Average of three involved limbs, most often the distal aspects of central upper-extremity digits. **Abnormal gestational histories were found in 60% of cases**, and **33% had clubfeet**. Average whole-body impairment was 20%, requiring a mean of three reconstructive procedures. The authors support "early amnion rupture sequence" as an accurate description of the pathogenesis.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Dupuytren's Disease / Surgical Technique
cards.front: Dupuytren's — Dermatofasciectomy + Full-Thickness Skin Graft Shows No Outcome Advantage Over Fasciectomy, Regardless of Severity
cards.back:
Wrong answer: A specific severe-presentation patient (multi-digit, MCP + PIP involvement, large contractures) as the one who benefits most from dermatofasciectomy + full-thickness skin grafting. Correct: **None of the above — no difference in outcome has been demonstrated between the two procedures.**

**Key point — this is a trap built on disease severity:** there is no patient profile, however severe, for which dermatofasciectomy + full-thickness skin grafting has been shown to outperform traditional fasciectomy. It has **not** demonstrated superior finger range of motion, recurrence rate, or patient satisfaction compared to fasciectomy alone — even in advanced, multi-joint, multi-digit disease.

**Supporting evidence:**
- Ullah et al. ran a prospective randomized study of 84 Dupuytren's cases treated with fasciectomy alone vs. dermatofasciectomy with full-thickness skin grafting, testing specifically whether the overlying skin needed to be excised. No difference in clinical outcome or recurrence rate.
- Roy et al. reviewed 79 cases of advanced Dupuytren's treated with radical fasciectomy (overlying skin preserved), adding full-thickness skin grafting to open areas once fingers were extended. Results were similar to those published for dermatofasciectomy.
cards.correctAnswer: None of the above — no difference in outcome has been demonstrated between the two procedures

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Vascular / Upper Extremity Ischemia
cards.front: Acute Subclavian Artery Thrombosis — Aortic Root Replacement Is Not Indicated (That's for Aortic Dissection)
cards.back:
EXCEPT question. Wrong answer: Manual aspiration (this IS an appropriate treatment, not the exception). Correct: **Aortic root replacement** — not indicated for subclavian arterial disease.

**Clinical picture:** absent brachial/radial/ulnar pulses with a **detectable monophasic Doppler waveform** (non-occlusive) plus sudden arm pain, cyanosis, and paresthesia = acute subclavian artery thrombosis.

**Why aortic root replacement is wrong:** it treats **ascending thoracic aortic dissection**, not isolated subclavian arterial disease. Don't let "aortic arch abnormality" as a listed risk factor pull you toward an aortic surgical answer — the disease process here is confined to the subclavian artery itself.

**Acute subclavian artery thrombosis:** a rare cause of upper limb ischemia.
- **Risk factors:** aortic arch abnormality, aortic arch syndrome (e.g., Takayasu's), trauma, arterial catheterization, pre-existing atheroma/stenosis at the subclavian ostium
- **Patterns:** slow occlusion (atherosclerosis), blue finger syndrome (repeated focal microemboli), acute global ischemia (large emboli)
- **Diagnosis:** conventional angiography — also allows percutaneous treatment in the same setting

**Treatment strategies (all appropriate, i.e., all wrong answers to this EXCEPT question):**
1. Medical therapy: heparin, aspirin, clopidogrel, IV thrombolysis, glycoprotein IIb/IIIa antagonists (abciximab, eptifibatide, tirofiban)
2. Open embolectomy and bypass grafting
3. Percutaneous endovascular procedures: intra-arterial thrombolysis, angioplasty, manual aspiration, stenting

**Supporting evidence:**
- Pellerin et al. described 2 cases of percutaneous endovascular management of acute left subclavian artery thrombosis — one treated with thrombolysis, stenting, and manual aspiration; the other with anticoagulation, stenting, and long-term anticoagulation.
- Rapp et al. reviewed patients with proximal lesions causing upper extremity ischemia: 14 had extrinsic compression, 17 had brachiocephalic atherosclerosis, 1 had an occluded graft, 1 had Takayasu's arteritis.
cards.correctAnswer: Aortic root replacement — not indicated for subclavian arterial disease
cards.wrongAnswer: Manual aspiration (this IS an appropriate treatment, not the exception)

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / PIP Arthritis / Implant Arthroplasty
cards.front: PIP Joint Arthroplasty — Silicone Implant Through a Volar Approach Gives Best ROM and Lowest Revision Rate
cards.back:
Wrong answer: There is no difference between approach and implant type. Correct: **Silicone implant with volar approach has best range of motion AND lowest revision rate** (not merely "same" ROM benefit with equal revision rate — the volar/silicone combination wins on both measures).

**PIPJ arthritis:** present in nearly **17% of women and 13% of men**. Presents with flexion contracture, decreased motion, swelling, and pain.

**Treatment selection by digit:**
- **Radial digits** (index, middle) — often treated with **fusion** due to stability demands
- **Non-border digits** (ring, small — the digit in this case is the middle finger, non-border for this purpose per the stem) — better suited to **arthroplasty**

**Implant and approach — the tested comparison:**
- **Silicone arthroplasty:** pain relief in **98%** of patients, relatively low complication rate
- **Dorsal approach:** the historic gold standard; technically more straightforward
- **Surface implants** (metal-on-plastic) are also available as an alternative to silicone
- **More favorable outcomes have recently been reported with the volar approach and with silicone implants specifically** — this combination shows the best range of motion with the lowest revision rate, outperforming other approach/implant combinations rather than merely tying with them.
cards.wrongAnswer: There is no difference between approach and implant type

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Metacarpal Fractures / Nonoperative Management
cards.front: Metacarpal Shaft Fracture Shortening — 2-5mm of Shortening (Not Angulation) Drives MCP Extension Lag and Grip Weakness
cards.back:
Wrong answer: 15-degree apex-dorsal angulation. Correct: **5mm of shortening.**

**Mechanism:** metacarpal shortening of **>2-5mm** can produce extension lag at the MCP joint and reduced grip strength, due to **loss of tension on the extensor mechanism** as the bone shortens relative to the tendon's fixed excursion. Angulation of 15 degrees, by contrast, is well within the acceptable range for most metacarpals and would not be expected to cause this outcome.

**Acceptable angulation for nonoperative treatment of metacarpal shaft fractures (splint/cast), assuming a stable pattern with no rotational deformity:**
- Index and long fingers: **10-20 degrees**
- Ring finger: **30 degrees**
- Small finger: **40 degrees**

A 15-degree apex-dorsal angulation is acceptable for any of these metacarpals — it is not the abnormal finding driving this patient's extension lag.

**Supporting evidence:**
- Strauch et al. ran a cadaveric study quantifying MCP extensor lag from metacarpal shortening: every **2mm of shortening produced an average 7 degrees of extensor lag** at the MCP joint.
- Al-Qattan et al. prospectively reviewed conservative management of spiral/long oblique metacarpal shaft fractures with palmar wrist splinting. Extension lag was seen initially in all digits but recovered by 1 year; grip strength at 1 year was 94% of the contralateral hand. Indications for surgery: severe angulation with failed closed reduction, rotational malalignment, or significant displacement with loss of bone-to-bone contact.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Scaphoid Fracture / Surgical Fixation
cards.front: Open Dorsal Scaphoid Screw Fixation vs. Percutaneous — Direct Visualization Decreases Risk of Screw Prominence, Not Tendon Injury Differences
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Increased risk of injury to the EPL tendon. Correct: **Decreased risk of screw prominence above subchondral bone.**

**Key principle:** scaphoid screw fixation should sit **just below the subchondral bone** — this is best judged by **direct visualization**, which is exactly what an open dorsal approach provides over a percutaneous approach.

**Why the EPL tendon answer is wrong:** EPL injury risk is not the distinguishing factor between open and percutaneous dorsal approaches in this comparison — screw seating accuracy is.

**Structures near a percutaneous dorsal scaphoid screw (within 2.2-3.1 mm):** the PIN, EDC, and EIP are all at increased risk with a percutaneous approach. The **APL tendon is not in this surgical field at all**, so it isn't at risk from either approach. Additional structures per the illustrated anatomy: the AIN lies deep relative to pronator quadratus; the sensory remnant of the posterior interosseous nerve sits adjacent to the interosseous membrane.

**Supporting evidence:**
- Adamany et al. performed an anatomic study using fluoroscopy for percutaneous scaphoid screw insertion: the screw was prominent above the subchondral bone in 2 of 12 specimens, flush or buried in the other 10. They recommend a limited dorsal incision to directly verify full screw seating — reducing prominence risk, which is the tested advantage of the open approach.
- Tumilty et al. inserted Herbert screws through a dorsal approach in 12 cadaveric wrists, then dissected to check for subchondral penetration. Plain radiographs were accurate in 5 of 6 specimens; fluoroscopy was accurate in all 6. They concluded fluoroscopy during Herbert screw placement may decrease the rate of subchondral penetration.
cards.correctAnswer: Decreased risk of screw prominence above subchondral bone
cards.wrongAnswer: Increased risk of injury to the EPL tendon

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Congenital / Amniotic Band Syndrome
cards.front: Amniotic Band Syndrome — Bronchopulmonary Dysplasia Is the Outlier (Linked to Prematurity, Not ABS)
cards.back:
EXCEPT question. Wrong answer: Rigid talipes equinovarus (this IS associated with ABS, not the exception). Correct: **Bronchopulmonary dysplasia** — linked to prematurity, not ABS.

**Amniotic band syndrome (ABS, Streeter dysplasia):** constricting amniotic bands form in utero; most-supported theory (extrinsic theory) holds that amniotic tissue entangles the developing fetus, causing compression and deformation. See also entry [18] for the clubfoot association.

**Orthopaedic manifestations:** characteristic extremity constriction bands, **clubfoot**, anterolateral tibial bowing, tibial pseudarthrosis, leg-length discrepancy, hemihypertrophy.

**Non-orthopaedic associated findings:** craniofacial abnormalities (including **cleft palate**), **cardiac defects**, renal abnormalities, **neural tube defects** (including **encephalocele**).

**Why bronchopulmonary dysplasia doesn't belong:** it's a disease of **prematurity and its treatment** (mechanical ventilation, oxygen toxicity in an immature lung) — a distinct, unrelated pathophysiology from the mechanical entrapment/compression that defines ABS.

**Diagnosis and management:** ABS is a clinical diagnosis; ultrasonography can help characterize associated defects in utero. Treatment is surgical release of constricting bands, urgently if there's neurovascular compromise. Long-term monitoring through skeletal maturity is needed to watch for contractures and growth disturbance.

**Supporting evidence:** Moran et al. reviewed diagnosis and management of ABS, favoring the extrinsic (entanglement) theory. Physical findings and associated features correlate with the developmental stage at the time of the insult. Appendages are more vulnerable overall, with a predilection for the more distal upper extremity. Management is individualized, ranging from cosmetic repair to emergent limb-sparing band release.
cards.correctAnswer: Bronchopulmonary dysplasia — linked to prematurity, not ABS
cards.wrongAnswer: Rigid talipes equinovarus (this IS associated with ABS, not the exception)

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Soft Tissue Coverage / Lateral Arm Flap
cards.front: Lateral Arm Flap — Based on the Posterior Radial Collateral Artery, a Branch of the Profunda Brachii
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Posterior ulnar recurrent artery. Correct: **Posterior radial collateral artery (PRCA).**

**Free antegrade lateral arm flap (LAF):** used for soft tissue coverage of the forearm (e.g., after necrotizing fasciitis debridement). Based on 3-5 **septocutaneous perforators** running in the intermuscular septum, originating from the **posterior radial collateral artery, a branch of the profunda brachii**.

**Flap characteristics:**
- Maximum width: 8 cm; maximum pedicle length: 8 cm
- Can be raised with the patient supine
- Donor site can be closed primarily
- Flap elevation often sacrifices the **posterior antebrachial cutaneous nerve**, causing forearm hypoesthesia
- The **radial nerve** runs nearby and must be protected during elevation to avoid transient radial nerve palsy

**Flap variants:** osteofasciocutaneous (with a vascularized humerus block), sensate (incorporating the posterior brachial cutaneous nerve), musculofasciocutaneous (with triceps), or fascial-only.

**Supporting evidence:**
- Sauerbier et al. described 21 patients undergoing LAF for hand and forearm defects. Max flap width 8 cm, max length 20 cm. All flaps survived; one required anastomotic revision. No other complications. They advocate this flap for good aesthetic appearance and reliable coverage.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Peripheral Nerve Injury / Seddon & Sunderland Classification
cards.front: Sunderland Second-Degree Nerve Injury — Endoneurium Stays Intact, Permitting Full Axon Regeneration
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Epineurium. Correct: **Endoneurium** — this is the structure that remains intact and enables regeneration.

**Sunderland classification maps onto the Seddon classification as follows:**
| Sunderland | Seddon | Structures disrupted | Structures intact | Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I | Neurapraxia | Myelin (segmental) — physiologic conduction block only | Axon, endoneurium, perineurium, epineurium | Full, weeks |
| II | Axonotmesis | Axon (physiologic disruption of the axon itself) | **Endoneurium**, perineurium, epineurium | Full, but slow (regeneration at ~1mm/day) |
| III | Axonotmesis | Axon + endoneurium | Perineurium, epineurium | Incomplete, variable |
| IV | Axonotmesis | Axon + endoneurium + perineurium | Epineurium only | Poor without surgery |
| V | Neurotmesis | Complete transection (all layers) | Nothing | None without surgical repair |

**Why endoneurium matters for Sunderland II specifically:** the endoneurial tubes remain intact and continuous, so regenerating axon sprouts have a preserved channel to follow back to their original end organs — this is why second-degree injuries achieve **complete functional recovery**, just slowly (regeneration proceeds over months).

**Source:** Lee et al. reviewed the pathophysiology and evaluation of peripheral nerve injuries — in Sunderland type II injuries, there is physiologic disruption of the axon itself, but because the endoneurium remains intact, axons are able to regenerate; this process takes months.
cards.correctAnswer: Endoneurium — this is the structure that remains intact and enables regeneration

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Ulnar Impaction Syndrome / Ulnar Shortening Osteotomy
cards.front: Ulnar Shortening Osteotomy — DRUJ Arthrosis Is a Contraindication, Not an Indication with "Encouraging Results"
cards.back:
EXCEPT question. Wrong answer: "Degenerative cystic changes of the ulnar carpal bones can resolve after ulnar shortening osteotomy" (this IS true, not the exception). Correct: **"Results are encouraging even for those with degenerative changes in the DRUJ"** — this is false; DRUJ arthrosis is a contraindication.

**Ulnar impaction syndrome:** caused by abutment of the ulnar head against the carpal bones. Worsened by wrist rotation and ulnar deviation — consistent with this patient's symptoms (jar opening, wringing a towel, gearshift). Positive ulnar variance, with or without carpal cystic changes, is often seen on plain radiographs. Central TFCC tears commonly coexist and can be addressed with simultaneous arthroscopic or open debridement.

**Key contraindication:** concomitant arthrosis of the **distal radioulnar joint (DRUJ)** is a contraindication to ulnar shortening osteotomy — outcomes are not "encouraging" in this population, which is why this statement is the false one (the exception) in the question.

**True statements about ulnar shortening osteotomy (the other four answer choices, all correct):**
- Care should be taken to avoid the **dorsal sensory branch of the ulnar nerve**
- Dorsal plate placement can cause **ECU tendinitis**
- Concomitant arthroscopy may be indicated for patients with concurrent **TFCC tears**
- **Degenerative cystic changes of the ulnar carpal bones can resolve** after the osteotomy

**Supporting evidence:**
- Baek et al. described 31 patients with improved Gartland and Werley scores after ulnar shortening osteotomy; all patients with degenerative cystic changes had resolution of the cysts at 1-2 year follow-up.
- Chun et al. reviewed 30 wrists in 27 patients with ulnar impaction syndrome treated with ulnar shortening osteotomy: very good outcomes, minimal complications, no nonunions. Their exclusion criteria specifically excluded any existing DRUJ arthrosis — reinforcing that DRUJ arthrosis is a contraindication rather than a population with encouraging results.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Thumb Pulley System / FPL Bowstringing
cards.front: Thumb Flexor Pulley Rupture — This Is the Oblique Pulley, Not the Radial Collateral Ligament
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Radial collateral ligament rupture. Correct: **Oblique pulley rupture.**

**Thumb pulley anatomy** (distinct from the finger pulley system — fingers have 5 annular + 3 cruciate pulleys): the thumb has **2 annular pulleys (A1, A2)**, **1 oblique pulley**, and **1 variable annular pulley (Av)**, arranged **A1 → Av → oblique → A2**.

**Oblique pulley specifics (Bayat et al., cadaveric study):**
- A1 and Av pulleys: 6mm long
- Oblique pulley: originates from the **ulnar side of the proximal phalanx base**, inserts on the **radial side of the distal phalanx base**; 4mm wide
- A2 pulley: 8mm wide
- The oblique pulley experiences **greater strain in extension than in flexion**
- Once both A1 and Av are sectioned, the oblique pulley alone can no longer prevent bowstringing

**Clinical relevance:** closed flexor pulley rupture is uncommon in the fingers (classically seen in rock climbers) and **rare in the thumb**. Loss of **both the oblique and proximal (A1) pulleys** leads to FPL bowstringing. This patient's history (steroid injection into a chronic FPL tenosynovitis, then rupture with a jar-opening injury) sets up pulley failure rather than a collateral ligament injury — the radial collateral ligament is a joint stabilizer, unrelated to the flexor tendon pulley system.

**Treatment:** pulley reconstruction with a tendon graft; **reconstructing the oblique pulley alone is sufficient** to prevent bowstringing.

**Supporting evidence:** a case report (author name illegible in the screenshot — check the reference list on Orthobullets if needed) described A1, oblique, and A2 pulley rupture leading to bowstringing, successfully treated with reconstruction of the oblique pulley alone.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Phalangeal Fractures / Proximal Phalanx
cards.front: Proximal Phalanx Fracture — Well-Reduced, No Rotation/Shortening/Angulation Means Short Immobilization, Not Fixation
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Reduction and percutaneous fixation. Correct: **Short-term immobilization and range of motion at 3 weeks.**

**Key point — the post-reduction exam findings drive the answer:** after reduction, this fracture has **no rotation, no shortening, no angulation**. A well-reduced, stable fracture like this does not need surgical fixation — it needs a brief period of protection, then motion.

**Nonoperative criteria for extra-articular proximal phalanx fractures:** angulation **<10°**, shortening **<2mm**, and **no rotational deformity** — treat with **3 weeks of immobilization followed by aggressive range of motion**.

**Displacement pattern to know:** proximal phalanx fractures characteristically displace into **apex volar angulation**, driven by the pull of the **central slip** on the distal fragment (into hyperextension) and the interossei on the proximal fragment. Routine follow-up is needed to confirm the reduction holds.

**Indications for operative treatment:** open fractures, and fractures that **cannot maintain reduction**. Options include CRPP or ORIF; very proximal phalanx fractures can be secured by passing a K-wire through the metacarpal head (**Eaton-Belsky pinning**).

**Supporting evidence:**
- Gaston and Chadderdon reviewed phalangeal fracture treatment: nonsurgical management for stable, extra-articular proximal phalanx fractures; surgical management for displaced fractures, unicondylar fractures, or fractures in high-level athletes.
- Kozin et al. reviewed operative treatment of phalangeal shaft fractures, advocating nonoperative management for stable fractures well-reduced in a position of MCP flexion, for up to 3 weeks.
cards.correctAnswer: Short-term immobilization and range of motion at 3 weeks

cards.front: Botulinum Toxin for Raynaud's — Improves Digital Perfusion by Blocking Acetylcholine Release, Not by "Increasing Sympathetic Innervation"
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Increasing sympathetic innervation by cleaving pre-synaptic SNAREs and preventing acetylcholine release. Correct: **Improving digital perfusion by cleaving pre-synaptic SNAREs and preventing acetylcholine release.**

**Why the wrong answer fails despite having the right mechanism:** it correctly identifies the SNARE-cleavage, acetylcholine-blocking mechanism but pairs it with the wrong physiologic effect. Blocking acetylcholine release **reduces**, not increases, sympathetically-mediated vasoconstriction — the net clinical result is **improved digital perfusion**, not "increased sympathetic innervation" (which would worsen vasospasm, the opposite of the therapeutic goal).

**Mechanism:** botulinum toxin cleaves pre-synaptic **SNAREs** (soluble NSF attachment protein receptor) and prevents acetylcholine release from intracellular vesicles. This blocks the pro-vasoconstrictive sympathetic signal at the digital vessels, relieving vasospasm.

**Raynaud's disease:** idiopathic vasospasm of the digital arteries without a known underlying cause (this patient's rheumatologic workup was negative, consistent with primary disease). Usually affects **pre-menopausal women**, presenting with pain and pallor, typically bilateral. Mainstays of treatment: avoiding cold exposure and tobacco, plus **calcium-channel blockers** as first-line medication. When these fail, **botulinum toxin injection** relieves vasoconstriction and decreases ischemia and pain.

**Supporting evidence:** Neumeister et al. reviewed botulinum toxin A for Raynaud's disease and syndrome — injections into the common digital vessel at the palm level produced marked increases (up to 300%) in digital perfusion. They concluded the mechanism is likely multifactorial: central and systemic effects on neurotransmitters in chronic pain pathways, local digital vessel tone, and sympathetic innervation.
cards.correctAnswer: Improving digital perfusion by cleaving pre-synaptic SNAREs and preventing acetylcholine release
cards.wrongAnswer: Increasing sympathetic innervation by cleaving pre-synaptic SNAREs and preventing acetylcholine release

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Pediatric Wrist / Kienbock's Disease
cards.front: Pediatric Kienbock's Disease — Temporary Scaphotrapeziotrapezoidal (STT) Pinning Is the Distinguishing Treatment
cards.back:
Wrong answer chosen (Figure B). Correct: **Figure D — Kienbock's disease in adolescents.** Radiographic findings: increased density and slight lunate collapse.

**Mechanism of temporary STT pinning:** decreases radiolunate contact stress while increasing load transfer through the **radioscaphoid articulation** — effectively offloading the diseased lunate. Compare with adult Lichtman-stage management in entry [7]; STT pinning is a pediatric-specific joint-leveling option distinct from the PRC/fusion options used for adult Stage IIIB/IV disease.

**None of the other pictured pediatric hand/wrist conditions are treated with STT pinning:**
- Radial clubhand
- Scaphoid fracture
- Hypoplastic thumb

**Supporting evidence:**
- Ando et al. retrospectively reviewed 6 adolescents treated with temporary scaphotrapezoidal (ST) pinning: all had improved wrist flexion/extension arc, improved strength, and increased lunate signal intensity on MRI compared to preoperative baseline (a sign of revascularization).
- Shigematsu et al. published a case study of an 11-year-old with wrist pain at rest and with use, treated with temporary STT pinning plus 8 weeks of cast immobilization. Both wrist ROM and grip strength improved, with lunate revascularization seen on follow-up MRI.
cards.correctAnswer: Figure D — Kienbock's disease in adolescents

cards.front: Macrodactyly — Time Epiphysiodesis to When the Finger Matches the Same-Sex Parent's Length, Not "Now" or "At Skeletal Maturity"
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Osteotomy at skeletal maturity. Correct: **Epiphysiodesis when the finger reaches adult length of the father** (i.e., the same-sex parent).

**Macrodactyly:** a rare congenital malformation causing enlargement of all structures of the affected digit (here, the middle finger).

**Timing is the key concept:** epiphysiodesis is performed **once the affected finger reaches the length of the same-sex parent's corresponding digit**, using that digit as a template for the target final length — not immediately ("now") and not deferred to skeletal maturity, since either extreme would leave the finger disproportionate to the rest of the hand (too short if done now while the hand is still growing, too long if the overgrown digit is left to keep pace with the rest of the growing hand until skeletal maturity).

**What doesn't work:** resection of hypertrophic nerves has **not** been shown to be effective in preventing overgrowth.

**Supporting evidence:** Ishida et al. reviewed 23 surgically treated macrodactyly cases: favorable results with epiphysiodesis/epiphysiodectomy, while resection of hypertrophic nerves failed to prevent overgrowth. Epiphysiodesis was performed once the affected finger reached the length of the same-sex parent's finger.
cards.correctAnswer: Epiphysiodesis when the finger reaches adult length of the father (i

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Pediatric Forearm / Congenital Radioulnar Synostosis
cards.front: Congenital Radioulnar Synostosis — Associated with Klinefelter's Syndrome (47 XXY), Not Trisomy 21
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Trisomy 21. Correct: **47 XXY (Klinefelter's syndrome).**

**Congenital radioulnar synostosis (CRUS):** presents with the forearm fixed in a position of pronation (here, 80 degrees) with normal ulnohumeral (elbow flexion/extension) motion. Diagnosis is most often made around **age 6**, when the child demonstrates fixed pronation and compensates with shoulder abduction.

**Pathogenesis:** thought to result from a failure of differentiation during the **7th week in utero**. More common in males (3:2). **60% of cases are bilateral.** There is some genetic association — most cases are inherited in an **autosomal dominant** fashion in chromosomally normal individuals, but **one-third of cases are associated with syndromes**, including:
- **Apert syndrome**
- **Arthrogryposis**
- **Klinefelter's syndrome (47 XXY)** — the tested chromosomal association here

Trisomy 21 and Trisomy 18 are not part of this syndromic association list.

**Management:**
- Most unilateral, asymptomatic cases: **non-operative management**. Slight fixed pronation is often well-tolerated functionally, since it approximates the position used for typing on a keyboard.
- Synostosis excision with soft tissue interposition yields only **mild** improvements in motion.
- Bilateral cases: **forearm derotational osteotomy** may be indicated.

**Supporting evidence:** Cleary and Omer reviewed 23 patients (36 elbows) with CRUS, average 30 degrees of fixed pronation. No surgical correction was attempted, since forearm position was not related to functional limitation or employment status — most patients had no limitations despite jobs requiring forearm use. They concluded treatment for CRUS is rarely indicated.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / PIP Dislocation / Dorsal Fracture-Dislocation Treatment
cards.front: Dorsal PIPJ Fracture-Dislocation — Extension-Block Splinting Is for Stable Reductions with <40% Articular Involvement
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Figure A. Correct: **Figure B.**

**Dorsal PIPJ dislocations** are common (jamming/hyperextension injuries). Without an associated fracture, or with only a small volar plate avulsion, these are typically treated with **closed reduction and buddy taping**.

**When dorsal extension-block splinting is indicated:** the injury must be **stable following reduction** and have an associated **intra-articular fracture of the middle phalanx base involving <40% of the articular surface**. The extension-block splint maintains reduction while permitting early motion within the splint's constraints — critical because early ROM (even if constrained) minimizes scar formation and subsequent PIPJ contracture.

**Why Figure A was the wrong choice:** it did not meet the specific radiographic criteria (stable post-reduction fracture pattern involving <40% of the articular surface) that make extension-block splinting the appropriate treatment — review the comparative fracture involvement across Figures A-E on Orthobullets to fix the visual pattern for both the <40% (splint-appropriate) and >40%-or-unstable (surgical) fracture patterns.

**Take-home decision tree for dorsal PIPJ fracture-dislocation:**
- No fracture / small volar plate avulsion → closed reduction + buddy taping
- Stable post-reduction, intra-articular fracture <40% of articular surface → dorsal extension-block splint + early motion
- Unstable post-reduction, or fracture ≥40% of articular surface → operative fixation

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Pediatric Hand / Congenital Clasped Thumb
cards.front: Congenital Clasped Thumb — New Topic: Flexion-Adduction Deformity from EPB/EPL Absence, First-Line Treatment Is Serial Splinting
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Thenar muscle tendon transfer. Correct: **Serial splinting of the thumb.**

**Congenital clasped thumb:** a flexion-adduction deformity of the thumb that persists beyond the 3rd or 4th month of life (the thumb is normally flexed into the palm in the first few months as a physiologic finding — persistence past that point is what makes it pathologic). Usually caused by absence or hypoplasia of the **extensor pollicis brevis (EPB)** and/or **extensor pollicis longus (EPL)**.

**Family history relevance in this case:** the mother's report that her other two children required operative management doesn't change the first-line approach for *this* child — conservative management is still attempted first for every new case, regardless of family history, since response to splinting is type-dependent and not predictable purely from siblings' outcomes.

**Tsuyuguchi classification (Type I-III), based on the anatomy of the deformity:**
- All types are initially managed conservatively with **serial splinting for 3-6 months**
- **Type I** deformities generally respond well to splinting
- **Types II and III** tend to have poor response to splinting and typically require operative management after conservative treatment fails

**Surgical options (reserved for failed conservative management):** first web-space widening and tendon transfer (e.g., to reconstruct extensor function).

**Supporting evidence:**
- Tsuyuguchi et al. reviewed 75 hands with congenital clasped thumb, identifying three deformity groups. All Type I patients did well with splinting; Type II and III had poor splinting response and required surgery.
- Hisham Abdel Ghani et al. reviewed characteristics in 40 patients with congenital clasped thumb, concluding conservative management should be initiated first in all Type I and II patients.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Rheumatoid Arthritis / MCP Joint Reconstruction
cards.front: RA MCP Joints with Fixed Deformity — MCP Arthroplasty Gives the Best Extensor Lag/Ulnar Drift Correction at 1 Year
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Tenosynovectomies with extensor reconstructions (central slip imbrication, Fowler distal tenotomy). Correct: **Metacarpophalangeal joint arthroplasties.**

**Key point — severity dictates the procedure:** this patient has **severe, fixed** MCP deformities (not passively correctable). **MCP arthroplasty is the procedure of choice for severe finger MCP joint arthritis with fixed deformities** — soft-tissue-only procedures (tenosynovectomy, extensor reconstruction) are for milder, non-fixed disease and would not be expected to correct an already-fixed deformity.

**Why the wrong answer fails:** tenosynovectomy with extensor reconstruction (central slip imbrication, Fowler tenotomy) addresses **extensor tendon balance**, not fixed joint-level deformity — it's a soft tissue procedure mismatched to a bony/joint-level problem.

**Thumb exception:** thumb MCP involvement in RA is treated with **arthrodesis** in most cases, not arthroplasty — the finger MCP vs. thumb MCP treatment algorithms diverge here.

**Supporting evidence:**
- Chung et al. (Level 2) studied 81 RA patients with MCP finger joint involvement treated with silicone implant MCP arthroplasty: both radial- and ulnar-sided fingers showed improved ulnar drift from baseline to 1 year.
- Kimball et al. (Level 5 review): MCP arthroplasty in RA patients yields an expected arc of motion of **40-60 degrees**, with improvement in finger extension (i.e., reduced extensor lag) and ulnar deviation.
- Joyce (Level 5) reviewed MCP arthroplasty implant designs, including three different silicone implant types.
cards.wrongAnswer: Tenosynovectomies with extensor reconstructions (central slip imbrication, Fowler distal tenotomy)

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Congenital Hand / Polydactyly Genetics
cards.front: Postaxial Polydactyly — Autosomal Dominant Pedigree; Same Pedigree-Reading Pitfall as Entry [13]
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Figure B. Correct: **Figure D** — the pedigree depicting an autosomal dominant pattern. As with entry [13] (congenital cleft hand), the miss here is pedigree-reading, not the underlying genetics concept. Re-apply the autosomal dominant pedigree checklist from entry [13]: affected in every generation, both sexes affected roughly equally, every affected individual has an affected parent, ~50% of an affected parent's offspring are affected.

**Polydactyly:** the most common congenital hand malformation — an extra digit. Diagnosis is clinical. Classified by location of the extra digit:
- **Preaxial** (thumb side) — more common in **Caucasians**
- **Central**
- **Postaxial** (small-finger side) — most common in **African Americans**; has an **autosomal dominant** inheritance pattern

**Red flag:** postaxial polydactyly in a **Caucasian** patient is rare and should prompt workup for associated syndromes, including **chondroectodermal dysplasia (Ellis-van Creveld syndrome)** — see entry [15] for EVC's autosomal recessive EVC-gene inheritance, a different genetic pattern from isolated postaxial polydactyly itself.

**Treatment:** formal reconstruction for well-formed extra digits; amputation for rudimentary/vestigial digits.

**Supporting evidence:**
- Umair et al. reviewed genetic and molecular features of polydactyly, highlighting recently identified genes/loci for non-syndromic polydactyly and the complex genetic mechanisms involved.
- Scott-Emuakpor et al. analyzed polydactyly in a Nigerian population: slight male preponderance, autosomal dominant inheritance pattern with **65% penetrance**; no phenotypic (illustration continues past visible text — review full explanation on Orthobullets for complete findings).
cards.correctAnswer: Figure D — the pedigree depicting an autosomal dominant pattern

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Flexor Tendon Injury / Partial Laceration Repair
cards.front: Partial (75%) FDP Laceration — Epitendinous Repair of the Cut Edges Alone Is Sufficient to Prevent Gap Formation
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Core and epitendinous suture repair. Correct: **Epitendinous suture repair of the cut edges only** — this alone is enough to prevent gap formation, so adding a core suture is unnecessary.

**General repair thresholds for partial flexor tendon lacerations:**
- **≤50% laceration with triggering**, or **50-60% laceration without triggering** → most surgeons choose **no repair**
- **Up to 75% laceration** → the tendon can tolerate early, non-resisted active mobilization even without formal core repair, but **most hand surgeons will still repair 75% partial lacerations**
- When repairing a 75% partial laceration: **epitendinous suture of the cut edges alone** is sufficient — a core suture adds bulk and potential gliding resistance without improving gap-formation prevention.

**Supporting evidence:** Haddad et al. compared repair vs. no repair of 75% lacerated sheep flexor tendons. Peripheral (epitendinous) repair alone or peripheral + core repair both reduced gap formation to ≤1mm at 500 cycles, with **no difference between the two repair groups**. Nonrepaired tendons gapped early (by 100 cycles). Conclusion: gapping is reduced by a peripheral repair, whether or not a core suture is added — meaning the core suture contributes nothing extra in this setting.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Carpal Instability / Midcarpal Instability (CIND)
cards.front: Midcarpal Instability — Diagnostic Exam Is a Clunk with Axial + Palmar Load Moving from Neutral to Ulnar Deviation
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Pain and a clunk on ulnar-to-radial deviation while pressure is held on the scaphoid (this describes **Watson's scaphoid shift test** for scapholunate instability, not midcarpal instability — a different test for a different diagnosis). Correct: **Pain and a clunk with axial and palmarly directed forces as the wrist is moved from neutral to ulnar deviation.**

**Carpal instability taxonomy — where midcarpal instability fits:**
- **Carpal instability dissociative (CID):** intrinsic ligamentous disruption (e.g., scapholunate or lunotriquetral ligament tears — this is the category Watson's test screens for)
- **Carpal instability non-dissociative (CIND):** extrinsic ligamentous disruption, either between carpal rows or between the proximal row and distal radius. Includes:
  - **Midcarpal instability (MCI)**
  - Radiocarpal instability

**Midcarpal instability exam finding:** a **clunk** as the wrist moves from a **neutral position with the forearm pronated** into **ulnar deviation**, combined with an **axial and palmarly directed load**. This reflects the proximal carpal row snapping from a volar-flexed position into extension as ulnar deviation is reached.

**Radiographic/dynamic findings:** plain radiographs are often normal or show only a mild VISI deformity. **Videofluoroscopy is diagnostic**, capturing the proximal row's volar-flexed-to-extended "snap" during ulnar deviation.

**Clinical context:** ligamentous laxity (as in this gymnast) is a recognized predisposing factor for midcarpal instability.

**Supporting evidence:**
- Lichtman et al. provided a historical overview of carpal instability, dividing it into dissociative and non-dissociative categories and describing multiple causes/patterns leading to carpal subluxation.
