- Apergis et al. described 14 cases of midcarpal instability treated with ligamentous reefing of the midcarpal and/or radiolunate joint: excellent results in 8 cases, good in 5, fair in 1.
cards.correctAnswer: Pain and a clunk with axial and palmarly directed forces as the wrist is moved from neutral to ulnar deviation

cards.front: Digit Replantation Reperfusion Injury — Allopurinol Blocks the Hypoxanthine-to-Xanthine Step and Improves Outcomes
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Leeches. Correct: **Allopurinol.**

**Why leeches don't fit this question:** leeches are used for **venous congestion** after replantation (via hirudin's anticoagulant effect, promoting local bleeding to relieve venous outflow problems) — a completely different mechanism from the ischemia-reperfusion pathway being asked about here. They don't inhibit xanthine oxidase or affect the hypoxanthine-to-xanthine-to-uric acid conversion at all.

**Reperfusion injury mechanism:** during ischemia, ATP breaks down toward hypoxanthine. On reperfusion, **xanthine oxidase** converts hypoxanthine → xanthine → uric acid, generating reactive oxygen species that drive reperfusion injury.

**Allopurinol** is a **xanthine oxidase inhibitor** — blocking this step reduces oxidative reperfusion injury. (Same mechanism as its use in gout: inhibiting xanthine oxidase lowers uric acid production.)

**Supporting evidence:** Waikakul et al. ran a randomized controlled trial (2-year follow-up) of thumb replantation with vs. without adjunctive allopurinol — 60 trial patients, 38 controls, all young healthy laborers with sharp or locally crushed proximal-phalanx thumb amputations and total ischemic time >10 hours. Standard replantation management was used in both groups, except the trial group received 300mg oral allopurinol on admission plus 300mg daily for 5 more days. The allopurinol group had a **lower infection rate, less postoperative pain and chronic swelling, and better recovery of sensation** than controls.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Pediatric Hand / Trigger Thumb Surgical Technique
cards.front: Pediatric Trigger Thumb — A1 Pulley Release Alone; Do Not Resect Notta's Nodule
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Pediatric trigger thumb, open release of the A1 pulley AND resection of the tendon nodule. Correct: **Pediatric trigger thumb, open release of the A1 pulley alone** (no nodule resection).

**Key point extending entry [16]:** once surgery is indicated for pediatric trigger thumb, the procedure is **A1 pulley release only** — the thickened FPL tendon nodule (**Notta's nodule**) is left alone. It resolves on its own once the pulley constriction is released; resecting it adds risk without added benefit.

**Diagnostic clarification — fixed flexion, not triggering:** despite the name, pediatric trigger thumb classically presents as a **fixed flexion deformity of the IP joint**, not true triggering/catching. It's now believed to be an **acquired**, not congenital, condition. **Notta's nodule** (thickening of the FPL tendon and its sheath) is the associated finding, distinct from the diagnosis itself.

**Non-surgical management:** its role remains unclear. If splinting/stretching is pursued, the required duration is long (up to 30 months) and compliance is difficult — this doesn't invalidate splinting as an option, but tempers expectations. Spontaneous resolution potential drops substantially **beyond age 2**, which supports moving to surgery at this patient's age.

**Why percutaneous release doesn't have an advantage here:** general anesthesia is required for the procedure regardless of percutaneous vs. open technique in this population, so there's no anesthesia-avoidance benefit to going percutaneous.

**Associated finding:** MCP hyperextension is associated with pediatric trigger thumb (compensatory, similar in spirit to the MCP hyperextension seen with untreated camptodactyly — entry [4] — though the underlying pathology differs).

**Timing guidance for surgery vs. observation (per Marek et al.):**
- A 2-year-old with a locked (non-triggering, fixed) thumb for 6 months → **surgery**
- A child **<1 year** with true triggering (not locked) → **observation**

**Supporting evidence:**
- Shah et al. reviewed pediatric trigger thumb, noting its association with MCP hyperextension and no advantage to percutaneous release since general anesthesia is required either way.
- Marek et al. performed a retrospective review and survey of pediatric trigger thumb surgery: age at surgery influences residual flexion contracture and recovery rate. Surgery was safe and effective; their recommendations are the age-based guidance above.
cards.correctAnswer: Pediatric trigger thumb, open release of the A1 pulley alone (no nodule resection)
cards.wrongAnswer: Pediatric trigger thumb, open release of the A1 pulley AND resection of the tendon nodule

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Scaphoid Fracture / Percutaneous Fixation vs. Cast
cards.front: Percutaneous Screw Fixation of Non-Displaced Scaphoid Waist Fractures — Faster Union, Not Better Long-Term Motion/Grip or Lower Union Rates
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Higher union rates. Correct: **Reduced time to fracture union.**

**Key distinction — percutaneous screw fixation speeds healing but does not change the ultimate outcome:** compared to cast immobilization, screw fixation gives a **shorter time to union** (roughly 6-7 weeks vs. 10-12 weeks) and faster return to work/sports — but **not** higher union rates, better final grip strength, or better final range of motion. The endpoint is the same; the path there is faster.

**Cost consideration:** despite higher upfront surgical cost, the **indirect cost savings from quicker return to work can offset the direct cost** of the procedure.

**Supporting evidence:**
- Arora et al.: indirect cost reduction from faster return to work offset the direct surgical costs. The operative group had a better mean DASH score than the conservative group. Mean time to union: **43 days (screw fixation) vs. 74 days (cast)**.
- Bond et al., in active military personnel: faster healing with screw fixation, but **no difference in ultimate union rates, final grip strength, or final range of motion** between percutaneous screw fixation and non-operative treatment. Mean time to union: **7 weeks (screw) vs. 12 weeks (cast)**. No significant difference in wrist ROM or grip strength at 2-year follow-up.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Peripheral Nerve Injury / Nerve-Specific Recovery Potential
cards.front: Nerve Graft Repair Motor Recovery Potential — Radial, Musculocutaneous, and Femoral Are the Best-Recovering Nerves
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Peroneal. Correct: **Radial** (of the choices offered).

**Nerve-specific motor recovery potential after graft repair (Roganovic's tiering) — worth memorizing as a set, since this is a distinct concept from the Sunderland/Seddon injury-severity classification in entry [26]:**
- **Excellent recovery potential:** radial, musculocutaneous, femoral
- **Moderate recovery potential:** median, ulnar, tibial
- **Poor recovery potential:** peroneal

**Why this matters clinically:** the same graft technique and the same injury severity can produce very different motor outcomes depending purely on *which* nerve was injured — the peroneal nerve is the classic example of a nerve with disproportionately poor recovery even after a technically sound repair, which affects counseling and expectations for patients undergoing common peroneal nerve graft reconstruction.

**Supporting evidence:**
- Roganovic performed a prospective study of 393 graft repairs across the median, ulnar, radial, tibial, peroneal, and musculocutaneous nerves, showing motor recovery potential differs significantly by nerve and by level of repair, per the tiers above.
- Mohler et al. recommend testing nerve action potentials at the time of nerve exploration to guide surgical treatment decisions.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Thumb CMC Arthritis / Intra-Articular Injection
cards.front: Thumb Basal Joint (CMC) Arthritis Injections — Steroid, Hylan, and Saline Are Roughly Equivalent; Verified Against Current Literature
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Steroid. Correct: **All of the above are equally effective** (steroid, hylan, and saline).

You flagged this as an older question and asked whether it still holds — checked against current literature and it does.

**Original Orthobullets evidence (older RCTs):**
- Heyworth et al.: all three injections (steroid, hylan, saline) were similarly effective for about 3 months, reducing pain and improving thumb function versus baseline.
- Stahl et al.: steroid and hylan were equally effective, but this study did not include a saline control arm.
- Henderson et al.: no advantage of hyaluronan over saline for **knee** arthritis over a 5-week course (extrapolated evidence, not thumb-specific).

**Current literature check (2023-2024 meta-analyses) confirms the same conclusion still holds:**
- A frequentist network meta-analysis comparing corticosteroid, hyaluronic acid, PRP, and placebo for thumb base osteoarthritis found no significant difference in pain and functional outcomes between corticosteroid injections and hyaluronic acid or PRP.
- A 2024 systematic review/meta-analysis of corticosteroid injection for trapeziometacarpal osteoarthritis, and a separate meta-analysis of corticosteroid vs. hyaluronic acid, both found no significant difference in VAS pain, grip strength, or tip pinch strength between corticosteroid and hyaluronic acid at short- and medium-term follow-up (one analysis found a modest reduction in activity-related pain favoring corticosteroid in the medium term, but the effect was small).
- The ongoing Norwegian PICASSO trial is randomizing patients to steroid injection, saline injection, or occupational therapy specifically to further test this question, which tells you the field still treats it as unsettled enough to warrant a large trial — consistent with "no clear winner" rather than steroid being definitively superior.

**Take-home for boards purposes:** the tested answer ("all equally effective") remains defensible based on current evidence, not just the older Orthobullets citations.

Sources:
- [Efficacy of Intra-Articular Corticosteroid Injection for Nonsurgical Management of Trapeziometacarpal Osteoarthritis: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of RCTs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0363502324000558)
- [Injection therapy for base of thumb osteoarthritis: a systematic review and meta-analysis](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6747875/)
- [Corticosteroid Versus Hyaluronic Acid Intra-articular Injections for Pain Relief in Trapeziometacarpal Joint Osteoarthritis: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12702583/)
- [Comparison between intra-articular injections of corticosteroids, hyaluronic acid, PRP and placebo for thumb base osteoarthritis: A frequentist network meta-analysis](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10587673/)
- [Study protocol for the PICASSO trial](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC11636302/)
cards.correctAnswer: All of the above are equally effective (steroid, hylan, and saline)

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Pediatric Hand / Trigger Finger (Distinct from Trigger Thumb)
cards.front: Pediatric Trigger Finger Revision Surgery — Extensile Incision, Release A3 Pulley, Then Resect an FDS Slip if Triggering Persists
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Horizontal incision with release of 1 FDS slip first, then A3 pulley release if triggering persists (wrong incision type AND wrong step order). Correct: **Extensile incision and release of the A3 pulley; if triggering persists, release 1 slip of the FDS tendon.**

**Two errors to fix in the wrong answer:** (1) the incision should be **extensile**, not horizontal — since pediatric trigger finger pathology can arise at multiple sites along the flexor sheath, an extensile approach is needed to explore and address whichever site is responsible; (2) the **order of steps** is release A3 pulley first, then resect an FDS slip if that doesn't resolve triggering — not the reverse.

**Pediatric trigger finger vs. trigger thumb — these are distinct diagnoses, don't conflate with entries [16]/[41]:**
| | Trigger thumb | Trigger finger |
|---|---|---|
| Relative frequency | **10x more common** than trigger finger | Less common |
| Presentation | Fixed **flexion contracture** (little true triggering) | Palpable **Notta's nodule proximal to A1 pulley**, flexion contracture, AND true triggering |
| Cause | A1 pulley constriction | Abnormal FDP/FDS relationship — more proximal FDS decussation, nodules in FDS or FDP, thickened A2 pulley, or tight A3 pulley |

**Revision surgery algorithm after failed A1 pulley release (this question):** extensile incision → release A3 pulley → if triggering persists, release 1 slip of FDS → (per general principles) circumferential FDP debulking would be a further escalation step if needed, though not reached in the correct answer choice here.

**Supporting evidence:**
- Moon et al. prospectively followed 7,700 children with no baseline incidence of trigger finger/thumb, then reviewed 35 trigger thumbs and 8 trigger fingers. Surgical release was performed in 23 thumbs; spontaneous resolution occurred in 12 thumb cases and all 8 finger cases. They concluded trigger finger develops earlier in life and has a higher spontaneous resolution rate than trigger thumb.
- Cardon et al. retrospectively reviewed 239 trigger digits in 176 children: trigger fingers accounted for 14% of digits and 10% of patients. Average age at presentation 31 months, average age at onset 11 months. Digit distribution: 9 long, 4 ring, 5 small.
cards.correctAnswer: Extensile incision and release of the A3 pulley; if triggering persists, release 1 slip of the FDS tendon

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Congenital Hand / Anomaly-Syndrome Associations Overview
cards.front: Congenital Hand Anomaly Syndrome Associations — Postaxial Polydactyly in a Caucasian Patient Needs an EVC/Chondroectodermal Dysplasia Workup
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Figure E (macrodactyly). Correct: **Figure B — postaxial polydactyly.**

**Why this is the answer:** postaxial polydactyly is **10x more common in African Americans**; its occurrence in a **Caucasian individual is rare** and warrants further workup for **chondroectodermal dysplasia (Ellis-van Creveld syndrome)** — reinforcing entries [15] and [37] on EVC's autosomal recessive EVC-gene inheritance and postaxial polydactyly's usual autosomal dominant pattern in isolation.

**This question is a good consolidated reference for congenital hand anomaly-syndrome pattern recognition — useful as a single lookup table:**
| Anomaly (Figure) | Key Fact | Associated Syndromes |
|---|---|---|
| Constriction band / pseudosyndactyly (A) | **Not** a failure of differentiation — caused by injury from amniotic bands **after** the fingers have already formed | Amniotic band syndrome / Streeter's dysplasia (entries [18], [24]) |
| Postaxial polydactyly (B) | Rare in Caucasians → workup needed | Chondroectodermal dysplasia (Ellis-van Creveld) |
| Preaxial polydactyly (C) | More common in Caucasians; usually sporadic | **Triphalangism variant** specifically associated with **Holt-Oram syndrome** and **Fanconi anemia** (ties to entry [1]'s radial club hand/FA workup) |
| Syndactyly (D) | Abnormal interconnection between adjacent digits | **Apert syndrome**, **Poland syndrome** |
| Macrodactyly (E) | Overgrowth of all structures of the involved digit (entry [32]) | **Neurofibromatosis**, **Klippel-Trenaunay-Weber syndrome** |

**Embryologic distinction worth remembering:** most congenital hand anomalies (polydactyly, syndactyly, macrodactyly) arise from a **failure of differentiation or formation during embryogenesis**, whereas constriction band syndrome is fundamentally different — a **post-formation injury** from amniotic bands, not a primary developmental failure.

**Source:** Kozin — a Current Concepts Review summarizing upper limb embryogenesis and the most common congenital upper extremity anomalies (same author associated with phalangeal fracture management in entries [23], [29]).

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Scaphoid Fracture / Surgical Approach Selection
cards.front: Displaced Scaphoid Waist Fracture with Cyst Formation — Volar Approach, Not Dorsal, for Distal Pole and Waist Fractures
cards.back:
Wrong answer: ORIF with bone grafting through a dorsal approach. Correct: **ORIF with bone grafting through a volar approach.**

**Approach selection by fracture location — the core rule (extends entry [23]'s dorsal-approach discussion to a different fracture location):**
- **Distal pole and waist fractures** → approached from the **volar** side, especially when there's a **humpback deformity** (this patient's presentation: delayed presentation with plateaued improvement at 6 weeks, cyst formation — consistent with a waist fracture that has developed some collapse/deformity)
- **Proximal pole fractures** → more easily accessed from the **dorsal** side

**When to add bone graft:** injuries with **significant comminution** or **cyst formation** from extended time-to-treatment are often augmented with bone graft — both features present in this patient given the 6-week delay before presentation. **Vascularized bone graft use in nonunion cases remains controversial.**

**Supporting evidence:**
- Rettig et al. reported on 14 patients undergoing acute surgical fixation of displaced scaphoid waist fractures: 13 united and regained functional wrist ROM and grip strength. They advocate early operative intervention for these fractures.
- Raskin et al. described the dorsal approach specifically for **proximal pole** scaphoid fractures — good visualization and the ability to bone graft through the same incision, with successful union reported.
cards.correctAnswer: ORIF with bone grafting through a volar approach
cards.wrongAnswer: ORIF with bone grafting through a dorsal approach

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Vascular / Buerger's Disease (Thromboangiitis Obliterans)
cards.front: Buerger's Disease — Painful, Not Painless; Arteriography Is Diagnostic; Smoking Cessation Is the Only Proven Treatment
cards.back:
Wrong answer: The condition is usually painless. Correct: **Arteriography is useful in the diagnosis of his condition.** (The "usually painless" statement is false — Buerger's disease classically presents with pain.)

**Buerger's disease (thromboangiitis obliterans):** an inflammatory occlusive disorder of small and medium-sized vessels of the digits, occurring almost exclusively in **male smokers**. Classic presentation: **rest pain, claudication, and ulceration** — this is a painful condition, not a painless one. It can mimic other autoimmune and vascular diseases, which is part of why the workup matters.

**Diagnosis:** when uncertain, **arteriography is the study of choice**. Classic finding: **"corkscrew" arteries**, resulting from vascular destruction — diagnostic of Buerger's disease in the appropriate clinical setting.

**Treatment — the one thing proven to work:** **smoking cessation is the only intervention shown to reduce the risk of amputation.** Aspirin has not been shown to decrease amputation incidence, and prophylactic amputation of unaffected digits does not improve outcomes.

**Disease pattern:** Buerger's disease characteristically progresses distally-to-proximally in terms of clinical involvement (small/medium vessels of the digits first), the reverse of the wrong distractor answer suggesting proximal-vessel-first involvement.

**Supporting evidence:** Phillips et al. reviewed vascular conditions of the upper extremity: Buerger's disease patients present with rest pain, claudication, and ulceration; smoking cessation decreases disease progression and amputation incidence.
cards.correctAnswer: Arteriography is useful in the diagnosis of his condition

cards.front: Felon — Midvolar Incision Centered on Point of Maximal Tenderness, Left Open for Secondary Closure; Never a Hemispherical/Fishmouth Incision
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Incision and drainage with a medial-to-lateral hemispherical incision and secondary closure (wrong incision type). Correct: **Incision and drainage with a midline volar incision and secondary closure.**

**Felon:** infection of the digital pulp at the fingertip. Presents with swelling and erythema over the affected finger; many patients have **no history of penetrating trauma** despite that being the presumed mechanism. Most common organism: **Staphylococcus aureus**.

**Treatment — once abscess formation has occurred, incision and drainage is indicated:**
- Incision centered directly over the **point of maximal tenderness** — in this case, midvolar
- **Fishmouth incisions are condemned** — they risk vascular compromise of the digital pad
- Recommended incisions: **midvolar** or **high lateral**, guided by where tenderness is maximal
- **Avoid proximal probing** — risk of inoculating the flexor sheath and converting a felon into a flexor tenosynovitis
- Leave the wound **open to heal by secondary intention**, promoting continued drainage; soaks starting at 24-48 hours
- Empiric antibiotics targeting **S. aureus**

**Why the "medial-to-lateral hemispherical incision" answer is wrong:** this describes a fishmouth-type incision pattern — exactly what's condemned due to digital pad vascular compromise, regardless of whether primary or secondary closure is planned afterward.

**Supporting evidence:** Abrams et al. reviewed hand infection treatment. For felons: incision and drainage once abscess has formed; fishmouth incisions condemned for vascular compromise; midvolar or high lateral incisions recommended, guided by point of maximal tenderness; avoid proximal probing (flexor sheath inoculation risk); empiric antibiotics plus healing by secondary intention.
cards.correctAnswer: Incision and drainage with a midline volar incision and secondary closure

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Pediatric Hand / Thumb Duplication (Wassel Classification)
cards.front: Thumb Duplication (Wassel Classification) — Bilhaut-Cloquet Is for Types 1-3, Not Type 6
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Type 6. Correct: **Type 2** (the Bilhaut-Cloquet procedure is successful in Wassel Types 1, 2, and 3).

**Bilhaut-Cloquet procedure:** central portions of bone and nail are removed, then the **radial half of one thumb is combined with the ulnar half of the other** to create a single, composite thumb. Best suited for **Wassel Types 1-3**, where the duplication occurs at a single, distal level (distal phalanx level or similar) with symmetric or near-symmetric components.

**Why higher Wassel types are treated differently:**
- **Asymmetric Type I or II duplications** with distinct components: an alternative approach is **ablation of the smaller thumb with transfer of the collateral ligament and centralization of the extensor tendon** — rather than the fusion-based Bilhaut-Cloquet technique.
- **Types 3 and 4** (Type 4 is the **most common** duplication pattern overall): typically treated by **selecting a dominant thumb and ablating the lesser counterpart**, after preserving and transferring the intrinsics and collateral ligaments from the ablated thumb to the retained one. **Radial thumb ablation is most common/preferred** (per Ogino and per Miller's Review).
- **Types 5 and 6:** managed with the **same principles as Type 4**, but with the added complexity of needing additional **intrinsic muscle reconstruction**.
- **Type 7** (triphalangeal thumb): treatment is variable based on presentation; reconstruction involves either excision or fusion of the extra phalanx.

**Why Type 6 was the wrong choice here:** it falls into the higher-Wassel-type group (5/6) that requires dominant-thumb selection with ablation and intrinsic reconstruction — not the central-fusion Bilhaut-Cloquet technique reserved for Types 1-3.

**Supporting evidence:** Baek et al. (Level 4) reviewed 7 patients with Type 3 and Type 4 duplication treated with an extra-articular modified Bilhaut-Cloquet procedure: excellent IP joint range of motion, no nail deformities, no growth arrest episodes.
cards.correctAnswer: Type 2 (the Bilhaut-Cloquet procedure is successful in Wassel Types 1, 2, and 3)

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Ulnar Variance / Wrist Biomechanics
cards.front: Ulnar Variance and Wrist Load Distribution — 80/20 Is the Neutral-Variance Value, Not the Value for -2.5mm
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Approximately 80% radius / 20% ulna. Correct: **Approximately 95% radius / 5% ulna** (at -2.5mm ulnar variance). You correctly recalled that 80/20 is the **neutral**-variance value — the miss was applying the neutral figure to a question asking about **negative** variance specifically.

**Ulnar variance:** describes the cranio-caudal position of the distal ulna relative to the distal radius at the wrist.

**Load distribution by variance (the full set to keep straight):**
- **Neutral variance:** ~80% radius / ~20% ulna
- **-2.5mm (negative) variance:** ~95% radius / ~5% ulna — less ulnar head prominence, so it accepts less load
- **+2.5mm (positive) variance:** ~60% radius / ~40% ulna — more ulnar head prominence, so it accepts more load (relevant to ulnar impaction syndrome, see entry [27])

**Quantified relationship (Palmer and Werner):** a 2.5mm **increase** in ulnar variance increases the ulnocarpal joint's share of load from 18% to 42%; a 2.5mm **decrease** in ulnar variance decreases the ulnocarpal joint's load share to 4.3%. (Note: these ulnocarpal-joint-specific percentages differ slightly from the "5%/20%/40%" framing above because they isolate the ulnocarpal joint's contribution specifically, but the direction and magnitude of the relationship are the same core concept.)

**Supporting evidence:**
- Palmer and Werner's biomechanical study established that wrist loading characteristics depend directly on radioulnar variance, with the load-shift magnitudes above.
- Friedman and Palmer reviewed the clinical diagnosis, pathophysiology, and treatment of ulnar impaction syndrome — the clinical entity that results from the increased ulnar-sided load seen with positive ulnar variance.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Traumatic Amputation / Ray Resection and Transposition
cards.front: Mutilating Ring Finger Amputation — Fourth Ray Resection with Fifth Ray Transposition Beats Revision Amputation, and Should Be Done Early
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Early revision amputation. Correct: **Early fourth ray resection with fifth ray transposition.**

**Why revision amputation loses here:** it's an option, but it frequently leads to **decreased grip strength** and leaves a stump that is **often repeatedly traumatized** — worse functional and cosmetic outcomes than ray transposition for a mutilating injury at this level.

**Why replantation isn't the answer:** traumatic amputation of the ring finger at the **PIP joint level or more proximal** is a **relative contraindication to replantation**, because post-operative stiffness typically leaves a **nonfunctional digit** even if the replant survives.

**Why "early," not "delayed":** the correct answer specifies early intervention — delaying the fourth ray resection/transposition doesn't confer benefit that would offset the downside of leaving a traumatized central-ray stump longer than necessary.

**Fourth ray resection with fifth ray transposition — the mechanism of benefit:** closes the gap between the remaining digits, producing a **more cosmetic hand appearance** while simultaneously **increasing grip strength** (the transposed fifth ray restores a functional border to the hand rather than leaving a gap next to the ulnar-most digit).

**Supporting evidence:**
- Monreal performed a retrospective review of mutilating ring finger injuries treated with fourth ray resection and radial transposition of the fifth ray: improved grip strength and Result Assessment Scores (RAS) after the procedure. Fourth ray resection with fifth ray transposition and hamate-capitate arthrodesis was found to be **technically easier than metacarpal osteotomy**, while still improving cosmesis and hand function.
- Bhat et al. reviewed functional outcomes and cosmesis after single-digit amputations: for patients with significant gapping after central digit amputation, adjacent digit transposition provided improved cosmesis, and was associated with significantly higher DASH scores. (The screenshot text was cut off before their full conclusion on how DASH scores compared between groups — worth pulling up the full explanation on Orthobullets if you want the precise comparison, since higher DASH normally indicates worse function and the visible text doesn't fully clarify which group that applied to.) They concluded that despite the management approach chosen for finger amputations, most patients adapt reasonably well overall.
cards.correctAnswer: Early fourth ray resection with fifth ray transposition

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Carpal Tunnel Syndrome / Atypical Presentation from Space-Occupying Lesion
cards.front: Atypical Carpal Tunnel Syndrome from a Space-Occupying Lesion — Get Tissue Diagnosis Before Treating, Even When the Lesion Looks Like Gout
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Surgical excision. Correct: **Establish a tissue diagnosis and refer to a rheumatologist.**

**Why jumping straight to excision is wrong:** even though the clinical picture (painful nodule flaring after alcohol, volar wrist location, CT/MRI findings) points toward **gout** as the presumed diagnosis, the appropriate next step is confirming that diagnosis with **tissue sampling** before committing to a treatment pathway — and directing the patient toward **medical management** (rheumatology referral, e.g., colchicine prophylaxis) rather than surgery as the first move, since gout-driven space-occupying lesions may respond to medical therapy without needing excision at all.

**Atypical CTS red flags — this stem is a template for recognizing when CTS is not idiopathic:**
- **Male** (idiopathic CTS is much more common in **females**)
- **Not middle-aged** (i.e., unusual age for typical CTS)
- **Unilateral** involvement (idiopathic CTS from repetitive use / obesity tends to be more symmetric bilateral risk)
- When these atypical features are present, think **space-occupying lesion**: gout, synovial sarcoma, lipoma, or ganglion.

**Typical (idiopathic) CTS for contrast — the presentation this patient does NOT have:**
- Most common compressive neuropathy, affects up to 10% of the general population
- Risk factors: female sex, advanced age, obesity, repetitive motion activities
- Symptoms: thenar atrophy, numbness in the radial 3.5 digits, night pain, positive Tinel's and Phalen's
- First-line management: non-operative (NSAIDs, night splints, activity modification); carpal tunnel release reserved for those who fail conservative management

**Supporting evidence:** Chen et al. described 23 unusual CTS cases caused by space-occupying lesions. In patients with an atypical presentation — male, non-middle-aged, or unilateral — they recommend investigating for gout, synovial sarcoma, lipoma, and ganglions as underlying causes.
cards.correctAnswer: Establish a tissue diagnosis and refer to a rheumatologist

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Ulnar Nerve Palsy / Bouvier Test for Tendon Transfer Planning
cards.front: Bouvier Test — Blocking Full MCP Extension and Checking for PIP Extension, to Distinguish Simple vs. Complex Ulnar Claw Hand
cards.back:
Wrong answer: PIP joints flex easily with MCP joints extended but not with MCP joints flexed (describes a different maneuver, not the Bouvier test). Correct: **Full MCP extension is blocked, and [the examiner checks whether] PIP joint extension does or does not occur.**

**Clinical context:** ulnar nerve injury produces **intrinsic minus hand / ulnar claw hand** — loss of intrinsic muscle function. Before planning tendon transfers, you need to know whether the deformity is "simple" or "complex," and the Bouvier test answers that.

**How to perform the Bouvier test:** hold the wrist in **neutral**, **block full MCP joint extension**, and observe whether the patient can still extend the PIP joints.

**Interpreting the result:**
- **Positive test** (patient **can** extend the PIP joints despite blocked MCP extension) → indicates **simple claw hand** with an **intact central slip**. Tendon transfer goal: re-create MCP flexion only. Technique: attach tendon to the A1 pulley, A2 pulley, or proximal phalanx.
- **Negative test** (patient **cannot** extend the PIP joints) → indicates **complex claw hand**. Tendon transfer must correct **both** MCP flexion **and** PIP extension, via insertion into the lateral bands.

**Why the wrong answer fails:** it describes PIP flexion behavior relative to MCP position — a different physical finding altogether, not the MCP-extension-blocking maneuver that defines the Bouvier test.

**Supporting evidence:** Sammer and Chung reviewed pertinent median and ulnar nerve anatomy/function in the forearm and hand, covering the differing clinical deficits by injury location and the treatments (risks, benefits, indications, postoperative care) for reconstructing them.
cards.correctAnswer: Full MCP extension is blocked, and [the examiner checks whether] PIP joint extension does or does not occur

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Cubital Tunnel Syndrome / Ulnar Nerve Subluxation
cards.front: Cubital Tunnel Syndrome with Ulnar Nerve Subluxation — the "Snapping Sensation" Clue Means Transposition, Not In-Situ Decompression Alone
cards.back:
Wrong answer: In situ cubital tunnel decompression. Correct: **Cubital tunnel decompression with anterior transposition.**

**The stem clue you missed:** "flexion elicits a **snapping sensation over the medial elbow**" — this describes **ulnar nerve subluxation**, not just simple compression. Subluxation changes the treatment: in-situ decompression alone leaves a subluxating nerve free to keep re-traumatizing itself with elbow flexion, so decompression must be paired with **anterior transposition** to actually eliminate the mechanical problem.

**Cubital tunnel syndrome:** the **second most common** compressive neuropathy of the upper extremity (median/CTS is first). Potential compression sites along the ulnar nerve's course: arcade of Struthers, intermuscular septum, flexor carpi ulnaris fascia, anconeus epitrochlearis, the **Osborne ligament**, and fascial bands within the FCU distally.

**Why the nerve subluxates:** the ulnar nerve sits superficially at the cubital tunnel, and elbow flexion increases tension/traction on it — this combination of superficial position plus flexion-related traction is what predisposes it to both compression **and** subluxation.

**Why anterior transposition specifically helps:** moving the nerve anterior to the medial epicondyle (subcutaneous or submuscular) **shortens the distance the nerve has to travel** with elbow flexion, reducing the traction (and therefore the subluxation and symptoms) that would otherwise persist even after simple decompression.

**Supporting evidence:** Nelson et al. reviewed 417 arthroscopic elbow procedures over 13 years: overall 14% complication rate. Most common complications: elbow stiffness, heterotopic ossification, infection, and transient ulnar neuritis. Infection rate was higher with intraoperative steroid injections. (This reference addresses arthroscopic elbow complications broadly — relevant context for why arthroscopic debridement, answer choice 1, is not the procedure indicated for this nerve-subluxation problem in the first place.)
cards.correctAnswer: Cubital tunnel decompression with anterior transposition

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Scaphoid Fracture / Screw Position and Nonunion
cards.front: Scaphoid Fracture Nonunion After Suboptimal Screw Position — Fix Is Center-Center Positioning via Trapezial Excision, Not Switching to a Dorsal Approach
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Using a dorsal approach with an antegrade screw. Correct: **Achieving a more center-center screw position.**

**Clinical picture:** scaphoid fracture nonunion after volar, retrograde ORIF, with a **suboptimal screw position** — tip completely dorsal to the proximal pole (Figure B), starting point too radial (Figure A). The problem is the screw's positioning within the volar approach that was already used, not the choice of volar vs. dorsal approach itself.

**Why center-center position matters:** it provides for **maximal screw length purchase and construct stiffness**, which together give the highest chance of union. Union rates with operative treatment are generally cited at 90-95%, but that assumes good screw positioning — a suboptimal position (as in this case) undermines those numbers.

**Why the dorsal approach isn't the fix here:** a dorsal approach is **not required** to achieve optimal screw position — both dorsal and volar approaches can achieve center-center placement when executed correctly. This ties back to the location-based approach selection in entries [23] and [47]: approach choice depends on fracture location (proximal pole → dorsal tends to be easier; distal pole/waist → volar), not on fixing a malpositioned screw after the fact.

**The actual technical fix, if sticking with a volar approach:** the **proximal portion of the trapezium may need to be excised** to obtain a proper start point. Excising trapezium allows the start point to move more ulnar and dorsal. Without this step, the start point ends up too volar and radial — exactly the error seen in this case.

**Supporting evidence:** Dodds et al. assessed the biomechanical stability of scaphoid fracture fixation in a cadaveric model: less fracture motion was seen with **longer screws**, and they recommended placing the longest possible screw along the **central axis** of the scaphoid to achieve the stiffest construct.
cards.correctAnswer: Achieving a more center-center screw position
cards.wrongAnswer: Using a dorsal approach with an antegrade screw

cards.front: TFCC Radioulnar Ligament Injury and DRUJ Subluxation — the Relationship Is Crossed: Volar Ligament Injury Causes Dorsal Ulnar Subluxation
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Structure E. Correct: **Structure A — the volar radioulnar ligament.**

**The key relationship to memorize — it's crossed, not matched:** injury to the **volar** radioulnar ligament causes **dorsal** subluxation of the ulna (relative to the radius). By extension, injury to the **dorsal** radioulnar ligament would be expected to cause **volar** subluxation. Don't default to matching the ligament name to the subluxation direction — the mechanics run in the opposite direction.

**TFCC anatomy:** supports stability of the ulnar carpus and the DRUJ; transmits axial forces from the hand to the forearm; stabilizes the radius relative to the ulna during pronation/supination. Components: dorsal and volar radioulnar ligaments, central articular disk, meniscus homolog, ulnar collateral ligament, ECU tendon subsheath, and the origin of the ulnolunate and ulnotriquetral ligaments.

**Mechanism in this case:** disruption of the **deep fibers of the TFCC** (the **ligamentum subcruentum**) causes **DRUJ instability** and subluxation of the ulna relative to the radius. Here specifically, volar radioulnar ligament injury → dorsal ulnar subluxation.

**Diagnosis:** clinical — ulnar-sided wrist pain worse with ulnar deviation, positive **"fovea" sign**. MRI can confirm.

**Treatment:** generally conservative first (NSAIDs, immobilization); surgical debridement, TFCC repair, or ulnar shortening procedures depending on symptom severity and underlying cause.

**Supporting evidence:** Wijffels et al. reviewed untreated DRUJ injuries: MRI is more sensitive and specific than other modalities for TFCC tears that can cause DRUJ instability. They concluded operative treatment of DRUJ injuries should focus on restoring both osseous and ligamentous anatomy.
cards.correctAnswer: Structure A — the volar radioulnar ligament

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Wrist Arthritis / Radioscapholunate Fusion for CIND
cards.front: Isolated Radioscaphoid/Radiolunate Arthritis — Radioscapholunate (RSL) Fusion Preserves Motion by Leaving the Midcarpal Joint Free
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Figure B. Correct: **Figure C — radioscapholunate (RSL) fusion.**

**Why this preserves motion — the core logic:** this patient has arthritis limited to the **radiocarpal** joint (radioscaphoid and radiolunate articulations specifically), with a **functional, uninvolved midcarpal joint**. Fusing only the radiocarpal articulation (RSL fusion) eliminates the painful, arthritic joint while **leaving the midcarpal joint mobile** — that's where the preserved wrist motion comes from. A more extensive fusion would sacrifice midcarpal motion unnecessarily.

**Added technical step — distal scaphoid pole excision:** removing the distal pole of the scaphoid **after** RSL fusion further improves wrist motion, since the distal scaphoid would otherwise abut the (still-mobile) midcarpal joint and limit its excursion.

**Classification context — carpal instability non-dissociative (CIND):**
- **Radiocarpal instability**: between the radius and proximal carpal row (this patient's problem)
- **Midcarpal instability**: between the proximal and distal carpal rows (see entry [39])
- Associated conditions with CIND can include intracarpal ligament injury (scapholunate or lunotriquetral), acute carpal tunnel syndrome, or compartment syndrome

**Supporting evidence:** Biswas et al. reviewed the RSL fusion technique using headless compression screws and local autograft through a dorsal approach, describing technique improvements and clearly defined outcomes for this procedure.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Scar Revision / Z-Plasty Technique
cards.front: Z-Plasty Angle-Lengthening Relationship — 60° Corners Give the Greatest Lengthening (75%), Not a Wider or Narrower Angle
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Figure A. Correct: **Figure C — the 60° Z-plasty.**

**Z-plasty angle-to-lengthening relationship — memorize this set as a group:**
- **30° corners** → lengthens the scar by **25%**
- **45° corners** → lengthens the scar by **50%**
- **60° corners** → lengthens the scar by **75%** (the maximum of the commonly used angles, and the answer here)

**Technique:** Z-plasty creates **two triangular flaps of equal size**, which are then transposed to reposition the scar and reorient its direction. This breaks up a straight-line scar (making it less visually obvious) while releasing tension in the scar tissue, improving both function (contracture release) and cosmesis.

**Orthopaedic-specific uses:** improving motion in the setting of a contracture, and as a **prophylactic technique** to prevent contractures from developing after index surgeries in the fingers (not just for revising existing scars).

**Supporting evidence:**
- Garg et al. reviewed scar revision techniques following facial trauma, emphasizing anatomy, wound healing understanding, and meticulous planning. They note scar revision does not erase surgical scars but can improve cosmesis with proper planning.
- Zito et al. reviewed the history, indications, and techniques of Z-plasty for surgical scar revision, describing the biomechanical properties of skin that allow for manipulation/revision of scar tissue. Orthopaedic indications include release of motion-limiting scars, contracture release, and cosmesis of aberrant scar formation.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Basilar Thumb Arthritis / Wagner Approach Radial Artery Anatomy
cards.front: Wagner Approach for Thumb CMC Suspension Arthroplasty — Radial Artery Is Safest at the 1st Metacarpal Base, Most at Risk Near the Trapezium
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Retraction of the FCR tendon with small hohmann retractors just proximal to the trapezium prior to removal. Correct: **Dissection at the level of the 1st metacarpal base.**

**The key spatial relationship:** during a Wagner approach for thumb CMC (TMC) suspension arthroplasty, the radial artery is **most at risk** during dissection of the **dorsoradial capsule proximal to the trapezium**. Moving distally and more ulnar — to the **1st metacarpal base** — is where the artery is **least at risk**. Retraction maneuvers performed near the trapezium (including FCR retraction just proximal to it) fall into the higher-risk zone, not the safe zone.

**Basilar thumb arthritis (TMC/1st CMC arthritis):** common, more prevalent in women — seen in **25% of men and 40% of women over age 75**. Risk factors: female sex, increasing age, ligamentous laxity. Presents as chronic thumb base pain with radiographic TMC osteoarthritis.

**Treatment pathway:** conservative first (NSAIDs, thumb spica bracing, corticosteroid injection — as already tried and failed in this patient). Surgical treatment depends on disease stage; **trapeziectomy +/- ligament reconstruction** is the most commonly performed procedure, with multiple surgical approach options available (Wagner being one).

**At-risk neurovascular structures during these approaches, broadly:** superficial sensory branches of the radial nerve, and the radial artery itself.

**Supporting evidence:** Pickrell et al. reviewed basilar thumb arthritis, noting it most frequently affects the elderly and postmenopausal women. Initial conservative treatment uses activity modification, orthoses, and (per the visible text) other nonoperative measures before surgery is considered.
cards.correctAnswer: Dissection at the level of the 1st metacarpal base
cards.wrongAnswer: Retraction of the FCR tendon with small hohmann retractors just proximal to the trapezium prior to removal

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / ECU Pathology / ECU Synergy Test
cards.front: ECU Tendonitis — the ECU Synergy Test, Not the Finkelstein Test Maneuver, Is the Confirmatory Exam
cards.back:
Wrong answer: "Patient grasps their thumb within their hand and clenches it with the other fingers, followed by passive ulnar abduction of the wrist by the examiner" — this describes the **Finkelstein test** for **De Quervain's tenosynovitis** (1st dorsal compartment), not ECU pathology. Correct: **ECU synergy test** — elbow flexed to 90°, forearm fully supinated, fingers fully extended, patient performs resisted thumb and middle finger abduction.

**Why this distractor is dangerous:** it's a real, correctly-described physical exam maneuver — just for the wrong condition. Ulnar-sided wrist pain has multiple possible causes (ECU tendonitis/subluxation, TFCC pathology, De Quervain's variants), and the exam findings in this stem (MRI showing ECU tendon thickening/T2 hyperintensity, no intra-articular pathology) point specifically to the **ECU tendon**, not the 1st dorsal compartment tendons that Finkelstein's test evaluates.

**ECU tendonitis and its spectrum:** often coexists with **ECU subluxation/dislocation** out of its ulnar groove — termed **"snapping ECU."** Subluxation results from attenuation or rupture of the ECU subsheath, with the tendon subluxating during **supination** (see entry [9] for the treatment-position implications of ECU instability).

**ECU synergy test technique:** elbow resting on the exam table in 90° flexion, forearm in full supination, wrist neutral, fingers extended — patient performs **resisted thumb and digital abduction**. Reproduction of ulnar-sided wrist pain = positive test, indicative of ECU tendonitis.

**Treatment:** initial — rest and splinting. Refractory cases — surgical reconstruction of the ECU subsheath, possibly combined with wrist arthroscopy to evaluate and treat concurrent pathology (e.g., TFCC tears).

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Hand Infections / Pyogenic Flexor Tenosynovitis
cards.front: Pyogenic Flexor Tenosynovitis — Age >43 Is a Poor-Outcome Risk Factor, Not the Presenting Kanavel Signs Themselves
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Presence of fusiform swelling at the time of presentation (this is a Kanavel sign used for **diagnosis**, not a risk factor for poor **outcome**). Correct: **Patient age.**

**Why the diagnostic signs aren't the answer:** this patient has **all four Kanavel signs** — fusiform swelling, tenderness along the flexor tendon sheath, flexed resting posture of the digit, and pain with passive extension. These signs establish the *diagnosis* of pyogenic flexor tenosynovitis; they are not, by themselves, prognostic risk factors for a *worse outcome* after treatment. The question is asking about outcome risk, not diagnostic criteria — don't conflate the two categories of Kanavel-related facts.

**Pyogenic flexor tenosynovitis:** diagnosis is usually clinical, based on history and the four Kanavel signs above. Treatment: surgical irrigation and debridement, followed by culture-directed antibiotics.

**Risk factors specifically for poorer outcomes after treatment** (increased amputation risk, decreased total active motion) — this is the list the question is actually asking about:
- **Age greater than 43 years** (the tested answer)
- Diabetes mellitus
- Peripheral vascular disease or renal failure
- Presence of **subcutaneous purulence** (this patient's purulence was confined to the tendon sheath, without subcutaneous extension — a favorable feature, not a risk factor, in this case)
- Digital ischemia
- **Polymicrobial infection** (this patient had a monomicrobial MRSA infection — also a favorable feature here, not a risk factor)

**Supporting evidence:** Chapman and Ilyas reviewed diagnosis and management of pyogenic flexor tenosynovitis, noting a lack of standardized treatment algorithms in the literature and high complication rates regardless of the treatment protocol used. Management typically combines prompt antibiotic initiation with surgical decompression; they call for further research to better define exact treatment thresholds.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Vascular / Hypothenar Hammer Syndrome
cards.front: Hypothenar Hammer Syndrome — Blunt Trauma Produces a True Aneurysm with an Intact Endothelial Lining, Not a False (Pseudo) Aneurysm
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Recanalization of the lumen occurs after blunt injury, creating a false aneurysm. Correct: **Contains a true endothelial lining resulting in a uniform dilatation of the artery.**

**The true vs. false (pseudo) aneurysm distinction — this is the core tested concept:**
- **True aneurysm** (what blunt trauma produces here): contains **all arterial wall layers**, including a **true endothelial lining**. This intact lining allows the vessel to **gradually and uniformly dilate**.
- **Pseudoaneurysm** (false aneurysm): results from **penetrating** arterial wall injury. Extravasated hematoma organizes and then **recanalizes**, forming a lumen with **no endothelial lining**.

**Why "recanalization creating a false aneurysm" is the wrong mechanism for this case:** recanalization-without-endothelium is the pseudoaneurysm pathway (penetrating injury), not the blunt-trauma true-aneurysm pathway that fits hypothenar hammer syndrome.

**Hypothenar hammer syndrome:** post-traumatic digital ischemia from aneurysmal change of the **ulnar artery at Guyon's canal**, caused by repetitive blunt trauma (classically hammering with the hypothenar eminence, or here, catching fastballs). Presentation: hypothenar/ring-finger pain, paresthesias along the **ulnar two digits**, cold intolerance, and mottling/blanching from ischemia.

**Associated injury pattern:** this patient's **hook of hamate fracture** is consistent with the blunt mechanism causing ulnar artery injury at Guyon's canal — the two injuries share a common traumatic vector.

**Supporting evidence:** Bernik et al. published a case report of ulnar artery aneurysm and hypothenar hammer syndrome, noting repetitive striking of the palmar ulnar artery as a known cause, with ischemia and digit pain as hallmark symptoms. Despite its rarity, they recommend considering HHS in hand/finger ischemia workups; intervention options include resection and anastomosis.
cards.correctAnswer: Contains a true endothelial lining resulting in a uniform dilatation of the artery
cards.wrongAnswer: Recanalization of the lumen occurs after blunt injury, creating a false aneurysm

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Radial Longitudinal Deficiency / Holt-Oram Syndrome
cards.front: Holt-Oram Syndrome — Screen with Echocardiogram for Cardiac Defects, Not CBC (That's the Fanconi Anemia Workup)
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Complete blood count (CBC). Correct: **Echocardiogram.**

**Why this is a trap:** CBC is exactly what you'd order to screen for **Fanconi anemia** in a radial club hand patient (see entry [1]) — but this patient's specific findings (bilateral RLD, **radioulnar synostosis**, thumb aplasia, syndactyly of the radial two digits) point to a **different** RLD-associated syndrome: **Holt-Oram syndrome**, which requires a **different** screening test.

**Holt-Oram syndrome:** an **autosomal dominant** disorder caused by mutations in the **TBX5** gene. Distinguished from isolated radial longitudinal deficiency (RLD) by its hallmark feature: **congenital heart defects**, most commonly **atrial septal defects (ASDs) and ventricular septal defects (VSDs)**. This is why the appropriate additional test is an **echocardiogram**, not a CBC.

**Radial longitudinal deficiency (RLD):** congenital hypoplasia/absence of the radius causing radial deviation of the hand/wrist and forearm shortening; occurs on a spectrum and is often syndromic (see entry [1] and entry [12] for the full RLD spectrum and centralization timing).

**Full genetic screening checklist for RLD patients (per Kozin et al.) — match the syndrome to the correct test:**
- **Holt-Oram syndrome** → **echocardiogram** (ASD/VSD)
- **Fanconi anemia** → **CBC, peripheral smear, chromosomal breakage analysis** (entry [1])
- **VACTERL** (vertebral anomalies, anal atresia, tracheoesophageal fistula, renal abnormalities, limb differences) → targeted workup by system
- **CHARGE syndrome** (coloboma, heart defects, atresia choanae, growth/developmental retardation, genital/urinary abnormalities, ear abnormalities/deafness) → targeted workup by system

**Supporting evidence:** Kozin et al. reviewed common pediatric congenital hand deformities, covering the RLD spectrum as it relates to thumb hypoplasia (ranging from mild thumb size reduction to complete absence). They emphasize that initial evaluation of these patients must include screening for Holt-Oram syndrome, VACTERL, Fanconi anemia, and CHARGE syndrome, with physical exam and radiographic features guiding which specific genetic/diagnostic tests to pursue.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Vascular / Vein Graft Failure Timeline (extends entry [63])
cards.front: Vein Graft Failure Timeline After Hypothenar Hammer Syndrome Repair — Thrombosis Early, Intimal Hyperplasia Later
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Graft compression secondary to chronic unaddressed hook of hamate nonunion. Correct: **Migration and proliferation of vascular smooth muscle cells at the site of surgical repair** (i.e., **intimal hyperplasia**).

**This patient's case:** hook of hamate fracture complicated by ulnar artery aneurysm at Guyon's canal (hypothenar hammer syndrome — see entry [63]), treated with aneurysm resection and reversed interpositional saphenous vein grafting. Symptoms recur at **6 months**.

**The key timeline to memorize for vein graft failure after vascular reconstruction:**
- **Immediately post-op:** ~98% patency
- **Within the first month:** patency drops to <88%, due to **thrombosis** — the most common cause of clinical failure in this acute window
- **Months 1-18 (the most biologically active period):** an additional 30-50% of grafts fail, this time due to **intimal hyperplasia** — migration and proliferation of vascular smooth muscle cells at the site of the anastomosis/surgical repair

**Why the timeline matters for this question:** failure at **6 months** falls squarely in the 1-18 month intimal hyperplasia window, not the acute thrombosis window (<1 month) and not a mechanical compression problem from an unrelated, unaddressed hamate nonunion.

**Supporting evidence:** Hui-Chou et al. reviewed current treatment options for hypothenar hammer syndrome, noting clinical signs/symptoms include a palpable mass and distal digital embolization (to the long finger among others, per the visible text).
cards.correctAnswer: Migration and proliferation of vascular smooth muscle cells at the site of surgical repair (i
cards.wrongAnswer: Graft compression secondary to chronic unaddressed hook of hamate nonunion

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Radial Longitudinal Deficiency / Isolated vs. Syndromic Etiology
cards.front: Radial Clubhand — When Isolated (No Other Anomalies), It's Most Commonly a Spontaneous Mutation
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Madelung's deformity. Correct: **Radial clubhand** — when it occurs in isolation, it is most commonly the result of a **spontaneous mutation**, more so than the other listed congenital hand anomalies.

**Radial clubhand (radial deficiency/RLD):** rare, occurring in about **1:50,000-100,000** births, with a **male predilection**. Often diagnosed in utero, presenting shortly after birth or in early childhood, as here.

**The isolated-vs-syndromic distinction is the key concept:** in isolation (no other associated findings), RLD most commonly arises from **spontaneous mutation**. However, RLD has a **high syndromic association rate (30-44%)**, which is exactly why a thorough workup is still required even when the anomaly looks isolated on initial exam — you can't know it's truly isolated without completing the workup. Associated syndromes to screen for: **TAR (thrombocytopenia-absent radius), Fanconi anemia, Holt-Oram syndrome, and VACTERL/VATER** (ties together entries [1], [12], and [64]).

**Recommended workup for RLD (per Wall et al.):** thorough physical exam, **CBC** (Fanconi screening), **echocardiogram** (Holt-Oram screening), **abdominal ultrasound**, and possible referral to genetic counseling for syndromic evaluation.

**Treatment:** centers on improving hand function, dependent on the degree of radial hypoplasia — options include tendon transfers, pollicization, and/or hand centralization (see entry [12] for centralization timing specifics).

**Supporting evidence:**
- Dy et al. reviewed embryology, diagnosis, and evaluation of congenital hand anomalies (radial/ulnar longitudinal deficiency, central deficiency/cleft hand, thumb hypoplasia, syn-/polydactyly, amniotic constriction band), emphasizing their rarity, high rates of syndromic association, and the need for thorough history/exam with referral for further workup.
- Wall et al. reviewed current concepts in RLD management, detailing the CBC/echocardiogram/abdominal ultrasound/genetics-referral workup above and noting treatment depends on the degree of radial hypoplasia.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Thumb MCP Arthritis / Arthrodesis Position
cards.front: Thumb MCP Arthrodesis Position — 20 Degrees Flexion with Slight Pronation, Not Supination
cards.back:
Wrong answer: 20 degrees of flexion and slight **supination**. Correct: **20 degrees of flexion and slight pronation** — you had the flexion angle right, but the rotation direction backward.

**Why arthrodesis, not arthroplasty, for the thumb MCP specifically:** thumb MCP osteoarthritis is uncommon (1-5%), with a **male predilection for primary OA**; females more often develop it secondary to **inflammatory arthropathies** (as in this patient's RA). At the thumb MCP joint specifically, forces vary greatly across the joint, making arthroplasty **challenging** — and because the majority of thumb motion comes from the **CMC joint**, arthrodesis of the MCP is **easily tolerated** functionally. **This is the opposite of finger MCP joints**, where **arthroplasty is generally preferred** over arthrodesis (see entry [36] for RA finger MCP management).

**Ideal arthrodesis position for the thumb MCP:** **20-25 degrees of flexion with slight pronation** — this positions the thumb optimally for pinch. Fixation options include K-wires, plates, or intramedullary screws.

**Supporting evidence:** Dr. Rizzo reviewed MCP joint arthritis, covering current non-operative and operative treatments. Incidence has decreased with modern biologic medications for inflammatory arthropathies, but patients with primary or inflammatory arthritis still require surgery at times. Arthrodesis is superior for pain relief; pyrocarbon and silicone arthroplasty are preferred specifically for **index-through-small finger** MCP arthritis, while arthrodesis is preferred for the **thumb** MCP — for the index-through-small fingers, more constrained silicone implants are used (text continues past what's visible in the screenshot).
cards.wrongAnswer: 20 degrees of flexion and slight supination

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / ECU Instability / Provocative Physical Exam Finding
cards.front: ECU Subluxation — Provocative Exam Finding Is Pain with Flexion AND Supination (Extends Entries [9] and [61])
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Pain with movement of the wrist from ulnar to radial deviation with the wrist in slight extension. Correct: **Pain with flexion and supination of the wrist.**

**This completes the ECU subluxation picture across three entries:**
- Entry [9]: the **treatment/immobilization position** is pronation, extension, radial deviation (the stable position)
- Entry [61]: the **ECU synergy test** confirms **ECU tendonitis** (elbow flexed 90°, forearm supinated, resisted thumb/digit abduction)
- Entry [68] (this one): the **provocative exam finding for subluxation itself** is **pain with flexion and supination** — the tendon subluxates in this position because the ECU subsheath (6th dorsal compartment) normally restrains it, and with subsheath attenuation/rupture, there's no restraint during flexion/supination.

**Mechanism:** the ECU tendon runs within the 6th dorsal compartment and its subsheath. When the subsheath is attenuated or ruptured, nothing restrains tendon movement during **flexion and supination** — patients often describe a **snapping sensation** as the tendon subluxates over the dorsal wrist. The subluxation typically **reduces when the forearm returns to a pronated position** — directly explaining why the stable/treatment position in entry [9] is pronation, extension, and radial deviation.

**Natural history if untreated:** can progress to **ECU tendonitis**.

**Treatment:** first-line — immobilize the arm in **slight pronation** to allow subsheath healing. If conservative management fails — **ECU subsheath reconstruction** to address the instability directly.

**Supporting evidence:**
- MacLennan et al. reviewed diagnosis and anatomic reconstruction of ECU subluxation, describing a newer suture-anchor subsheath reconstruction technique in 21 patients with persistent subluxation — significant improvement in wrist ROM, grip strength, pain, satisfaction, and outcome scores.
- Garcia-Elias described ECU and 6th dorsal compartment anatomy (fibro-osseous tunnel plus subsheath). ECU tendinopathy can occur from overuse-related thickening, or the subsheath can rupture, allowing the ECU to sublux in a **volar** direction; both nonoperative and operative strategies exist for each problem.
cards.correctAnswer: Pain with flexion and supination of the wrist
cards.wrongAnswer: Pain with movement of the wrist from ulnar to radial deviation with the wrist in slight extension

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Metacarpal Fractures / Rotational Malunion
cards.front: Untreated Multiple Metacarpal Shaft Fractures — Rotational Malunion Causes Digit Scissoring/Overlap, Not Isolated Pinch Weakness
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Loss of key pinch strength. Correct: **Digits overlap when making a fist.**

**Why fracture location doesn't protect against rotational malunion:** you reasoned the metacarpals would maintain alignment based on where the fractures were — but rotational malunion isn't primarily about fracture location or angulation tolerance, it's about the fracture fragments healing with any degree of **rotational** malalignment along the metacarpal's long axis. Even a fracture pattern/location that looks stable in the coronal or sagittal plane (the angulation you'd assess on a single radiographic view) can still heal with axial rotation that isn't obvious until the patient makes a fist.

**Why rotation causes scissoring specifically:** each degree of rotational malunion at the metacarpal shaft is amplified at the fingertip due to the lever-arm effect of the digit's length — a small rotational malunion at the fracture site translates into significant fingertip malposition, causing the digit to **cross over or "scissor" against its neighbor** when the fingers are flexed into a fist. This is a distinct mechanical consequence from angulation malunion (which affects the joint's extensor lag, per entry [22]) or pure shortening (which affects grip strength via extensor tension, also entry [22]).

**Acceptable tolerances for closed treatment (stable, closed fractures without malrotation) — this is a separate, non-rotational concept:**
- Index and long finger: **10-15° shaft/neck angulation**
- Small finger: up to **40° shaft angulation**, **50-70° neck angulation**

**General indications for operative treatment:** open or unstable fractures, **malrotation**, intra-articular involvement, and multiple metacarpal fractures (all present or possible in this multi-metacarpal-fracture scenario). Fixation options: crossed K-wires, plate-screw constructs, headless intramedullary screws.

**Treatment for established rotational malunion:** corrective osteotomy.

**Supporting evidence:** Duerinckx and Caekebeke described a 2021 case series of minimally invasive, fluoroscopy-guided corrective osteotomy for metacarpal fractures with rotational malalignment, using a headless intramedullary compression screw. Benefits: minimal soft tissue injury/periosteal stripping, and the intramedullary screw allows early active range of motion.
cards.correctAnswer: Digits overlap when making a fist

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Ulnar Impaction Syndrome / USO vs. Wafer Procedure Outcomes
cards.front: Ulnar Shortening Osteotomy vs. Arthroscopic Wafer Procedure — USO Has More Reoperations, Wafer Has Earlier Return to Work
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Earlier return to work (this is actually an **advantage of the wafer procedure**, not something USO offers relative to wafer). Correct: **Increased rate of reoperation.**

**Why this trips people up:** the two procedures are equivalent on most outcome measures, and the two "different" outcomes are opposite in direction — mixing up which procedure has which advantage flips the answer entirely.

**Ulnocarpal abutment syndrome — two surgical options being compared:**
- **Arthroscopic wafer procedure**
- **Ulnar shortening osteotomy (USO)**

**What's equivalent between the two at 1 year:** clinical outcome scores, grip strength, range of motion, and pain scores (DASH scores as well).

**What's different — and in which direction:**
- **USO** is associated with **increased reoperation rates** (often for **symptomatic hardware requiring removal** — 37% of USO patients required reoperation for hardware irritation) and **longer return-to-work times**.
- **Arthroscopic wafer procedure** allows for an **earlier return to work** and **lower complication rates**.

**Ties to entry [27]:** this comparison assumes USO is otherwise indicated (i.e., no DRUJ arthrosis) — the reoperation/return-to-work tradeoff here is a separate consideration from the DRUJ-arthrosis contraindication covered in entry [27].

**Supporting evidence:** Afifi et al. ran a prospective randomized controlled trial comparing the arthroscopic wafer procedure to USO for ulnocarpal abutment syndrome. The wafer procedure allowed earlier return to work and had lower complication rates, with equivalent grip strength, ROM, and DASH scores; 37% of USO patients required reoperation for hardware irritation.

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Carpal Tunnel Syndrome / APB Anatomy and Motor Testing
cards.front: APB Insertion — Radial Side of the Base of the First Proximal Phalanx, Not Ulnar
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Ulnar side of the base of the first proximal phalanx. Correct: **Radial side of the base of the first proximal phalanx.**

**Abductor pollicis brevis (APB):** a thenar muscle innervated **solely by the median nerve distal to the carpal tunnel**, via the **recurrent motor branch**. Because it has no dual/ulnar innervation backup (unlike some other thenar muscles), APB strength is the standard motor test for carpal tunnel syndrome — weakness here is specific to median nerve compromise.

**CTS pathophysiology:** median nerve compression within the carpal tunnel (bounded by carpal bones and the transverse carpal ligament), which also houses **nine flexor tendons**. Sensory symptoms: numbness/tingling/pain in the **radial three digits and the radial half of the fourth digit**. Motor symptoms: weakness in **thumb abduction** from APB weakness. Chronic/advanced CTS: visible **thenar eminence atrophy**, partly from APB atrophy.

**Diagnostic exam nuance:** Neral et al. found that manual muscle testing (MMT) of thenar muscles is generally **unreliable** for CTS. Comparing median-innervated APB strength to ulnar-innervated intrinsic strength (first dorsal interosseous, FDI) did **not** improve MMT sensitivity/specificity. What did work: using a **dynamometer**, comparing APB strength to either **ABP (abductor pollicis brevis... note: likely typo in source for a comparator muscle) strength in the contralateral hand**, or FDI strength in the **ipsilateral** hand — this approach successfully detected CTS in confirmed-positive cases.
cards.correctAnswer: Radial side of the base of the first proximal phalanx
cards.wrongAnswer: Ulnar side of the base of the first proximal phalanx

cards.subtopic: Hand & Wrist / Mallet Finger / Bony Mallet with Subluxation
cards.front: Bony Mallet Finger — Operative Fixation Once Volar Subluxation Occurs, Regardless of the Usual Splinting-First Approach
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Extension splinting 24 hours/day for 12 weeks. Correct: **Closed reduction and percutaneous pinning.**

**Why splinting isn't enough here:** this patient's bony mallet involves **nearly 50% of the articular surface** *with* **volar subluxation of the distal phalanx** — this combination defines an **unstable** injury pattern. Extension splinting is the correct treatment for the vast majority of mallet fingers, including most bony mallets, but it is specifically **not sufficient once volar subluxation is present**, regardless of how long the splinting duration is extended.

**Mallet finger:** disruption of the terminal extensor tendon distal to the DIP joint, typically from a sudden forced-flexion impaction injury to an extended fingertip. Clinical exam: distal phalanx resting at **~45° of flexion**, no active DIP extension.

**Standard (non-subluxated) treatment:**
- Bony mallet: extension splinting of the DIP joint for **6-8 weeks**
- Purely tendinous mallet: extension splinting for **10-12 weeks**
- Splinting protocol generally: full-time DIP splinting (PIP left free) for 6 weeks around the clock, often followed by an additional 6 weeks of **nighttime-only** splinting

**Indications for surgery (this patient meets one of these):**
- **>50% intra-articular surface involvement leading to volar subluxation** of the distal phalanx (this case)
- Chronic injuries
- Significant concomitant arthritis

**Supporting evidence:**
- Avery et al. reviewed sports-related wrist and hand injuries: conservative extension splinting is appropriate for almost all mallet fingers, **including those with bony fragments, as long as there is no significant joint subluxation** — reinforcing that subluxation, not fragment size alone, is what flips the answer to surgery.
- Gruber et al. ran a prospective RCT comparing night splinting to no splinting after mallet finger treatment: 51 patients enrolled, 41 available at final follow-up (details continue past the visible screenshot text).

cards.front: TKA Revision — Causes by Timing
cards.back:
Infection is the most common cause for **early** TKA revision (<2 years). Aseptic loosening is the most common cause for **late** revision (>2 years). The common distractor is swapping infection to late — don't. PE wear was historically the #1 overall revision indication, but highly crosslinked UHMWPE has dropped it off the top spot. The percentage of revisions for instability and malalignment has also decreased over time (Sharkey et al. follow-up study).

Overall revision hierarchy (historical → current): PE wear → aseptic loosening. Current leading causes overall: aseptic loosening, infection, instability, periprosthetic fracture, arthrofibrosis.

cards.front: THA — Femoral Offset vs. Neck Length (Trendelenburg)
cards.back:
Trendelenburg sign = pelvis drops contralateral to stance leg → weak ipsilateral abductors. In THA, **femoral offset** (horizontal distance from femoral head center to femoral shaft axis) determines the abductor moment arm. **Decreasing offset shortens the abductors, reduces their mechanical advantage, and worsens Trendelenburg.** Femoral neck length affects leg length, not offset — these are distinct parameters. Don't conflate them.

To improve abductor function in THA: increase offset (extended offset stem) or lateralize/move cup superiorly. Biomechanics: Force Abductors × abductor moment arm = Body weight × BW moment arm. Offset drives the abductor side; cup position drives both sides.

cards.front: Metal-on-Metal THA — Small Head Diameter and Serum Metal Ions
cards.back: In MOM hip arthroplasty, smaller femoral head diameter = higher wear rate = elevated serum cobalt and chromium. Larger heads (≥36mm) generate better fluid-film (hydrodynamic) lubrication, which reduces ion release. Smaller heads have higher contact stress per unit area → more metal debris → higher risk of adverse local tissue reaction (ALTR/ALVAL). Small implant diameter is a recognized risk factor for elevated metal ions in MOM bearings.

cards.front: UKA Contraindications — Inflammatory Arthritis
cards.back:
Inflammatory arthropathy is an absolute contraindication to UKA regardless of how isolated the disease appears radiographically. It will progress in the native compartments and lead to failure requiring conversion to TKA. A 5-degree varus deformity is NOT a contraindication — varus <10 degrees is within UKA criteria.

Classic UKA inclusion criteria: age >60, low activity demand, weight <82 kg, minimal rest pain, ROM >90°, varus deformity <10°, flexion contracture <10°, no inflammatory arthritis. Most common cause of revision after Oxford medial UKA: lateral arthrosis (34%), then component loosening (31%) (Price et al., 682 knees, 20-year survival 91%).

cards.front: Periprosthetic Patella Fracture — Classification and Treatment
cards.back:
Ortiguerra and Berry classification: Type I = intact extensor mechanism + well-fixed implant → non-operative. Type II = extensor mechanism disruption (loose or stable implant) → revision surgery (poor results). Type III = intact extensor mechanism + loose implant → revision surgery.

Within Type I, fracture pattern determines treatment: **transverse → long leg cast; vertical → early active ROM** (vertical fractures are more stable). A fall onto a flexed knee produces a transverse pattern → cast, not immediate motion. Do not choose early active ROM for a Type I transverse fracture — that's only correct for vertical Type I.

Risk factors: excessive patella resection, cementless components, elevated BMI.

cards.front: CR-TKA vs. PS-TKA — Contraindications to CR Design
cards.back:
CR-TKA requires a functional PCL for femoral rollback during flexion. Inflammatory arthritis (RA, SLE, etc.) is a contraindication — chronic synovitis attenuates the PCL, making it unreliable. **Mild varus or valgus deformity is NOT a contraindication to CR-TKA.**

Contraindications to CR-TKA: inflammatory arthritis, prior patellectomy, prior PCL rupture, bicrucirate ligament ruptures, intraoperative PCL over-release. Use PS-TKA in any of these — the cam-post mechanism substitutes for PCL function.

cards.front: PJI Workup — Alpha-Defensin When Patient Is on Antibiotics
cards.back:
Antibiotics suppress synovial WBC count and culture yield, producing false-negative aspiration results. If a patient is on antibiotics at the time of aspiration, **repeat aspiration with alpha-defensin synovial fluid immunoassay** — alpha-defensin is not affected by concurrent antibiotic use. It is an antimicrobial peptide released by neutrophils in response to pathogens and is detected by immunoassay independent of antibiotic exposure.

MSIS PJI threshold for THA: synovial WBC >3,000 or PMNs >80%. A suppressed count from antibiotics will falsely fall below threshold. Open biopsy is premature before less invasive workup is exhausted. If alpha-defensin is unavailable: hold antibiotics 2 weeks, then re-aspirate. No single test has 100% accuracy — use a combination (Della Valle et al.).

cards.front: 2016 AAOS TKA Guideline — Evidence Strength Tiers
cards.back:
**Strong:** obese patients have less improvement after TKA; periarticular/peripheral nerve anesthesia reduces post-op pain and opioid use; no difference between CR and PS implants.

**Moderate:** diabetes increases complication risk; neuraxial anesthesia improves perioperative outcomes; no difference in pain/function with patellar resurfacing.

**Limited:** Hep C/cirrhosis increases complication risks.

Trap: obesity feels like a controversial/weak recommendation, but AAOS rates it strong. Hep C/cirrhosis sounds clinically obvious but the evidence only reaches limited strength. The question asks which does NOT have strong evidence — the answer is Hep C/cirrhosis (limited).

cards.front: Sciatic Nerve Palsy After THA — Risk Factors
cards.back:
Risk factors for post-op sciatic nerve palsy after THA: female gender, DDH, revision surgery, extremity lengthening, posterior approach, cementless femoral fixation. **RA, obesity, Perthes, and SCFE are NOT risk factors** (Schmalzried et al., CORR 1997; Farrell et al., 27,000 primary THAs, 0.17% incidence).

Female gender as a risk factor is counterintuitive but well-established — don't swap it out for RA.

Initial management: hip extension + knee flexion (releases tension on sciatic nerve). If hematoma suspected → OR evacuation. Common causes: hematoma, tight dressings, direct injury, excess heat from PMMA polymerization.

cards.front: PJI Scoring — Inconclusive Score → OR for Histologic Exam
cards.back:
Parvizi 2018 criteria (ICM/MSIS): score ≥6 = infected → two-stage revision. Score 2–5 = inconclusive → **proceed to OR for histologic examination, cultures, and assessment for purulence.** Score 0–1 = not infected.

This patient scored 4 (2 points for elevated serum CRP + 2 points for synovial PMNs >90%) = inconclusive. Next step is OR for histologic exam, not two-stage revision. Two-stage revision requires a confirmed diagnosis. Sensitivity 97.7%, specificity 99.5% (Parvizi et al., 2018).

cards.front: Hip Resurfacing Metallosis — Edge-Loading vs. Activity-Related Wear
cards.back:
In Birmingham Hip Resurfacing (BHR) with elevated metal ions (cobalt/chromium), the most common cause of metallosis is **edge-loading** — not simple activity-related wear. Edge-loading occurs when the cup is malpositioned (typically steep abduction angle), causing the femoral head to load the rim of the acetabular component rather than the bearing surface. A history of high-demand activity (e.g., running) precipitates edge-loading but doesn't cause wear on its own.
