Metallosis can cause pseudotumor formation and destruction of hip abductors. Workup: serum metal ions + MARS MRI. Cobalt cutoff >5.7 mcg/L and chromium >5.5 mcg/L indicate metallosis in bilateral BHRs (Matharu et al.). Brooks: 0.23% metallosis rate in BHR, all cases from edge-loading.

cards.front: Antibiotic Cement for Stage 2 Revision TKA — MRSA Coverage
cards.back:
For stage 2 revision TKA after MRSA PJI: use **pre-mixed gentamicin cement + hand-mixed vancomycin**. Pre-mixed antibiotic cement has superior mechanical and elution properties vs. hand-mixed (Jiranek et al.). Vancomycin must be hand-mixed in for MRSA coverage — there is no commercially available pre-mixed vancomycin cement.

Antibiotic cement is FDA-approved only for stage 2 of two-stage revision. For primary TKA, FDA recommendation is plain cement without antibiotics (antibiotic cement in primary TKA is technically off-label and has not been shown to reduce deep infection rates — Wang et al., 2,293 primary TKAs).

cards.front: Constrained Acetabular Component — Indications
cards.back:
Indications for constrained acetabular component: recurrent dislocations from unrepairable soft-tissue insufficiency (abductor insufficiency or capsular attenuation); severe cognitive/neuromuscular disease; late dislocations with well-positioned components.

**PE wear alone is NOT an indication** — address the wear with liner exchange or cup revision. A constrained liner does not fix the wear problem. Late dislocations without component loosening or malposition (well-positioned components, soft-tissue failure) IS an indication. Shrader et al. (JBJS 2003, Mayo registry): constrained cup eliminated or prevented instability in 107/109 patients.

cards.front: Iliopsoas Tenotomy After THA — Positioning to Protect Neurovascular Bundle
cards.back:
For extra-capsular iliopsoas tenotomy at the lesser trochanter: **external rotation + slight flexion** brings the tendon anterolaterally toward the surgeon and away from the femoral neurovascular bundle (nerve/artery/vein), which lies medially. Internal rotation moves the lesser trochanter toward the bundle — wrong direction.

The MFCA is the relevant structure for transcapsular release (posterior capsule, femoral head vascularity) — not for the extra-capsular approach at the lesser trochanter. Don't conflate the anatomic risks of the two techniques. Both trans- and extra-capsular techniques have equivalent clinical outcomes (Ilizaliturri et al.).

cards.front: PE Liner Exchange — Most Common Complication
cards.back: After isolated polyethylene liner exchange with well-fixed femoral and acetabular shell components, **dislocation is the most common complication.** Lachiewicz: 7/35 hips dislocated. Boucher: 25% dislocated. Beaule: 22% dislocated even when cementing a new liner into a fixed cementless shell. Liner-shell fixation failure, osteolysis, and PE fracture occur at much lower rates.

cards.front: Recurrent THA Dislocation with Abductor Insufficiency — Constrained Liner
cards.back:
Large femoral head is first-line for recurrent THA instability in high-risk patients (elderly, dementia, NMD, unable to comply with precautions). **Constrained liner is reserved for: failure of/contraindication to large head; elderly/low-demand patients; deficient or non-repairable abductor mechanism** (Sikes et al.).

Extended offset femoral component addresses abductor moment arm — appropriate when instability is from inadequate offset with a functional abductor mechanism. It does not fix an irreparable abductor. When a patient already has a large head AND has Trendelenburg gait (non-functional abductors) AND is elderly, constrained liner is the answer, not extended offset.

cards.front: Alpha-Defensin Immunoassay — What It Detects
cards.back:
Alpha-defensin immunoassay detects an **intra-articular antimicrobial peptide** in synovial fluid — not leukocytosis (that's synovial WBC/cell count), not a cytokine, not a serum marker. Alpha-defensin is abundant in neutrophils and macrophages; levels rise substantially in PJI and are detectable by immunoassay independent of antibiotic use.

Stats (Bonanzinga et al.): sensitivity 97%, specificity 97%, PPV 88%, NPV 99% (95% CI 96–99%). False-positive rate is higher in MOM arthroplasty due to adverse local tissue reaction.

cards.front: Ceramic Head Fracture Revision — Must Use Ceramic, Not Metal
cards.back:
After ceramic femoral head fracture, revision must use **another ceramic head — never metal.** Microscopic ceramic fragments always remain despite copious irrigation. Ceramic is harder than CoCr; retained fragments abrade a metal head → **massive third body wear.** This is mechanically distinct from metallosis (which is MOM articulation debris).

Causes of ceramic fracture: trauma, dislocation, head-neck taper mismatch, impacting head with excess force, intraoperative metal taper damage (Traina et al.).

cards.front: UHMWPE Manufacturing — Fatigue Crack Susceptibility
cards.back:
**Direct compression molding** (resin molded directly to finished part) = lowest susceptibility to fatigue crack formation and propagation. Ram bar extrusion and compression molding have equivalent wear rates, but compression molding is superior for fatigue crack resistance.

High-dose irradiation crosslinks PE and reduces wear, but crystallinity >70% increases PE failure rates — ideal is ~50%. 10 Mrad achieving >99% crystallinity exceeds safe thresholds. Calcium stearate caused fusion defects (unfused PE particles) → reduced mechanical properties → abandoned.

cards.front: THA Joint Reactive Force — Acetabular Cup Medialization
cards.back:
Equation: Abductor force (AB) × abductor moment arm (A) = Body weight (W) × body weight moment arm (B). Joint reactive force ≈ AB + W.

**Medialization of the acetabular cup** moves the center of rotation medially → shortens B (body weight moment arm) → abductors need less force to balance → joint reactive force decreases. Typically paired with increased femoral offset to restore the abductor moment arm (A), which otherwise also shortens with medialization.

**Larger femoral head-to-neck ratio** improves jump distance (stability) but does not affect the moment arm equation — it does not decrease joint reactive force.

Increasing femoral offset increases A → AB decreases → joint reactive force also decreases (alternative correct mechanism).

cards.front: Osteophyte Formation in OA — Indian Hedgehog (Ihh) Signaling
cards.back:
Osteophyte formation in OA = endochondral ossification mediated by **Indian hedgehog (Ihh)** signaling. In normal adult cartilage, Ihh is inactive. In OA, Ihh is upregulated in quiescent chondrocytes → activates the prenatal endochondral ossification pathway → osteophytes and subchondral cysts. Inhibiting Ihh reduces OA severity in mouse models (Wang et al.).

**Sclerostin** is a Wnt pathway inhibitor produced by osteocytes — it blocks LRP5/6, shutting off Wnt signaling and suppressing osteoblast activity. More sclerostin = less bone formation; less sclerostin = more bone formation. Sclerostin is not involved in osteophyte formation or endochondral ossification. Romosozumab (anti-sclerostin antibody) activates Wnt → used for osteoporosis. Van Buchem disease/sclerosteosis = loss-of-function sclerostin mutation → massive bone overgrowth.

RANKL/OPG = osteoclast regulation. PPARγ = adipocyte vs. osteoblast lineage commitment.

cards.front: Motor Nerve Palsy After THA — Obesity Is Not a Risk Factor
cards.back:
Risk factors for nerve palsy after primary THA (Farrell et al., 27,000+ patients): DDH, posttraumatic arthritis, posterior approach, cementless femoral fixation, limb lengthening. **Obesity/BMI is NOT a risk factor.** RA is also not a risk factor (Schmalzried et al.).

See entry [9] for the same study — different EXCEPT answer (RA). Average time to maximal recovery for complete and incomplete palsies: ~2 years.

cards.front: PE Wear Rate — Osteolysis Threshold
cards.back:
**Wear rate >0.1 mm/year = significant risk of osteolysis.** Below 0.1 mm/year, osteolysis is rarely observed (Dumbleton et al. meta-analysis). A liner measuring 0.05 mm/year is well below threshold → minimal osteolysis expected, not greater than conventional THA.

Sochart et al. (235 Charnley THAs, 20-year follow-up): hips not revised averaged 0.09 mm/year; hips revised for loosening averaged 0.19 mm/year.

cards.front: Offset Acetabular Liner — Early Component Loosening
cards.back:
Lateralized/offset acetabular liners translate the center of hip rotation laterally → increased joint reaction forces + PE wear + eccentric torsional loading at liner-shell and bone-implant interfaces → failure of ingrowth → early aseptic loosening.

Archibeck et al. (1919 primary THA): aseptic loosening 0.12% standard liner vs. **4.2% with 7mm offset liner** at ≥2 years. Glori: torsion from offset liner is comparable to in vitro forces causing interference fit failure — limit weight bearing initially after use. Offset liner adds 4–10mm offset on the acetabular side and restores soft tissue tension, but at significant mechanical cost to fixation.

cards.front: Hip Resurfacing Femoral Neck Fracture — Risk Factors
cards.back:
Femoral neck fracture = most common early complication of hip resurfacing. Risk factors: superior femoral neck notching, varus femoral component placement, incomplete femoral implant seating, poor bone quality/cysts (AVN, obesity).

Cup **retroversion** → femoral neck impingement → fracture risk. Cup **excessive inclination** (abduction angle) → edge-loading and instability, but NOT femoral neck fracture. Don't conflate inclination and retroversion — they are different cup orientation parameters with different failure modes.

Hip resurfacing relies on proximal femur bone-implant interface for stability — anything weakening this interface leads to neck fracture.

cards.front: AAOS Acetabular Bone Loss Classification
cards.back:
AAOS Classification: Type I = segmental; Type II = cavitary (contained) → bone graft ± jumbo cup; Type III = combined → jumbo cup + augmentation ± posterior column plate; **Type IV = pelvic discontinuity → anti-protrusio cage with screw fixation + posterior column plate** (or custom triflange, or trabecular metal cup); Type V = arthrodesis → conversion.

Trap: Type III and IV both involve cages and posterior column plates — the distinction is Type IV requires **screw fixation** to bridge the discontinuity. Pelvic discontinuity on X-ray: obturator ring disruption + separation between superior and inferior hemipelvis. Trabecular metal cup showed better outcomes than cage for discontinuity (Paprosky).

cards.front: PE Sterilization — Gamma Irradiation, Free Radicals, and Gas Environment
cards.back:
Gamma irradiation always produces **free radicals** regardless of gas environment. Free radicals → cross-linking → improved wear resistance.

Gas environment determines oxidation risk:
- **Air/oxygen:** free radicals + immediate oxidative degradation → premature PE failure (old technique)
- **Nitrogen (inert gas):** free radicals with minimal oxidation during processing, but free radicals persist in PE and cause delayed in vivo oxidation → still need thermal treatment
- **Remelting** (above melting point) or **annealing** (below melting point): both quench residual free radicals to prevent in vivo oxidative degradation

Gas plasma and ethylene oxide sterilize without radiation → no free radicals, no cross-linking, no oxidation, but also no wear resistance improvement (McKellop et al.).

cards.front: TKA Sex Differences — Females Have Greater Implant Survivorship
cards.back:
Females undergoing TKA with standard (non-gender-specific) components have **greater implant survivorship** and greater WOMAC improvement vs. males. Equal SF-12 improvement. Less improvement in Knee Society function/total scores (MacDonald et al., Level 2, 5279 primary TKAs, ≥2 years).

Gender-specific TKA components (narrower M/L, decreased anterior flange, increased trochlear groove angle) have no evidence of superior outcomes over standard components in females (Greene).

cards.front: Aseptic Loosening After Hemiarthroplasty — Thigh/Knee Pain + Pedestal Formation
cards.back:
**Thigh and knee pain** after hip arthroplasty = femoral stem loosening (subsiding stem tip irritates distal cortex). Groin pain → acetabular pathology (erosion, cup loosening). Don't confuse the two pain distributions.

Radiographic signs of aseptic loosening: progressive radiolucent lines, **pedestal formation at distal femoral stem**, change of implant position on sequential films, positive bone scan. Gruen zones 5/6 = distal medial/lateral stem.

Austin-Moore hemiarthroplasty is associated with acetabular erosion (metal head on native cartilage) — but acetabular erosion presents with groin pain. Normal CRP/ESR makes infection unlikely. Portillo et al.: aseptic loosening = most common reason for revision; revision <2 years = most likely infection.

cards.front: Paprosky Femoral Bone Loss Classification
cards.back:
| Type | Metaphysis | Diaphysis | Treatment |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| I | Minimal loss | Intact | Standard cementless stem |
| II | Extensive loss | Intact | Fully coated cementless stem |
| IIIA | Severe loss | **>4 cm preserved** | **Fully coated cementless stem** + cable + bone graft |
| IIIB | Severe loss | **<4 cm preserved** | **Modular tapered (fluted) stem** |
| IV | Extensive loss | Extensive loss | Total femur prosthesis or allograft-prosthetic composite |

Pivot: **4 cm of diaphyseal bone** separates IIIA (enough for fully coated) from IIIB (need tapered fluted modular). Modular tapered stem for IIIB, not IIIA.

cards.front: Periprosthetic Distal Femur Fracture — Bridge Plating vs. RIMN
cards.back:
Bridge plating vs. retrograde IM nailing (RIMN) for periprosthetic distal femur fracture above stable TKA: **similar nonunion rates, similar secondary procedure rates. RIMN has slightly higher malunion rate** → bridge plating has lower malunion rate by comparison (Ristevski et al., systematic review, 719 fractures).

Both are acceptable constructs. Most cases have a stable TKA implant → internal fixation preferred over non-operative.

cards.front: Inferior Gluteal Artery — Location During Surgical Hip Dislocation
cards.back:
The inferior gluteal artery (IGA) exits the pelvis through the greater sciatic notch between the piriformis and coccygeus → reliably found **along the border of the piriformis tendon**. It is NOT under the gluteus medius (that is the superior gluteal artery).

IGA = primary femoral head blood supply in 17% of hips. MFCA = primary in the majority; runs constant extracapsular course, perforates capsule at superior gemellus, runs along posterosuperior femoral neck. Protected by obturator externus during surgical hip dislocation (Gautier et al.). Foveal artery has no significant vascular contribution (Kalhor et al.).

cards.front: Peroneal Nerve Palsy After THA — Recovery Rate
cards.back:
**35–40% of patients with complete peroneal nerve palsy after THA recover full strength → 60–65% never recover full strength.** Don't confuse recovery rate with non-recovery rate.

Farrell: 10/28 (36%) with complete palsy achieved full recovery, average 21.1 months. Schmalzried: ~41% complete recovery, 44% mild residual deficit. Good prognosis if: motor function present immediately postop OR some recovery within 2 weeks. Nerve palsy prevalence ~1%; sciatic nerve (peroneal division) involved in 80%.

cards.front: Iliopsoas Impingement After THA — Treatment Algorithm
cards.back:
Confirm diagnosis with iliopsoas sheath injection (temporary relief = positive). Then:
- **Radiographs within normal limits** → iliopsoas tendon release (tenotomy/resection)
- **Anterior acetabular overhang on imaging** → acetabular revision to reduce anterior impingement

The X-ray is the decision branch. If you can't tell from imaging whether overhang is present, CT or cross-table lateral radiograph is the next step (Lachiewicz et al.).

cards.front: Intraoperative MCL Transection During TKA
cards.back:
Two acceptable salvage strategies:
1. **Direct repair + CR or PS implant + hinged knee brace × 6 weeks** (Leopold et al.: all stable, no revision at 45 months)
2. **Unlinked constrained implant** ± repair (Lee and Lotke: preferred for midsubstance injuries — higher failure with repair + CR)

Missing the 6-week hinged brace is the trap. Repair alone without bracing is insufficient. Tibial avulsions → suture anchor repair; midsubstance tears → favor constrained implant over repair.

MCL injury mechanism during TKA: medially placed retractors, medial subperiosteal elevation, oscillating saw during tibial or posterior femoral condyle cuts.

cards.front: PE Wear Failure Risk Factors — Gamma Irradiated in Air
cards.back:
Risk factors for wear-related failure with gamma-irradiated-in-air PE (Fehring et al., 2091 TKAs): increasing shelf age, younger patient age, **male gender**, polyethylene sheet vendor, polyethylene finishing method. **CR vs. PS knee design is NOT a risk factor.**

Rough tibial baseplate also a risk factor: Collier et al. — osteolysis in 34% with gamma-in-air + rough baseplate vs. 9% with inert gas irradiation or no irradiation + polished surface. Male gender is counterintuitive but confirmed — higher activity demands drive wear.

cards.front: THA Instability — Cup Medialization Decreases Stability
cards.back:
Medialization of the acetabular cup shortens the abductor moment arm → reduced soft tissue tension → greater laxity → **decreased stability.** It is the one intervention that does NOT increase stability. (See entry [20] — medialization reduces joint reactive force, but the tradeoff is reduced abductor tension.)

Interventions that DO increase stability: extended offset femoral component (lengthens abductor lever arm), advancing trochanter distally (same mechanism), constrained liner (increases functional cup depth and jumping distance), larger femoral head (increases head-to-neck ratio and range before impingement).

cards.front: Periprosthetic Patella Fracture — Extensor Deficiency + Adequate Bone Stock → ORIF
cards.back:
Periprosthetic patella fracture decision tree:
- Intact extensor mechanism + well-fixed implant → non-operative (see entry [5])
- **Deficient extensor mechanism + adequate bone stock → ORIF** (suture or implant fixation)
- Deficient extensor mechanism + inadequate bone stock → extensor mechanism allograft
- Loose patellar component → revision or removal

Extensor mechanism allograft is NOT the answer when bone stock is adequate. Inability to SLR = deficient extensor mechanism.

cards.front: Radiostereometric Analysis (RSA) — Most Accurate PE Wear Measurement
cards.back:
RSA is the most accurate and precise method to evaluate polyethylene wear in arthroplasty. Tantalum beads are inserted into bone at index surgery; an immediate postop radiograph establishes the bead baseline; repeated films over time track component position relative to beads. **RSA can only be used prospectively** — beads must be placed at surgery. Also expensive and requires technical expertise (McCalden et al.).

Computer-assisted edge-detection radiographs are less accurate. Retrospective RSA is not possible.

cards.subtopic: Adult Reconstruction — THA Revision / Acetabular Bone Loss
cards.front: Massive Non-Contained Acetabular Defect — Ilioischial Ring + Structural Graft
cards.back:
Non-contained defects involving both anterior and posterior columns at >50% of the weight-bearing surface require an **ilioischial reconstruction ring + structural corticocancellous graft**. The ring spans intact ilium to intact ischium, bypassing the deficient columns entirely; the structural graft packs behind the ring and incorporates over time, actually restoring bone stock.

A bilobed cementless component fills two cavities with metal — it replaces bone rather than restoring it, and cannot achieve stable fixation when both columns are compromised and >50% of the weight-bearing surface is involved. Morselized allograft + cementless cup works for minor contained defects. The question asks which procedure "restores the most bone stock" — that answer is always structural graft, not a metal implant. Goodman et al. (61 ilioischial rings, 15-year follow-up): allograft failure was the most common complication, but the construct remains standard for this level of loss.

cards.front: Early THA (<50 years) — DDH Is Most Common Etiology Excluding AVN
cards.back:
Excluding osteonecrosis, **DDH (developmental dysplasia of the hip) is the most common cause of THA before age 50** (Figure D — shallow acetabulum, lateral femoral head uncoverage, dysplastic morphology). FAI is increasingly recognized as a contributor to early OA but remains secondary to DDH in cross-sectional and retrospective series.

Clohisy et al. (three-center retrospective, primary THA <50 years): OA was the most common cause of early native hip failure (47.5%); of those OA cases, 48.4% were attributable to DDH. FAI findings were common but considered a potentially underrecognized, not the leading, cause. When treating a patient for FAI, screen for underlying DDH — unrecognized DDH leads to poor outcomes after hip arthroscopy osteoplasty.

Radiographic DDH: CE angle <20°, acetabular index >10°, lateralized femoral head, deficient anterior and lateral coverage.

cards.subtopic: Adult Reconstruction — THA / PE Wear & Osteolysis
cards.front: Distal Femoral Osteolysis in THA — Proximal Circumferential Coating Creates the Seal
cards.back:
Osteolysis occurs when PE particles enter the "effective joint space" (any area where joint fluid contacts bone) and activate macrophages. The distal pathway runs from the articulation down the femoral shaft around the stem. **Diaphyseal filling does not block this pathway** — particles still enter the interface proximally and track distally. What prevents distal osteolysis is sealing the pathway at the metaphysis.

A **proximal circumferentially coated ingrowth stem** creates a biological seal once bone ingrows around the full proximal circumference → particles cannot bypass the seal → zero distal osteolysis. Sinha et al. (101 hips): 0% distal osteolysis with this design. Tradeoff: 82% showed evidence of proximal stress shielding (load transferred at ingrowth zone), and 38% showed proximal osteolysis from stress shielding — but none distal.

Collared stems address calcar support and bone quality; they do not seal the effective joint space.

cards.front: Vancouver AG Periprosthetic Fracture — Displacement Determines Treatment
cards.back:
Vancouver AG (greater trochanter) treatment is NOT uniformly non-operative:
- **<2cm displaced** → non-operative, protected weight bearing
- **>2cm displaced** → ORIF with trochanteric claw plate, cables, or locking plate

AL (lesser trochanter) = almost always non-operative — non-weight bearing, iliopsoas pull doesn't cause structural failure.

"Vancouver A = non-op" is only true for AL and minimally displaced AG. Displaced AG (>2cm, as in this question's figure) requires fixation. Trochanteric claw plate preferred: wraps around trochanter, superior axial pull-out resistance vs. cables alone (Sariyilmaz et al.: grip plate fixation more stable than cable fixation in biomechanical testing). Femoral component revision is NOT indicated when the stem is well-fixed.

cards.front: MOM THA Pseudotumor — Associated with Increased Linear Wear Rate, Not Co:Cr Ratio
cards.back:
Pseudotumor presence correlates with **increased implant linear wear rate** — not a specific cobalt:chromium ratio. Kwon et al.: pseudotumor group had 8.1 vs. 1.79 microns/year (femoral) and 7.36 vs. 1.28 microns/year (acetabular) compared to non-pseudotumor revisions. More wear → more debris → local tissue reaction → pseudotumor.

Key facts: pseudotumors in **10–15%** of MOM THAs (not majority). Histology = **lymphocyte-predominant** (macrophages also present, but lymphocytes predominate — this is ALVAL pattern). Treatment = revision to ceramic-on-polyethylene. Observation is not appropriate when pseudotumor is identified on MARS MRI.

cards.subtopic: Adult Reconstruction — THA Instability / Constrained Liner
cards.front: Broken Constraining Ring on Constrained Liner → Dislocation Risk
cards.back:
On AP radiograph, the metal constraining ring of a constrained acetabular liner appears as a dense ring at the cup rim. A **broken or discontinuous ring = liner dissociation** → dramatically increased dislocation risk. Daniel didn't recognize the broken ring sign, chose aseptic loosening instead.

Dislocation rate after primary THA: 0.5–10%; after revision THA: up to 28%. Shapiro et al. (85 constrained THAs for chronic instability): 2.4% dislocation rate — both cases caused by constrained liner dissociation, not component loosening.

Treatment options for THA instability: component reorientation, trochanteric reattachment/advancement, capsulorrhaphy, elevated liner, bipolar conversion, femoral neck lengthening, resection arthroplasty, or constrained cup. When the constraining ring breaks → instability source is the liner itself → revision of the liner is required.

**Note:** "broken ring" also appears in pelvic discontinuity (disrupted obturator ring on AP pelvis = separation of superior/inferior hemipelvis). Two different broken rings, two different diagnoses — context determines which one.

cards.front: Proximal Femoral Replacement for Pathologic Fracture — Infection Is Most Common Complication
cards.back:
Jacofsky et al. (42 patients, hip arthroplasty for salvage of failed pathologic proximal femur fracture fixation): deep prosthetic infection = most common complication (~10%). **All infections occurred in previously irradiated patients** — prior radiation is a major infection risk factor, not protective. HHS improved 42 → 83; 41/42 patients ambulatory at follow-up; implant survivorship free of revision at 5 years = 90%; free of aseptic failure = **97%** (aseptic failure rate is ~3%, NOT >50%).

Traps: (1) aseptic failure sounds likely in oncologic patients but is actually rare — infection drives failure, not loosening; (2) pre-op radiation increases infection risk, doesn't decrease it.

cards.front: Return to Driving After THA — 4–6 Weeks
cards.back: Driving reaction time returns to preoperative levels in **4–6 weeks** after primary THA (answer: 4 weeks). Ganz et al. (55 patients, 34–85 years, simulation driving assessment): reaction time worsens at 1 week postop, then recovers — most patients back to baseline by 4–6 weeks. Three patients converted from one-footed to two-footed drivers postoperatively. Factors affecting return: postoperative pain, narcotic weaning, extent of limb lengthening, rehab progression.

cards.front: Osteopenia and Bone-Cement Interface — More Porosity = Stronger Interface
cards.back:
Osteopenic bone is more porous → cement penetrates deeper into intertrabecular spaces → better mechanical interlock → **improved** bone-cement interface strength (higher fracture resistance). Graham et al. (JBJS 2003, bovine femora): fracture toughness at bone-cement interface correlated with increased bone porosity and maximum cement penetration depth. Cement pressurization increases penetration and further improves fracture resistance.

Clinical implication: young patients with dense cortical bone have WORSE cemented THA outcomes because cement cannot interdigitate well. "A lack of porosity is associated with reduced mechanical integrity of the cemented interface and may contribute to the relatively poorer results of cement fixation in young male patients." Elderly osteopenic patients are better cemented candidates.

cards.subtopic: Adult Reconstruction — THA Revision / Femoral Component
cards.front: Loose Cemented Femoral Stem with Osteolysis — Revision to Long-Stem Cementless, Not Cemented
cards.back:
Circumferential radiolucency + cement mantle fracture = osteolysis and loosening → revision indicated urgently (cortical thinning → impending periprosthetic fracture). Choice is **cementless long-stem**, NOT cemented.

Cement in revision requires intact cancellous bone to interdigitate — proximal femoral osteolysis destroys that substrate. A long-stem cementless prosthesis bypasses the osteolytic proximal femur and achieves diaphyseal fixation in unaffected distal bone (Paprosky/Della Valle principle). Second reason: failed cemented revision → cement removal from compromised femur is destructive; cementless stems preserve remaining bone stock for future surgery.

Contrast with entry [57]: osteopenic bone improves cement interdigitation in **primary THA** where bone is still structurally intact. In revision with existing osteolysis, the bone is qualitatively destroyed, not just porous. Bisphosphonates unproven for osteolysis and useless once stem is already loose. Cane is inadequate — walker until revision.

cards.front: PE Wear Debris Biologic Response — Proportional to Number, Not Size
cards.back:
Severity of biologic response to particulate PE debris is **directly proportional to number (quantity)** — dose-dependent. More particles → more macrophage activation → more TNF-alpha secretion → more osteolysis.

Size determines per-particle potency, not total severity: particles sized **0.2–7-8 μm are most stimulatory** (within macrophage phagocytic range). Outside this window, response drops off — no direct proportional correlation with size across the full range. No correlation with composition or molecular weight either (Campbell et al., Clin Orthop Relat Res 2004).

Trap: knowing 0.2–7-8 μm is the active range makes size feel correct. But the question asks what scales *directly proportionally* with severity — that's number, not size.

cards.front: Charnley All-PE Acetabular Shell — 35-Year Failure Rate 20–30%
cards.back: Failure rate (revision for any reason) at 35 years for Charnley all-polyethylene acetabular shell = **20–30%**, not >75%. The Charnley THA is remarkably durable. Callaghan et al.: 88% intact at 20 years, **78% intact at 35 years** (~22% failure rate). Della Valle et al.: 77.3% survivorship at mean 21 years. Kavanaugh et al.: 84% surviving 20 years without revision; revision probability increases with younger age at index surgery (27% if <59 yo, 13% if 59–65, 7.5% if 65–70, 12% if >70 yo).

cards.subtopic: Adult Reconstruction — THA Instability / Spinopelvic Parameters
cards.front: Spinopelvic Stiffness → Hip Hypermobility → THA Dislocation Risk
cards.back:
**Sacral slope (SS) change <10° from standing to sitting = stiff spinopelvic junction** → hypermobile hip → increased THA dislocation risk. Normal SS change = 11–29°; normal PFA change = 51–69°.

Mechanism: normally, the pelvis tilts posteriorly 15–20° when sitting, sharing the motion burden with the hip. In a stiff spine, the pelvis doesn't tilt → femur must compensate with greater excursion to achieve the same hip flexion → hypermobility → impingement/dislocation. Heckmann et al.: for every 1° decrease in spinopelvic motion, 0.9° increase in femoral motion → impingement and dislocation risk.

Stiff pelvis can be "stuck standing" (posteriorly tilted → impinges anteriorly → posterior dislocation risk) or "stuck sitting" (anteriorly tilted → impinges posteriorly → anterior dislocation risk).

Trap: PFA change of 60° is normal (within 51–69°), not stiff. A change <51° would indicate stiffness.

cards.front: TKA Constraint Hierarchy — Non-Linked Constrained vs. Rotating Hinge
cards.back:
**Non-linked constrained TKA** (tall tibial spine in deep femoral box): constrains **partial varus-valgus** (allows 2–3°) + **partial internal-external rotation** (allows 2°). AP translation is NOT constrained — no linkage between components.

**Rotating hinge (linked)**: constrains varus-valgus + IE rotation + **AP translation** (the hinge physically connects the components).

Constraint hierarchy (least → most): PS/cruciate-substituting → constrained condylar (non-linked) → rotating hinge (linked).

Indications for constrained condylar: MCL or LCL functional loss, inability to balance flexion/extension gaps, severe valgus deformity. Scuderi: use least constraint achieving stability — more constraint = greater bone-implant interface stress = loosening risk. Rodriguez et al. (44 patients, constrained condylar, 5.5-year follow-up): only 1 femoral loosening, 0 tibial loosening.

cards.front: PE Free Radical Reduction — Remelting > Annealing; Vitamin E Works, Vitamin C Does Not
cards.back:
Largest reduction of free radicals: **thermal stabilization through remelting** (heat above melting point → quenches most free radicals). Annealing (below melting point) also reduces free radicals but cannot quench as completely as remelting — residual free radicals persist.

**Vitamin E** quenches free radicals in PE and protects against oxidative degradation. **Vitamin C has not shown the same benefit** — both are biological antioxidants but only vitamin E has evidence in PE. Gamma irradiation in nitrogen generates the same number of free radicals as gamma in air — the gas environment affects downstream oxidation, not initial radical formation (see entry [27]).

Ranking: remelting > annealing > vitamin E. Gas environment determines oxidation risk, not radical count.

cards.front: UKA Tibial Stress Fracture — Excessive Impaction Force Is NOT a Delayed Cause
cards.back:
EXCEPT question: periprosthetic tibial stress fracture at 3 months after medial UKA. Excessive impaction force is the EXCEPT — it causes immediate intraoperative fracture, recognized acutely, not a delayed stress fracture at 3 months.

Risk factors for delayed tibial stress fracture after UKA: posterior tibial cortex penetration with guide pin, peripheral medial cortical guide pin placement, tibial resection guide replacement with re-drilling of guide holes (stress risers from additional holes), and **tibial component undersizing** (increases mechanical stress on remaining proximal tibial bone). Brumby et al.: stress fractures at median 8 weeks post-UKA, attributed to mechanical weakening from guide pin and lug holes.

cards.front: UHMWPE Wear Resistance vs. Free Radical Reduction — Different Answers
cards.back:
**Most positive effect on wear resistance → Radiation** (cross-linking is the mechanism; more radiation dose = more cross-links = less wear). Modern UHMWPE: 5–10 Mrad in inert environment directly increases cross-link density → improves wear resistance.

**Largest reduction of free radicals → Remelting** (see entry [63]). These are different questions with different answers — do not conflate them.

Remelting tradeoff: heats above melt point → converts crystalline to amorphous → removes free radicals BUT reduces crystallinity → potential decrease in fatigue strength/mechanical properties. Annealing: below melt point → preserves crystallinity, leaves more free radicals. Neither remelting nor annealing creates cross-links — only radiation does. Ethylene oxide and gas plasma sterilize without radiation → no cross-linking, no wear resistance improvement.

cards.front: UKA Failure by Timing — Early = Aseptic Loosening, Late = OA Progression
cards.back:
**Early UKA failure (<5 years):** aseptic loosening (most common). Risk factors: all-polyethylene tibial tray, fixed-bearing design, component malalignment, poor initial fixation → progressive subsidence → TKA conversion with augments.

**Late UKA failure (>5 years):** OA progression (lateral arthrosis, most common overall per Price et al. — 34%, vs. 31% loosening — but this blends early and late data).

Parallel: TKA early (<2 years) = infection; late (>2 years) = aseptic loosening. UKA early (<5 years) = aseptic loosening; late = OA progression. Same pattern: mechanical failure early, disease progression late. See entry [4] for UKA contraindications.

cards.subtopic: Adult Reconstruction — HTO Biomechanics / Knee Kinematics
cards.front: Tibial Posterior Slope — Effect on ACL vs. PCL Stability
cards.back:
Posterior tibial slope creates an anterior-directed vector on the tibia under axial load.

**PCL-deficient knee:** tibia sags posteriorly → **increased slope** counteracts posterior sag → more stability.
**ACL-deficient knee:** tibia slides anteriorly → **decreased slope** reduces anterior translation → more stability.

Rule: slope and PCL deficiency go together (increase both); slope and ACL deficiency go opposite (decrease slope to compensate for ACL loss). Savarese et al.: decreased posterior tibial slope causes posterior tibial translation (destabilizes PCL-deficient knee); increased slope causes anterior tibial translation (destabilizes ACL-deficient knee). Relevant when planning HTO — procedure changes coronal AND sagittal plane, so ligament status must be considered preoperatively.

cards.front: Cemented Femoral Stem Failure — PMMA Precoating Adds a Failure Interface
cards.back:
**Increases failure: PMMA precoating.** Precoating the stem adds a second interface (precoat-to-new-cement bond) at risk of debonding. One interface (stem-to-cement) is inherently stronger than two.

**Does NOT increase failure:**
- Higher Young's modulus (>115 GPa, e.g., stainless steel, CoCr): stiffer cemented stems IMPROVE performance. Do not apply cementless stress-shielding logic here — in cemented fixation, stem stiffness aids load transfer through the mantle.
- Calcar collar contact: adds minimal strength, does not cause premature failure.
- Smoother corners: DECREASE failure by eliminating stress risers.
- 2mm cement mantle: ideal thickness. Less than 2mm decreases construct strength; more is acceptable.

Titanium Young's modulus = 115 GPa. CoCr and stainless steel are stiffer (higher modulus) and are the traditional cemented stem materials.

cards.subtopic: Adult Reconstruction — Revision TKA Gap Balancing
cards.front: Revision TKA — Elevated Joint Line with Equal Enlarged Gaps → Distal + Posterior Augments
cards.back:
Scenario: revision TKA, equal flexion and extension gaps (stable, full extension), joint line elevated (patellar impingement). Error: femoral component resting too proximal (on bone left behind after component removal) → component too small + too high → equal but enlarged gaps + elevated joint line.

**Fix: increase femoral component size + posterior augments** (decreases flexion gap) **+ distal augments** (lowers joint line, decreases extension gap). Must address BOTH gaps simultaneously.

Isolated distal augments alone (the wrong answer): tightens extension gap only → flexion gap remains large → flexion instability. Isolated posterior augments alone: tightens flexion gap only → extension gap remains large → extension instability. Only by correcting both simultaneously is the joint line restored with balanced gaps.

cards.subtopic: Adult Reconstruction — THA Revision / Acetabular Bone Loss
cards.front: Paprosky Acetabular Classification — IIB vs. I
cards.back:
| Type | Rim | Migration | Ischial Lysis | Teardrop | Treatment |
|------|-----|-----------|---------------|----------|-----------|
| I | Intact | <3mm | None | Intact | Cementless cup ± screws |
| IIA | Superior loss, >50% intact | <3cm superior | None | Intact | Cementless cup + screws |
| IIB | Superior rim loss, >50% intact | Superolateral, <3cm | None | Intact | Cementless cup + screws |
| IIC | Medial wall defect | None | None | Medial | Cementless cup + screws ± augment |
| IIIA | Severe loss | >3cm superolateral | Mild | Intact | Modular porous augments + cup or cage |
| IIIB | Severe loss | >3cm superolateral | Severe | Absent | Cage + structural allograft or triflange |

**IIB:** superior acetabular rim loss + superolateral migration <3cm. Type I has neither — intact rim, minimal migration. Treatment is the same (cementless cup + screws) for Types I and II; pivot to cage/triflange at Type IIIA/B. This was a classification question, not a treatment question.

cards.front: Osteolysis — Implicated Particle Size Is Submicron (<1 Micron)
cards.back:
Particles implicated in osteolysis are **submicron (<1 micron)**. At this size, particles are phagocytosed and retained within macrophages → macrophage cannot degrade them → persistent activation → osteolytic cascade. Campbell et al.: UHMWPE particles isolated from tissues around failed THRs were submicron, rounded or elongated.

Connects to entry [59]: the 0.2–7.8 μm range is most stimulatory for biologic response overall; the submicron end (<1 μm) is specifically retained intracellularly and drives osteolysis. Particles >10 μm are too large for macrophage phagocytosis — they elicit a giant cell response instead, not the histiocytic macrophage response responsible for osteolysis.

McKellop 4 wear modes: Mode 1 = bearing on bearing (intended); Mode 2 = bearing on non-bearing; Mode 3 = third-body abrasive particles entrapped between bearing surfaces; Mode 4 = non-bearing on non-bearing.

---

# Joints Test 2

cards.front: Pseudotumor After MOM Hip Resurfacing — Risk Factors
cards.back:
Risk factors for pseudotumor/ALTR after MOM resurfacing (Glyn-Jones et al., BJJ 2009): female gender, **age <40 in women**, small components, hip dysplasia as underlying diagnosis. Large-diameter components are **protective**, not a risk factor — failure is least likely in men and with larger components. Small components → malpositioning + edge-loading → elevated metal ions → pseudotumor.

Best MOM resurfacing candidates: men younger than 50. Caution against use in women. See entry [3] (small MOM head diameter = elevated serum metal ions).

cards.front: Hip Arthrodesis — Optimal Position
cards.back:
Optimal hip arthrodesis position: **5° adduction, 5–10° external rotation, 20–35° flexion.** Adduction (NOT abduction) — abduction creates functional LLD and abnormal gait. Too little flexion impairs sitting.

Contraindications: ipsilateral knee, contralateral hip, or lumbar spine pathology. Long-term: these problems develop ~20 years post-arthrodesis (Callaghan et al.); 15% conversion to THA by 20 years.

cards.front: PJI Serum Markers — IL-6 Has Highest Correlation
cards.back: Serum marker hierarchy for PJI correlation: **IL-6 > CRP > ESR >> serum WBC (ineffective).** CRP and ESR are elevated in PJI and are standard screening tools, but IL-6 has the highest correlation. Serum WBC is not useful for PJI diagnosis (Barbari et al., systematic review + meta-analysis, JBJS 2010).

cards.front: Paprosky Type IV Femoral Defect — APC or PFR
cards.back:
Paprosky Type IV = complete loss of femoral isthmus + extensive metaphyseal and diaphyseal bone loss → biologic fixation unlikely. Options: allograft-prosthesis composite (APC), long cemented stem, impaction grafting + long cemented stem, proximal femoral replacement (PFR). Not a modular tapered fluted stem (that is IIIB).

Viste et al. (44 patients, Paprosky IIIB or IV, PFR): HHS 42.8 → 68.5; 86% survivorship at 5 years, 66% at 10 years; 27% complications including 6 dislocations.

cards.front: Late TKA Loosening — PE Wear → Osteolysis → Loosening
cards.back:
Sequence: PE wear → debris → focal osteolysis → component loosening → femoral migration and varus shift. Presentation at 12 years with normal ESR/CRP and radiographic osteolysis = PE wear-driven failure.

Extensor mechanism dysfunction: requires clinical evidence of instability or poor extensor function. Full extension rules this out. Infection: excluded by normal inflammatory markers. Technical error: excluded by 12 years of good function with good initial alignment. Timeline rule: loosening >2 years = PE wear/aseptic; loosening <2 years = infection until proven otherwise.

cards.front: 2013 AAOS Hip OA Guidelines — Strongly Recommended Treatments
cards.back:
Strongly recommended: weight loss, low-impact physical activity, non-narcotic analgesia (NSAIDs), **intraarticular corticosteroids** (short-term pain relief). Corticosteroid mechanism: binds nuclear steroid receptors → interrupts inflammatory/immune cascade via mRNA changes.

NOT supported: prescription opioids, acupuncture, needle lavage, hyaluronic acid, glucosamine/chondroitin, arthroscopic lavage.

Sodium channel blockade = local anesthetics (not the answer). Hypothalamic action = acetaminophen/antipyretics.

cards.front: Retrograde Nailing Through TKA — Extension Deformity and PS Box Consideration
cards.back:
CR TKA femoral notch recess → forces more **posterior start point** for retrograde nail → **extension deformity** (usually minimal and inconsequential if done properly). Bone stock is not the concern.

All modern CR implants accommodate a retrograde nail. PS implants have a cam post box: most have an 8–12mm opening (nail can pass), but some have a **closed box** → retrograde nail impossible. Must verify PS box status before planning retrograde nailing. Retrograde nailing = less soft tissue damage than open lateral plating.

cards.front: Mobile-Bearing TKA — PE Wear and Bone-Implant Stress
cards.back:
Mobile-bearing TKA allows dual-surface articulation (poly moves on both the femoral component and the tibial tray). This increases sagittal femorotibial conformity → reduces PE shear stress (pressure = force/area) → theoretically less wear and less bone-implant-interface stress.

Despite sound biomechanical rationale, **no documented survivorship advantage** over well-positioned fixed-bearing TKA in short- or intermediate-term studies (Callaghan et al. ICL review). Still in use, particularly in younger/higher-demand patients, but not a proven clinical upgrade over fixed-bearing.

cards.front: Rectus Femoris Origins — Direct Head from AIIS, Reflected Head from Acetabular Rim
cards.back:
The rectus femoris has two heads: **direct (straight) head originates from the AIIS**; reflected head originates from the groove just superior to the acetabular rim. In THA, a hypertrophic AIIS causes anterior impingement with hip flexion — the impinging structure (AIIS) is where the direct head originates. AIIS resection resolves impingement (Pan et al.; Davidovitch et al.: two cases of intraoperative instability resolved after AIIS resection).

The reflected head is adjacent (acetabular groove) but does not originate from the AIIS — the names are counterintuitive because "direct" sounds like it should go straight to a bone prominence, but it's the straight-anterior origin point from the AIIS. Sartorius originates from the ASIS, not AIIS.

cards.front: Bilateral Symmetric Perthes-Like Hip Disease → COL2A1, Not Perthes
cards.back:
Wrong answer: activity modification + serial imaging for Legg-Calvé-Perthes. Correct: **skeletal survey + COL2A1 genetic sequencing**.

**Why this isn't Perthes:**
- Classic Perthes: **unilateral**, ages 4–8, progressive osteonecrosis of one femoral head
- Bilateral involvement occurs in only 10–15% of Perthes cases and is typically **asynchronous**
- **Synchronous, symmetrical bilateral disease** is uncommon for idiopathic Perthes → think inherited skeletal dysplasia

**Why COL2A1:**
- Type II collagen (COL2A1) disorders present with early bilateral symmetrical hip involvement mimicking Perthes — same delayed ossification, irregularity, femoral head deformity
- Associated extrasketal features: **high myopia**, retinal detachment, hearing loss, orofacial abnormalities
- Can present with minimal or absent extrasketal features early — the family history is the tell
- Father: early-onset OA (type II collagenopathy → premature cartilage degeneration)
- Paternal aunt: severe myopia ← COL2A1 signature finding

**Next step: skeletal survey + COL2A1 sequencing** — not empiric treatment for the wrong diagnosis.

**The trap:** delayed ossification + femoral head irregularity + limp in a child = Perthes pattern-match. The bilateral, synchronous, symmetrical involvement and family history of myopia + early OA break the pattern. Always ask: is this truly bilateral and simultaneous? If yes, think skeletal dysplasia.

*Reference: Kannu et al. — COL2A1 mutations identified in children with bilateral Perthes-like presentation; genetic testing instrumental in atypical or bilateral cases*
cards.wrongAnswer: activity modification + serial imaging for Legg-Calvé-Perthes

cards.front: Achondroplasia — Zone of Proliferation (FGFR3 Defect)
cards.back:
Wrong answer: C (zone of hypertrophy). Correct: **B (zone of proliferation)**.

**Achondroplasia:** autosomal dominant, **gain-of-function FGFR3 mutation** → STAT1 upregulation → inhibits chondrocyte proliferation in the **zone of proliferation**. Result: short-limbed dwarfism, frontal bossing, normal intelligence, hearing impairment.

**Physis zones — what goes wrong where:**

| Zone | Disease |
|---|---|
| Resting (reserve) | Multiple epiphyseal dysplasia |
| **Proliferative** | **Achondroplasia** (FGFR3 inhibits chondrocyte division here) |
| Hypertrophic | Salter-Harris fractures (mechanically weakest); rickets (hypertrophic chondrocyte accumulation) |
| Provisional calcification | Scurvy (failure of osteoid deposition) |

**The trap:** Zone of hypertrophy (C) is the Salter-Harris zone — it's the most tested physis zone in trauma, so it's the reflexive answer. Achondroplasia's defect is one zone proximal, in proliferation.

**Physeal bar resection criteria:** >2 years growth remaining + bar <50% of physis cross-sectional area → resect with interposition (fat, PMMA, silastic, fibrin, dura).

cards.front: Arthrogryposis Hip Dislocation — Early Open Reduction *(guessed correctly)*
cards.back:
Got this right by guessing. Needs reinforcement.

**Why open reduction (not Pavlik, not observation, not waiting):**
Hip dislocations in arthrogryposis occur in utero and are **rigid** — the stiff, fibrotic soft tissues make them completely non-amenable to closed reduction or Pavlik harness. This is the fundamental distinction from idiopathic DDH.

**Unilateral vs. bilateral dislocation in arthrogryposis:**
- **Unilateral → always operate:** untreated unilateral dislocation → pelvic obliquity → difficulty sitting/standing → progressive scoliosis. This is the surgical imperative.
- **Bilateral → often observe:** children with bilateral arthrogrypotic hip dislocations can ambulate surprisingly well. Surgery is individualized and less urgent.

**Timing:** Open reduction **before 1 year of age** → best success rate, reduces need for further surgery.

**Approach:** Anteromedial (modified Smith-Petersen) or anterolateral — both give good results. Miao et al.: modified Smith-Petersen → high success rate, low AVN, low recurrence.

**Anchor:** Arthrogryposis hip dislocation = rigid = always needs open reduction. Unilateral = operate early to prevent pelvic obliquity and scoliosis.

cards.front: MMC Calcaneovalgus — Tibiotalar Valgus Predicts Worse Outcome
cards.back:
Wrong answer: rigid forefoot abduction. Correct: **tibiotalar valgus**.

**L5 MMC → calcaneovalgus** is the most common associated foot deformity (hindfoot valgus + forefoot abduction). Foot/ankle deformities occur in 60–90% of MMC due to L1–S1 nerve root involvement.

**Why tibiotalar valgus predicts worse surgical outcomes:**
Calcaneovalgus surgery (calcaneal slide, calcaneal lengthening, subtalar arthrodesis, Grice extra-articular arthrodesis) corrects deformity at the **subtalar and hindfoot level**. If the apex of valgus is at the **tibiotalar joint itself** (tibiotalar valgus), these procedures cannot address it — the ankle joint is the problem, not just the hindfoot. Result: residual deformity and less favorable outcome.

**Preoperative evaluation:** standing AP ankle radiograph → assess talar tilt and tibiotalar valgus before any surgical planning.

**Rigid forefoot abduction** is a component of the calcaneovalgus deformity and can limit correction, but it is not the specific preoperative finding most predictive of surgical failure.

**Surgical options for calcaneovalgus:**
- Calcaneal slide osteotomy
- Calcaneal lengthening osteotomy (Modified Evans — Doğan et al.: average 7.3mm lengthening, favorable outcomes in MMC patients)
- Subtalar arthrodesis
- Grice extra-articular arthrodesis

cards.front: Painful CP Hip (GMFCS V, Deformed Head) → Proximal Femoral Resection, Not Reconstruction
cards.back:
Wrong answer: GMFCS V + open reduction with VDRO, femoral shortening, psoas release, and pelvic osteotomy (reconstruction). Correct: **GMFCS V + proximal femoral resection** (salvage).

**GMFCS level first:** 15-year-old, non-ambulatory, cannot maintain antigravity head/trunk posture, transported and no independent mobility = **GMFCS V** (the most severe level). That immediately kills the two GMFCS I options — this child is not ambulatory.

**Why resection, not reconstruction:**
- The hip is chronically dislocated AND the CT shows significant **femoral head flattening/deformity**. A deformed, arthritic head in a skeletally mature, non-ambulatory patient is no longer reconstructable.
- Reconstruction (VDRO + pelvic osteotomy ± femoral shortening) is for the **younger child with a preserved, congruent femoral head** — you can relocate and remodel it.
- Once the head is destroyed and the patient is a non-ambulator with a painful sitting hip, the goal shifts to **pain relief and sitting tolerance**, not a functional weight-bearing joint → **proximal femoral resection** (or McHale: femoral head resection + valgus osteotomy).

**Background:** Hip instability is uncommon in ambulatory CP but very common in non-ambulatory CP. **50–75% of dislocated CP hips become painful.**

**Evidence:**
- Muthusamy et al. — Level 4, 27 CP patients, ~8-yr follow-up after proximal femoral resection: improved hip pain, ROM, ADLs, quality of life.
- Leet et al. — Level 4, femoral head resection with traction vs. McHale (head resection + valgus osteotomy): McHale group had shorter LOS, less postoperative superior migration, fewer complications. Both improved sitting tolerance and decreased pain.

**The trap:** the reconstruction option is loaded with impressive-sounding procedures (VDRO + shortening + psoas release + pelvic osteotomy) and pattern-matches to "fix the dislocated hip." But GMFCS V + destroyed head = salvage, not reconstruction. Match the surgery to (1) ambulatory status and (2) whether the head is salvageable.
cards.wrongAnswer: GMFCS V + open reduction with VDRO, femoral shortening, psoas release, and pelvic osteotomy (reconstruction)

cards.front: Osteogenesis Imperfecta **Type V** — IFITM5, No Collagen Defect, Hyperplastic Callus
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Type I. Correct: **Type V**.

**The four features in the stem map to Type V exactly:**
1. Autosomal dominant inheritance
2. **No type I collagen mutation** — this is the discriminator. Types I–IV are COL1A1/COL1A2. Type V is **not** a collagen defect.
3. **Hyperplastic (hypertrophic) callus** after fracture
4. Figure = **congenital radial head dislocation**

**Type V OI:**
- Gene: **IFITM5** (BRIL), recurrent **c.-14C>T** mutation. Chromosome **11p15**.
- **Second most common** form of OI.
- Triad tested: **hyperplastic callus + interosseous membrane calcification (forearm/leg) + anterolateral radial head dislocation.**
- Bone is microscopically abnormal ("mesh-like" lamellation) but type I collagen is normal.
- Bisphosphonates still help (reduce fracture rate).

**Why not Type I:** Type I is the mildest, most common form — quantitative COL1A1 defect, blue sclerae, normal stature. It **is** a collagen defect, so "no type I collagen mutation" rules it out immediately.

**Anchor for the whole OI set:** Types I–IV = collagen (COL1A1 chr 17 / COL1A2 chr 7). Types V (IFITM5), VI (SERPINF1), VII (CRTAP) = **non-collagen**. If a stem says "no collagen mutation," it's V, VI, or VII — and hyperplastic callus + radial head dislocation = **V**.

**From the answer choices (Orthobullets classification):**
- Type I: AD, **quantitative** collagen defect, blue sclerae, hearing deficits — **mildest survivable**
- Type II: **AR**, qualitative defect, blue sclerae — **perinatal lethal**
- Type III: **AR**, qualitative defect, normal sclerae, fractures at birth — **most severe survivable**
- Type IV: AD, qualitative defect, normal sclerae — moderate

Note the inheritance split: I and IV dominant, **II and III recessive** per the exam key (classic Sillence teaches I–IV dominant, but match the Orthobullets framing). Quantitative (I) = milder; qualitative (II–IV) = worse.

cards.front: Can't Bear Weight + Physeal Tenderness, Normal X-ray → MRI (Occult Physeal Fracture)
cards.back:
Wrong answer: NSAID challenge. Correct: **MRI**.

**Setup:** 11-year-old, trauma (collision at basketball), **unable to bear weight**, pain **localized over the distal femoral physis**, effusion. Afebrile (98.5), **WBC 9k, CRP 1.7, ESR 33** — not a septic picture. Radiograph doesn't show an obvious fracture.

**Why MRI:** Point tenderness over an open physis + inability to bear weight after trauma = **occult nondisplaced physeal fracture until proven otherwise**. When plain films are negative/equivocal, **MRI** confirms the diagnosis and evaluates the soft tissues (ligaments, physis).
- Distal femoral physeal fractures = 2–5% of physeal injuries; **most commonly Salter-Harris II**.
- **Stress radiographs have fallen out of favor** for nondisplaced SH I — patient discomfort, possible sedation. MRI replaced them.
- Zions et al.: MRI especially useful for nondisplaced distal femoral physeal fractures with a traumatic event + swelling + inability to bear weight; these fractures behave differently than in adults, need close follow-up for growth complications (physeal arrest).

**Why the wrong answers fail:**
- **NSAID challenge** — not a diagnostic step for a suspected physeal fracture. (NSAID response is a feature of osteoid osteoma, not a trauma workup.) Choosing it means you dismissed the fracture.
- **Knee aspiration** — the "cold for 3 days" + effusion is a septic-arthritis distractor, but afebrile with normal WBC/CRP argues against infection; this is traumatic.
- **Hinged brace/PT** or **ORIF** — can't pick a treatment before you've made the diagnosis; premature.

**Anchor:** Kid + open physis + focal physeal tenderness + can't bear weight + normal x-ray = **MRI for occult physeal fracture** (Salter-Harris II most common at the distal femur). Don't treat or dismiss before imaging confirms it.

cards.front: Tibial Eminence Fracture — Type II → Closed Reduction + Cast in EXTENSION (Not Surgery, Not 90° Flexion)
cards.back:
Two errors in one: called it **Type III** and jumped to **ORIF**. Correct: **Type II, closed reduction + long leg cast in extension**.

**Meyers & McKeever classification (tibial eminence / tibial spine = bony ACL avulsion, kids 8–14):**
- **Type I:** nondisplaced
- **Type II:** hinged — anterior portion elevated, **posterior cortex still intact (hinge)**
- **Type III:** completely displaced/separated (no cortical contact)
- (Type IV: comminuted)

**Treatment:**
- **Type I & II → closed reduction + long leg cast in EXTENSION.** Extension lets the femoral condyle press the fragment back down; the intact posterior hinge reduces. Immobilize in full/near extension.
- **Type III (and irreducible II) → open or arthroscopic reduction + internal fixation.** Block to reduction is usually **entrapped anterior horn of the medial meniscus** (or intermeniscal ligament).
- In Type II fixation (when needed), **suture fixation is biomechanically superior to screw fixation.**

**Two traps I fell for:**
1. **Over-classifying II as III** — this fracture has a posterior hinge (Type II), not full separation. Read the amount of displacement on the lateral.
2. **Casting position** — the "90° flexion" options are wrong; tibial eminence fractures reduce in **extension**, not flexion. Flexion tensions the ACL and pulls the fragment up.

**Anchor:** Tibial spine fracture = pediatric bony ACL avulsion. Type I/II → **cast in extension**. Type III/irreducible → **fix it** (watch for entrapped medial meniscus anterior horn). Casting is in extension, never 90° flexion.
cards.correctAnswer: Type II, closed reduction + long leg cast in extension

cards.front: Young CP + Dynamic Equinus → Serial Casting (Delay Heel Cord Lengthening Until Age 4+)
cards.back:
Wrong answer: percutaneous heel cord lengthening, right. Correct: **serial casting of the right foot and ankle**.

**Setup:** 3-year-old, spastic right hemiparesis, toe-walking. Right ankle passively dorsiflexes to **5° short of neutral** (dynamic/spastic equinus, not a rigid fixed contracture). Botox to right calf 1 month ago.

**Why serial casting now:**
- The equinus is still **dynamic** — Botox + **serial casting** (with or without botox) is the technique to reach a neutral ankle and make orthotic fitting possible. You can't fit a good AFO over even mild fixed equinus, so cast first, then brace.
- **Delay surgical tendo-Achilles lengthening until at least age 4.** The gastrosoleus musculotendinous unit is still developing; lengthening too early → high recurrence and risk of **overlengthening → calcaneus (crouch) gait**, which is worse and hard to fix.

**Why not the other options:**
- **Percutaneous heel cord lengthening (chosen)** — surgery too early (patient is 3, deformity is dynamic). Premature TAL is the classic overtreatment trap in young CP.
- **Bilateral AFOs** — the **left** ankle walking in equinus is **compensatory** for the right-sided deformity. Fix the affected right side and the left normalizes; it doesn't need bracing.
- **Right AFO immediately** — can't seat an orthosis over the current equinus; need to cast to neutral first.

**Anchor:** Young CP + dynamic (passively correctable) equinus = **botox + serial casting**, then orthosis. Save tendo-Achilles lengthening for **age ≥ 4** with a true fixed contracture — early TAL overlengthens into calcaneus gait. Compensatory posturing on the unaffected side doesn't get its own brace.
cards.correctAnswer: serial casting of the right foot and ankle

cards.front: Macrodactyly (Local Gigantism) — Closed Physes → Ray Amputation; Cosmesis Is POOR
cards.back:
Wrong answer: "cosmetic result after surgical intervention is often favorable." Correct: **complete amputation of the second ray decreases recurrence rate.**

**Diagnosis:** 14-year-old, one enlarged toe (2nd), no longer fitting shoes, **normal toe nails** = **local gigantism / macrodactyly**. Nonhereditary, disproportionate overgrowth of one toe (usually 2nd).

**Management by physeal status:**
- **Open physes** → epiphysiodesis can arrest further growth.
- **Closed physes** (this patient) → epiphysiodesis is too late; need **bony reduction and/or complete ray amputation** to correct size and **prevent recurrence.** Ray amputation lowers recurrence.

**Why the chosen answer is wrong:** surgical outcomes are frequently **poor cosmetically** — complications include recurrence, cutaneous necrosis, and infection (Turra et al.). Saying cosmesis is "often favorable" is the opposite of the truth.

**Associations to know (nerve/overgrowth link):** macrodactyly relates to **nerve trunk disease** — many cases represent **undiagnosed neurofibromatosis** (Turra et al.). Also seen with **Proteus** (mosaic AKT1) and **Klippel-Trenaunay-Weber** (mosaic PIK3CA), both PI3K-AKT-mTOR overgrowth disorders (Guidera et al.). Conservative care is the mainstay; **complete ray resection** prevents forefoot enlargement/recurrence.

**Anchor:** Macrodactyly = nonhereditary single-toe overgrowth, normal nails. **Open physis → epiphysiodesis; closed physis → ray amputation.** Cosmesis after surgery is poor. Think NF / Proteus / Klippel-Trenaunay behind the overgrowth.
cards.correctAnswer: complete amputation of the second ray decreases recurrence rate
cards.wrongAnswer: cosmetic result after surgical intervention is often favorable

cards.front: Perthes, Age >8 + Lateral Pillar B → Surgical Containment (Osteotomy), Not Bracing
cards.back:
Wrong answer: continued activity limitation and bracing. Correct: **femoral or pelvic osteotomy.**

**Setup:** 9-year-old, painless progressive limp, near-full abduction (still contained/mobile). Imaging = LCP with femoral head collapse, **lateral pillar group B**.

**The two variables that decide treatment (Herring):**
1. **Age at onset** — the dominant prognostic factor. Bone age >6 / chronologic **>8 years** = worse remodeling potential.
2. **Lateral pillar (Herring) classification:** A (no height loss, always good), **B (>50% height maintained)**, B/C border, C (<50% height, poor regardless).

**Herring's result (the tested fact):** children **>8 at onset with lateral pillar B or B/C border** have **better outcomes with surgery — femoral varus osteotomy or pelvic (Salter) osteotomy** (containment). Contrast:
- **<8 with group B** → good outcome regardless of treatment (don't need surgery).
- **Group C** at any age → frequently poor regardless of treatment.

So this patient (>8 + pillar B) sits squarely in the group that benefits from **operative containment**.

**Why bracing is wrong:** abduction bracing/orthoses have **not** been shown to improve outcomes and have fallen out of favor — a large multicenter study found no benefit over no treatment. Choosing "continued bracing" ignores the age + pillar data that call for surgery.

**Why the others fail:** hip aspiration (this is painless, chronic — not septic); core decompression (that's for AVN/osteonecrosis in adults, not Perthes); metabolic work-up (unilateral Perthes with a classic picture doesn't need a metabolic hunt — bilateral symmetric disease would).

**Anchor:** Perthes treatment = **age + lateral pillar.** Age >8 + pillar B/(B-C border) → **containment osteotomy** (femoral varus or Salter). Younger or pillar A → good without surgery; pillar C → poor regardless. **Bracing doesn't change outcomes.** (Principle of containment: keep the plastic femoral head seated in the acetabulum while it reossifies.)

cards.subtopic: Brachial Plexus Birth Palsy / Tendon Transfer
cards.front: Hoffer Transfer in Erb's Palsy — Contraindication Is a CONVEX (Dysplastic) Glenoid, Not the IR Contracture
cards.back:
Wrong answer: severe internal rotation contracture. Correct: **convex glenoid.**

**Setup:** 5-year-old, difficult birth (macrosomia), classic **Erb's palsy** posture (arm adducted, internally rotated, "waiter's tip"), only 5° active ER, deltoid 4/5, normal hand. Question asks the **contraindication** to the **Hoffer procedure** (latissimus dorsi + teres major transfer to the posterosuperior rotator cuff to restore ER).

**Why convex glenoid:** the Hoffer/Sever-L'Episcopo transfer works by **inducing glenohumeral remodeling in a growing child.** That only works if the joint can still remodel. **Advanced glenohumeral dysplasia — convex or absent glenoid, complete dislocation, humeral head flattening — cannot remodel**, so the transfer fails. In that setting you do a **proximal humeral derotational osteotomy** instead (repositions the arm without relying on joint remodeling).

**Why my answer is wrong (the core trap):** a **severe internal rotation contracture is the INDICATION** for the Hoffer transfer, not a contraindication. I inverted the logic — the question asked what makes you NOT do it. The IR contracture + weak external rotators is exactly the problem the transfer treats.

**Full contraindication list:**
- **Convex/dysplastic glenoid** (advanced GH dysplasia) → derotational osteotomy instead
- **Teres major / latissimus dorsi weakness < 4/5** (the transfer needs a strong donor)
- **Deltoid weakness < 2/5** (this patient's deltoid is 4/5 — fine)

**Distractors:** "no motor function of subscapularis" — subscapularis is an internal rotator; its status isn't the transfer contraindication. "Positive Hornblower's" indicates teres minor/ER weakness = part of the *indication*. "Glenoid anteroversion" — brachial plexus dysplasia causes glenoid **retroversion**, not anteversion.

**Anchor:** Hoffer (lat dorsi + teres major → posterosuperior cuff) restores ER in Erb's palsy by driving glenohumeral remodeling. **Contraindicated when the joint can't remodel = convex/absent glenoid** (→ derotational osteotomy), or when donor (teres major/lat) < 4/5 or deltoid < 2/5. The IR contracture is the reason you operate, not a reason to stop.

cards.front: Stiff-Knee Gait in Spastic Diplegia = Rectus Femoris Overactivity → Distal RF Transfer
cards.back:
Wrong answer: adductor magnus. Correct: **rectus femoris.**

**The concept:** In spastic diplegic CP, **stiff-knee gait** (reduced/late peak knee flexion in swing, poor foot clearance, tripping) is caused by **rectus femoris overactivity during pre-swing and early swing.** The RF is a **two-joint muscle** (hip flexor + knee extensor); when spastic, it restrains the knee flexion that normally happens at terminal stance/initial swing.

**Treatment — distal RF transfer, not release:** the goal is to **keep RF's hip-flexion pull (for limb advancement) while removing its knee-extension restraint.** A **distal rectus femoris transfer** (to a knee flexor — sartorius, gracilis, or semitendinosus) does this better than a simple release.
- Chambers et al. (70 CP patients): swing-phase peak knee flexion improved in the **transfer** group vs. release → distal RF transfer preferred.
- Ounpuu et al. (136 limbs): when preop knee ROM was >80% of normal, transfer and release performed similarly — so patient selection matters, but transfer is the default.

**Why adductor magnus is wrong:** the adductors drive **scissoring gait** (hip adduction), not stiff-knee gait. I confused the two CP gait patterns. Adductor overactivity → adductor release/lengthening; it has nothing to do with knee flexion in swing.

**Anchor:** Stiff-knee gait in CP = **rectus femoris** (two-joint, overactive pre-/early swing) → **distal RF transfer** (retain hip flexion, free up knee flexion). Scissoring gait = adductors. Don't cross the two.

cards.front: L5 MMC + Calcaneus Foot → Posterior Transfer of Tibialis Anterior (NOT Triple Arthrodesis)
cards.back:
Got the **level right (L5)** but the **procedure wrong** — chose triple arthrodesis. Correct: **L5, posterior transfer of the anterior tibial tendon.**

**Level read:** ambulator + **talipes calcaneus** (calcaneus foot on x-ray) = **L5** myelomeningocele. The calcaneus deformity comes from a **functioning tibialis anterior (L4/L5) with weak/absent plantarflexors** (gastroc/soleus, S1–S2) → unopposed dorsiflexion.

**Procedure — posterior tibialis anterior transfer:** move the anterior tibial tendon posteriorly (through the interosseous membrane to the calcaneus) to **rebalance dynamic forces** and get a plantigrade foot. Park et al. (31 pts, L5/sacral MMC + calcaneus): TA transfer increased peak plantarflexion, decreased peak dorsiflexion, restored forefoot/midfoot loading.

**Why not triple arthrodesis:** **avoid arthrodesis in most myelodysplastic feet** — insensate skin + rigid fusion → pressure sores and breakdown. Triple is reserved for **severe, rigid deformity in a sensate foot** only. Reflexively reaching for a fusion is the trap; the problem here is a *dynamic muscle imbalance*, which a **tendon transfer** fixes.

**MMC level → foot/ambulation map:**
- **L1:** externally rotated, flexed hips; equinovarus feet; **HKAFO**; non-functional ambulator.
- **L3:** adducted, flexed hips; recurvatum knee; equinovarus feet; **KAFO**; household ambulator.
- **L5:** community ambulator; **calcaneus foot** → **posterior TA transfer.**

**Anchor:** L5 MMC = ambulator with **calcaneus foot** (unopposed tibialis anterior) → **posterior TA tendon transfer**, not arthrodesis. Fusion is contraindicated in insensate myelodysplastic feet except for severe rigid deformity with sensation. This is the dynamic-imbalance cousin of entry [4] (L5 calcaneovalgus).
cards.correctAnswer: L5, posterior transfer of the anterior tibial tendon

cards.front: MPS Associations — Dural Ectasia Is the ODD ONE OUT (That's Marfan/EDS/NF1/AS)
cards.back:
EXCEPT question. 6-year-old on **enzyme replacement therapy** = **mucopolysaccharidosis (MPS)**. Asked which is NOT associated. I chose thoracolumbar kyphosis (which IS associated). Correct answer: **dural ectasia.**

**MPS skeletal features (dysostosis multiplex) — all the distractors:**
- Thoracolumbar **kyphosis/gibbus**
- **Genu valgum**
- **Carpal tunnel syndrome** (thickened flexor retinaculum + tenosynovium — CTS in a young child = red flag for MPS)
- **Atlantoaxial instability** (odontoid hypoplasia — Morquio classically)
- Also: hip dysplasia, **short ulna + Madelung deformity**, short metacarpals with proximal tapering, broad ("oar-shaped") ribs, broad clavicles, trigger finger, cervical stenosis

**Why dural ectasia is the exception:** dural ectasia is **NOT an MPS feature.** It's associated with **Marfan syndrome** (part of the Ghent criteria), **Ehlers-Danlos**, **neurofibromatosis type 1**, and **ankylosing spondylitis** — connective-tissue/dysplastic dura conditions, not lysosomal storage.

**Key MPS treatment fact (why the question set it up):** enzyme replacement therapy and HSCT improve **cardiac, respiratory, and somatic/visceral** function but **do NOT penetrate osteocartilaginous tissue** — so they **do not correct the skeletal disease.** These kids still need multiple orthopaedic procedures.

**Anchor:** MPS = dysostosis multiplex (kyphosis, genu valgum, CTS, atlantoaxial instability, Madelung/short ulna). **Dural ectasia belongs to Marfan/EDS/NF1/AS, not MPS.** ERT/HSCT fix the organs, not the bones.

cards.front: Late DDH (>18 mo / excessive-abduction reduction) → Open Reduction + Capsulorrhaphy ± Femoral/Pelvic Osteotomy
cards.back:
Got the concept (open reduction) but chose the **wrong soft-tissue combo.** Correct: **open hip capsulorrhaphy ± femoral osteotomy ± pelvic osteotomy** (option 4). I picked the option that added **adductor + hamstring tenotomy + gastrocnemius recession** (option 5).

**Why open reduction at all:** healthy **20-month-old** (>18 mo) with late DDH, and the arthrogram shows concentric reduction **only at 70–80° abduction** = **excessive abduction (>60°) needed to hold it** → high AVN risk in a spica. Both facts (age >18 mo AND unstable except in extreme abduction) push past closed reduction/Pavlik to **open reduction.**

**What open reduction of idiopathic DDH involves:** remove the **blocks to concentric reduction** — iliopsoas (tendon), **joint capsule** (capsulorrhaphy after), **ligamentum teres, pulvinar, labrum, transverse acetabular ligament** (Wenger). Then add stability: **femoral osteotomy** (VDRO ± shortening) and/or **pelvic osteotomy** (coverage). Approach: anterior or anteromedial.

**Why option 5 is wrong (the trap):** **adductor + hamstring tenotomy + gastrocnemius recession** is a **neuromuscular/spastic hip** soft-tissue release pattern (cerebral palsy). This is a **healthy** child with **idiopathic** DDH — those extra-articular muscle lengthenings aren't part of the idiopathic DDH open reduction. Adding them signals treating the wrong disease.

**Why the other options fail:**
- **Observation / Pavlik** — Pavlik is for infants <6 months; useless at 20 months (and Pavlik after ~6 mo risks AVN, doesn't work on an established dislocation).
- **Closed reduction + spica** — fails here because reduction needs >60° abduction to hold → AVN risk. Excessive abduction to maintain reduction is itself an indication to open.

**Anchor:** DDH by age — **<6 mo Pavlik; 6 mo–18 mo closed reduction + spica; >18 mo (or closed reduction needs >60° abduction / can't get concentric) → open reduction** + capsulorrhaphy ± femoral ± pelvic osteotomy. Idiopathic DDH releases target intra-articular blocks (iliopsoas, capsule, ligamentum teres, pulvinar, TAL) — **not** adductor/hamstring/gastroc (that's the neuromuscular hip).
cards.correctAnswer: open hip capsulorrhaphy ± femoral osteotomy ± pelvic osteotomy (option 4)

cards.front: Capitellum Fracture — Posterior Comminution = Worst Outcome
cards.back:
Wrong answer: 5mm vertical displacement postreduction. Correct: **posterior comminution**.

**Posterior comminution** is the finding most associated with higher complication rates and worse outcomes in capitellum fractures. It mandates more extensive surgical approaches (total elbow arthroplasty or olecranon osteotomy) and has a significantly higher complication rate.

**Marinelli et al. (45 patients, 2007–2015):**
- Type A (no posterior comminution): lateral approach + screw fixation → **29% complications**
- Type B (posterior comminution): TEA or olecranon osteotomy → **64% complications**
- Conclusion: posterior comminution significantly predicts poorer outcomes

**Capitellum fracture basics:**
- 6% of distal humerus fractures; mechanism = axial compression with elbow flexed → shear
- Associated injuries: radial head fracture +/- LUCL injury in up to 60%
- Lateral XR: **"double arc" sign** = subchondral bone from capitellum + lateral trochlea
- CT for full assessment of fracture pattern and posterior comminution

**Treatment by displacement:**
- <2mm: nonoperative (elbow immobilization)
- >2mm: ORIF, arthroscopic-assisted ORIF, fragment excision, or TEA

**The trap:** residual postreduction displacement matters, but the specific predictor of worst outcomes is the posterior comminution pattern — it changes the entire surgical approach and roughly doubles the complication rate.

cards.front: Spondylolysis Imaging — Oblique Views Add NO Diagnostic Information; AP + Lateral Alone Is Best Initial
cards.back:
Wrong answer: Oblique radiographs add additional diagnostic information (with more radiation). Correct: **AP and lateral radiographs alone are the best initial studies**.

**Modern data overturned classic teaching:**
- Classic: oblique views show "Scotty dog with collar" = pars defect
- **Current evidence: oblique views do NOT provide additional diagnostic information** beyond AP + lateral, AND expose the patient to additional radiation
- AP + lateral is sufficient as the initial radiographic study; pars defect visible on up to **80% of lateral radiographs**

**Spondylolysis imaging algorithm:**
1. **AP + lateral** lumbar spine → best initial (no obliques)
2. Negative or inconclusive → **MRI** (no radiation; comparable sensitivity/specificity to SPECT) [see entry 62]
3. **CT**: best for bony detail of pars; useful for surgical planning

**Other facts tested in this question:**
- Most common anatomic location: **pars intraarticularis of L5** (not L4)
- Prevalence: **4-6%** of the population (not 5-10%)
- Common in adolescent athletes: causes up to **47%** of low back pain in this population
- MRI = SPECT in sensitivity/specificity (answer 3 claiming MRI is less sensitive is FALSE)
cards.correctAnswer: AP and lateral radiographs alone are the best initial studies
cards.wrongAnswer: Oblique radiographs add additional diagnostic information (with more radiation)

cards.front: Post-op Epidural Hematoma — MRI Signal by Stage (All Four Phases)
cards.back:
Correct answer: **Hyperacute (<24 hrs) = hypointense T1, hyperintense T2** (due to oxygenated hemoglobin).

**Full MRI signal table — epidural hematoma:**

| Stage | T1 | T2 | Underlying molecule |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hyperacute (<24 hrs)** | Hypointense | **Hyperintense** | Oxyhemoglobin |
| **Acute (1-3 days)** | Isointense | Hypointense | Deoxyhemoglobin |
| **Subacute (3 days – 2 weeks)** | **Hyperintense** | Hypointense | Methemoglobin |
| **Chronic (>2 weeks)** | Hypointense | Hypointense | Hemosiderin |

STIR: hyperacute hematoma = **hyperintense** on STIR (answer 5 "hypointense on STIR" was wrong).

**Clinical facts:**
- Incidence: **1:1000** post-op spinal surgery
- Missed → permanent neurologic deficit
- Diagnosis: immediate MRI
- Treatment: urgent surgical evacuation; shorter time to evacuation → better neurologic recovery

**Kao et al:** Higher risk with elevated preoperative diastolic BP, gelfoam use for dural coverage, decreased post-op drain output. Earlier evacuation → better neurologic improvement.
