Hepatitis A is NOT blood-borne (fecal-oral). Staph aureus is a bacterium, not a virus. Both excluded by the "viral microbe" framing. West Nile and HTLV rarer and also screened.

Trap: HIV is the most feared transfusion infection, so it feels like the high-risk answer. The numbers invert that — HBV is most frequently transmitted. Rank: HBV > HCV > HIV for residual per-unit risk.

cards.front: Closed femur fracture blood loss — 1000-1500 cc each; bilateral ≈ 2000-3000 cc
cards.back:
*(Answered correctly but flagged by Daniel as a guess.)*

Estimated blood loss: closed femoral shaft 1000-1500 cc; closed tibia 500-1000 cc. Open fractures roughly double; bilateral closed femurs roughly double a single femur → bilateral closed femoral shafts = 2000-3000 cc total.

Lieurance et al. (53 isolated femur fractures): mean estimated loss 1276 cc; transfusion in 21 patients (avg 2.5 units PRBC), independent of fracture pattern or energy; preoperative hemorrhage drove transfusion need more than intraoperative loss. With exsanguinating external hemorrhage, control bleeding before ABCDE (Lee and Porter stepwise: direct pressure → elevation → wound packing → windlass → traction splint → tourniquet).

Imaging aside: bilateral ATYPICAL femur fractures — bisphosphonate history, transverse pattern with medial beaking, lateral cortical thickening/stress reaction, subtrochanteric/diaphyseal. The imaging + history combo is its own common test item.

Anchor: tibia 0.5-1 L, femur 1-1.5 L, double for open or bilateral.

cards.front: RSA for proximal humerus fracture — cemented (keyed) vs uncemented (modern trend), evidence mixed
cards.back:
Complex proximal humerus fracture-dislocation in an elderly patient with comminution, poor bone stock, and high head-AVN risk → reverse total shoulder arthroplasty (rTSA) with tuberosity repair. rTSA preferred over hemiarthroplasty/anatomic TSA because those depend on tuberosity healing. Indications for rTSA: >70 yr, severely comminuted, high AVN likelihood, poor tuberosity bone quality.

Orthobullets keys CEMENTED rTSA with tuberosity repair. Daniel flagged this as outdated (uncemented now widely used/trending preferred). Verified via WebSearch 2026-06-16 — evidence is genuinely mixed:
- Function largely equivalent cemented vs uncemented; some series favor cemented (higher forward flexion, ASES, satisfaction).
- Tuberosity healing: one series favored uncemented (91% vs 64% anatomic healing) — strongest argument for uncemented; healed tuberosities → better ROM/function.
- COMPLICATIONS: a meta-analysis found cemented LOWER (5.5% vs 9.7%), similar reoperation rates. Uncemented → more stress shielding (30% vs 0%); cemented → more radiolucent lines.

Exam strategy: the stem asks to "minimize complications" — published complication numbers favor cemented, so the keyed answer is defensible on its own terms. Real-world: uncemented (press-fit, fracture-specific) stems are increasingly preferred, mainly for tuberosity healing and avoiding cement issues. Not a clean "standard of care" win either direction.

Repair the greater tuberosity to restore infraspinatus/teres minor → ER strength (Jobin). Scapular notching is the most common rTSA complication (~25-32%).

Sources: cemented vs uncemented rTSA meta-analysis (J Shoulder Elbow / ScienceDirect S1058274621007606); cementless rTSA outcomes (S1058274620309307); cemented-vs-uncemented RSA acute fracture (S1058274621005784).

cards.front: Lateral perifibular fasciotomy — superficial peroneal nerve at risk proximally (not common peroneal)
cards.back:
Single lateral perifibular incision for leg fasciotomy: straight lateral, just posterior and parallel to the fibula, from distal to the fibular neck down to the lateral malleolus. Structure at risk during proximal dissection = superficial peroneal nerve.

The proximal extent stops DISTAL to the fibular neck, so it does not reach the common peroneal nerve or its bifurcation. At the proximal end, identify and protect the SPN; care also where SPN exits the lateral compartment fascia and runs anteriorly in the distal third. Deep peroneal nerve + anterior tibial artery are in the anterior compartment (away); lateral inferior genicular artery is more midline (away).

RECURRING TRAP: picked common peroneal nerve — same error as [40] (answer was also SPN). Common peroneal is endangered at the FIBULAR NECK; these distal/lateral leg approaches stay below the neck, so the nerve in the field is the SPN. Third SPN question ([40], [61], [67]) with a consistent CPN-for-SPN substitution.

cards.front: Chronic posterior sternoclavicular dislocation — open reduction in OR with thoracic backup
cards.back:
Posterior SC dislocation >3 weeks old = chronic → needs OPEN reduction (closed reduction reliably fails after ~3 weeks due to scarring). Do it in the OR with cardiothoracic surgery backup: medial clavicle overlies great vessels, trachea, esophagus (this patient has dysphagia).

Posterior dislocations are reduced even if initially asymptomatic, to prevent delayed mediastinal complications: respiratory distress, venous congestion/arterial insufficiency, brachial plexus compression, myocardial conduction abnormalities (Wirth and Rockwood). Reconstruct costoclavicular ligaments + resect medial clavicle head as needed for unstable/symptomatic injuries. NEVER use K-wires across SC joint (lethal mediastinal migration).

Contrast: chronic ANTERIOR dislocations usually nonoperative if asymptomatic.

Trap: the two nonoperative options are the anterior-dislocation answer applied to the wrong direction. Posterior gets reduced (mediastinal risk); closed reduction is off the table at 3 months → open reduction.

cards.front: Proximal femoral bursting after antegrade nailing — too-anterior piriformis start point
cards.back:
Anterior cortical bursting (split) of the proximal femur after antegrade nailing = starting point TOO ANTERIOR with a piriformis (single sagittal bow) nail. Too-anterior entry drives the nail against the anterior cortex → high hoop stresses → fracture.

Papadakis: anterior bursting in 56% of cadaver femora nailed too anteriorly, none with more posterior entry. Johnson: starting-hole position is the dominant factor; anterior displacement >6 mm → high hoop stress + bursting; fix by smaller-diameter nail or overreaming 1-2 mm. Antegrade nailing = 3-point fixation (sagittal: proximal anterior, mid posterior, distal anterior cortex), so entry geometry matters.

Distractors: external rotation torque after proximal interlocking (the pick) rotates the whole proximal fragment, can cause screw cut-out, NOT a proximal split. Excessive interfragmentary compression fragments the fracture site, not the entry. Trochanteric nail through piriformis fossa → valgus; piriformis nail through trochanter → varus (= entry [34]) — malalignment, not bursting.

Discriminator: entry too anterior = BURSTING; entry on wrong medial-lateral side = VARUS/VALGUS malalignment. Same "entry point matters" theme as [34], different failure mode.

cards.front: Hip fracture mortality — in-hospital ~6%, one-year 20-30%
cards.back:
Current in-hospital mortality after hip fracture surgery ~6%; one-year mortality 20-30% (series-dependent). Answer: in-hospital 6%, one-year 30%.

Jiang et al. (~4000 patients): 30.8% one-year mortality; ~1 in 15 die during hospitalization (~6.5%); higher with older age, male sex, pre-injury nursing-home residence, ≥10 comorbidities. Mundi et al. meta-analysis (31 yr): femoral neck mortality stayed ~20% over time. Brauer et al.: incidence fell over 2 decades while comorbidity rose, yet mortality declined.

Distractors bracket the truth (10%/35% just above, 2.5%/15% just below) — rewards the memorized pair. Anchor: ~6% in-hospital, 20-30% at 1 year.

Population note: this is baseline elderly hip fracture. Entry [45] is the DIALYSIS subgroup at ~45% two-year mortality — different population, much worse.

cards.front: Transabdominal GSW to the spine with bowel injury — broad-spectrum IV antibiotics x7 days
cards.back:
A GSW that perforates bowel and lodges in the spine, neurologically intact, is treated with 7 days of BROAD-SPECTRUM IV antibiotics — covering Gram-positive AND Gram-negative (not Gram-negative alone).

Roffi et al. (42 bowel-perforating GSW entering spine): extended broad-spectrum regimen + bedrest significantly reduced spinal/paraspinal infection; early bullet removal did not. Velmahos et al. (24 GSW spine + colonic injury): sepsis 8.4% vs 5% without bowel injury; retaining the bullet did NOT raise sepsis.

Surgery reserved for: neurologic deficit correlating with imaging compression by the missile, OR bullet in contact with CSF (lead toxicity). Neither applies here. IV beats oral.

Trap: antibiotic spectrum. Bowel flora is polymicrobial (Gram+, Gram-, anaerobes) → Gram-negative-only coverage (the pick) is inadequate; broad-spectrum is the point. Retained vertebral bullet does not by itself mandate removal.

cards.front: Femur fracture + head injury — intraoperative hypotension drives bad neurologic outcome, not the nail
cards.back:
In a femoral shaft fracture + closed head injury, the factor most likely to cause an adverse outcome is intraoperative HYPOTENSION. Hypotension (+ elevated ICP, hypoxia, over-resuscitation) lowers cerebral perfusion pressure; low CPP damages the injured brain. CPP = MAP - ICP — a drop in MAP or rise in ICP starves the brain.

Immediate reamed IM nailing is acceptable when hemodynamically stable and CPP maintained. If not, external fixation (damage control). Temporary skeletal traction for when labile ICP precludes the OR. Monitor ICP and protect CPP if fixing early.

Trap: blaming the procedure. The "second hit" concern with reamed nailing in head injury is really about avoiding hypotension/hypoxia during it — nailing itself isn't the problem if perfusion is held. The pick (immediate reamed nailing) is appropriate in a stable patient; the physiologic insult causes harm.

cards.front: Tourniquet during tibial nailing + reamed femoral nailing — increased pulmonary morbidity
cards.back:
Using a tourniquet during tibial nailing in a multitrauma patient who also gets reamed femoral nailing increases postoperative PULMONARY morbidity. Two parallel hits: limb reperfusion after tourniquet ischemia → pulmonary microvascular injury; reamed femoral nailing → marrow microemboli doing the same. Both raise pulmonary capillary permeability + edema. Pollak et al. (72 patients): more pulmonary morbidity in the tourniquet group.

Tourniquet does NOT cause thermal/heat effects. Karunakar (canine): no difference in reaming heat with vs without tourniquet — thermal necrosis is reamer-to-isthmus mismatch, not the tourniquet (= entry [44]). Giannoudis: most heat from large reamers (11-12 mm) in small isthmus (8-9 mm); tourniquet, steroids, knee flexion NOT associated with diaphyseal necrosis.

Trap: the stem mentions over-reaming + tourniquet, baiting "increased cortical bone temperature." Heat is a reaming/isthmus problem; the tourniquet's proven harm is PULMONARY. Two separate mechanisms in one stem.

cards.front: Braking time after ankle ORIF — normal by 9 weeks post-op (Egol)
cards.back:
Braking ability after operative ankle fracture fixation returns to normal by 9 weeks POSTOPERATIVELY. Egol et al. tested at 6, 9, 12 weeks vs healthy controls; total braking time normalized by 9 weeks. Patient is 8 weeks postop → answer is 1 week from now (9 - 8 = 1).

Trap: reference frame. Bedside heuristic is ~6 weeks AFTER weight-bearing begins, but Egol measures from DATE OF SURGERY. For the exam, anchor on "9 weeks from surgery," then do arithmetic against where the patient is. No firm DMV/legal standard exists — study-based guidance.

cards.front: Explain the ulnar nerve paradox
cards.back: A distal ulnar nerve lesion (e.g., at Guyon's canal/wrist) produces worse clawing of the ring and small fingers than a proximal lesion (e.g., at the elbow). With a distal lesion, the intrinsic muscles (lumbricals 3&4, interossei, hypothenar muscles) are paralyzed — but the FDP to the ring and small fingers, which is supplied by the ulnar nerve in the forearm (proximal to the lesion), remains intact. The intact FDP pulls the DIP into flexion unopposed, producing a pronounced claw. With a proximal lesion, FDP is also weak, reducing the flexion force — paradoxically less clawing. More distal lesion = worse claw.

cards.front: Where is the radial nerve reliably identified in the posterior arm?
cards.back: 4 cm proximal to the triceps aponeurosis. This is the consistent surgical landmark for locating the radial nerve during posterior arm exploration or proximal humeral fracture/fixation approaches.

cards.front: How do EMG/NCS findings distinguish preganglionic from postganglionic brachial plexus injury?
cards.back: Preganglionic injury (proximal to DRG): absent/abnormal motor conduction but INTACT sensory conduction. The dorsal root ganglion (DRG) cell body sits outside the spinal cord and is spared — its peripheral axon remains connected to the cell body and does not undergo Wallerian degeneration, so sensory NCS stays normal despite no clinical sensation. Postganglionic injury (distal to DRG): both motor and sensory conduction are abnormal. Clinical significance: preganglionic = nerve root avulsion = not reconstructable with nerve grafting. Postganglionic = potentially repairable.

cards.front: What are the 4 preclavicular (pre-trunk) branches of the brachial plexus and their roots?
cards.back: Long thoracic (C5,6,7), Dorsal scapular (C5), Suprascapular (C5,6), Subclavius (C5,6).
cards.correctAnswer: Long thoracic (C5,6,7), Dorsal scapular (C5), Suprascapular (C5,6), Subclavius (C5,6).

cards.front: What are the 7 cord branches of the brachial plexus, organized by cord?
cards.back:
- Lateral cord: Lateral pectoral (C5,6,7)
- Posterior cord: Upper subscapular (C5,6), Lower subscapular (C5,6), Thoracodorsal (C6,7,8)
- Medial cord: Medial pectoral (C8,T1), Medial cutaneous nerve to forearm (C8), Medial cutaneous nerve to arm (T1)
Mnemonic — cord branches of medial and lateral cords all have "Medial" or "Lateral" in their names respectively.

cards.front: What are the 5 terminal branches of the brachial plexus, their cords of origin, and root values?
cards.back:
- Lateral cord → Musculocutaneous (C5,6,7)
- Posterior cord → Axillary (C5,6) + Radial (C5–T1) [mnemonic: PAR = Posterior → Axillary + Radial]
- Lateral + Medial cords → Median (C5–T1)
- Medial cord → Ulnar (C8,T1)

cards.front: What metabolic complication should you monitor for during massive transfusion, and why?
cards.back: Hypocalcemia. pRBCs are stored with citrate as an anticoagulant/preservative. Citrate chelates ionized calcium. With rapid or massive transfusion, citrate load can outpace hepatic metabolism → ionized hypocalcemia → myocardial depression, coagulopathy, tetany. Monitor ionized Ca²⁺ and replace empirically (calcium gluconate or calcium chloride) during massive transfusion protocols.

cards.front: What fluoroscopic views guide placement of the LC-II screw?
cards.back: The LC-II screw runs from the AIIS to the PSIS (anterior inferior iliac spine to posterior superior iliac spine), traversing the iliac wing. Trajectory is confirmed on the obturator outlet view (to profile the screw in the iliac cortical densities, avoiding cranial/caudal breach) and the inlet view down the wing (iliac oblique/inlet) to confirm the screw stays within the wing and does not violate the inner or outer tables. Mnemonic: obturator-inlet, down-the-wing.

cards.front: What surgical approach is preferred for pilon nonunion revision?
cards.back: Posterior approach (posterior to the fibula, between the peroneals and FHL). The anterior soft tissue envelope is typically compromised from the index procedure (anterior or anterolateral ORIF), making repeat anterior dissection hazardous due to scarring and tenuous vascularity. The posterior approach accesses the posterior tibia through virgin tissue planes, allowing debridement, bone grafting, and fixation without disrupting the anterior wound bed.

cards.front: What are the union rates for nail dynamization vs. exchange nailing in tibial nonunion?
cards.back: Nail dynamization (removing interlocking screws to allow axial load): ~50% union rate. Exchange nailing (removal of existing nail, reaming, larger-diameter nail): ~88% union rate. Exchange nailing is the preferred intervention for aseptic tibial nonunion after IMN — it provides mechanical stability, stimulates healing via reaming debris (local growth factors, BMP), and increases nail diameter for better construct stiffness.

cards.front: What are the soft tissue coverage options for open tibial fractures by zone?
cards.back:
Coverage depends on defect location:
- Proximal 1/3 (knee region): Gastrocnemius rotational flap. Blood supply: sural artery (branches of popliteal).
- Middle 1/3: Soleus rotational flap. Blood supply: posterior tibial artery (segmental perforators).
- Distal 1/3: Reverse sural artery rotational flap or free flap (e.g., latissimus dorsi, anterolateral thigh). The distal third has poor local muscle bulk, making local rotational flaps insufficient for large defects.

cards.front: What bone void filler has the highest compressive strength for metaphyseal defects, and how does it compare to allograft?
cards.back: Calcium phosphate cement has the highest compressive strength among bone void fillers and is superior to allograft in resisting subsidence in metaphyseal voids. It is osteoconductive and resorbs slowly. Preferred when structural support is needed (e.g., depressed tibial plateau, distal radius metaphyseal defects). Calcium sulfate resorbs faster but has lower strength. Allograft (cancellous chips) is osteoconductive but compresses more under load.

cards.front: How do you manage an atypical femur fracture (bisphosphonate-associated fracture)?
cards.back: Discontinue bisphosphonates. Initiate anabolic therapy — teriparatide (PTH 1-34) or abaloparatide (PTHrP analog) — to stimulate bone formation and promote healing. Atypical femur fractures occur in the subtrochanteric/diaphyseal region, are associated with long-term bisphosphonate use (suppressed bone turnover), and are characterized by a transverse or short oblique pattern with cortical thickening/beaking on the lateral cortex. Surgical fixation with an IM nail is standard for complete fractures; incomplete fractures with prodromal thigh pain may also warrant prophylactic nailing.

cards.front: What are the risk factors for anterior cortical penetration when using a long femoral nail?
cards.back: Posterior start point, small stature, and low radius of curvature (i.e., a straighter femur relative to the nail's built-in bow). Most femoral nails have an anterior bow of ~1.5–3 m radius; a femur with a tighter bow or a nail inserted through a posterior entry point increases the mismatch between nail and canal geometry, driving the nail tip anteriorly. Short patients tend to have smaller, straighter femora that exacerbate this mismatch. The risk is highest at the diaphyseal-metaphyseal junction of the distal femur.

cards.front: What is the radiation protocol for heterotopic ossification prophylaxis after acetabular fracture ORIF?
cards.back: Single postoperative dose of 700 cGy, administered within 72 hours of surgery. Radiation prophylaxis is an alternative to indomethacin, particularly in patients who cannot tolerate NSAIDs. The extended iliofemoral approach carries the highest HO risk and is the approach most commonly cited in the context of radiation prophylaxis.

cards.front: What is the rate of conversion to THA after acetabular fracture in the elderly, and what is the strongest predictor?
cards.back: ~30% of elderly patients with acetabular fractures ultimately require THA. Non-anatomic reduction is the most powerful independent risk factor for conversion. This reinforces that quality of reduction drives long-term outcomes even in older patients, where the threshold for acute THA (vs. ORIF) is also a consideration.

cards.front: What are the risk factors for post-traumatic OA after posterior wall acetabular fracture? What are NOT risk factors?
cards.back: Risk factors: age >46, impaction of the posterior wall, comminution, femoral head damage (chondral injury), and quality of reduction (inadequate reduction = higher OA risk). NOT independent risk factors: gender, time to reduction, or time to OR. The quality of the final reduction is modifiable and the most surgically actionable predictor.

cards.front: What is subspinous impingement, and why is it a cause of persistent pain after FAI surgery?
cards.back: Subspinous (AIIS) impingement is a distinct FAI variant caused by a prominent anterior inferior iliac spine (AIIS) that abuts the femoral head-neck junction during hip flexion — separate from classic cam or pincer impingement. The AIIS is located just below the ASIS and is the origin of the rectus femoris; a prominent or low-riding AIIS creates extra-articular anterior impingement. It requires specific resection (subspinous decompression) at the time of hip arthroscopy. If missed, patients have persistent anterior hip pain despite adequate cam/pincer correction — alpha angle may be normal postoperatively. On imaging, a large bony prominence at the AIIS below the level of the acetabular rim is the defining finding. Common causes of failed FAI surgery: under-resection of cam (most common), subspinous impingement, capsular deficiency, and unaddressed dysplasia. Boards rule: post-op FAI pain + prominent AIIS on imaging = residual subspinous impingement.

cards.front: What are the return-to-play criteria and contraindications for cervical spine injuries in contact sports, including spear tackler's spine?
cards.back: Spear tackler's spine = developmental cervical stenosis + loss of normal lordosis (straightened or kyphotic) + radiographic evidence of prior trauma + history of head-first contact → **absolute contraindication to contact sports**, regardless of surgical treatment. Surgery (decompression/fusion) addresses the disc herniation but does not correct the underlying stenosis or restore lordosis — the structural risk for catastrophic cord injury persists. Torg-Pavlov ratio: normal >0.80; ratio <0.80 = congenital stenosis (technique-dependent, less reliable in large athletes). Absolute canal stenosis: <10mm. Normal canal diameter: >17mm. Transient quadriplegia (neuropraxia) alone, with full neurologic recovery and normal imaging, may allow return to play after counseling — but NOT when combined with spear tackler's anatomy. Boards rule: spear tackler's spine = disqualify permanently. Transient quad with normal anatomy = may return after shared decision-making.

cards.front: Which meniscal tear pattern most significantly increases tibiofemoral contact pressures, and why?
cards.back: A complete radial tear extending to the meniscocapsular junction — biomechanically equivalent to a posterior root tear and to a total meniscectomy. The circumferential (hoop stress) fibers of the meniscus are interrupted by the radial tear; once the tear reaches the meniscocapsular junction (complete), the meniscus can no longer distribute axial load into hoop tension, and contact pressures increase significantly above the intact state. Partial radial tears (50%, 75%) do NOT significantly increase contact pressures — only complete radial tears do. Horizontal cleavage tears with extrusion and parameniscal cysts cause symptoms but do not disrupt hoop stress as completely. Boards hierarchy for biomechanical impact: complete radial tear = root tear > bucket-handle (displaces but maintains some hoop function) > partial radial > horizontal cleavage. Clinical implication: complete radial tears and root tears should be repaired when possible — they are functionally equivalent to meniscectomy if left untreated.

cards.front: What is the presentation of post-arthroscopic hip capsular deficiency, and how does it differ from FAI?
cards.back: FAI (preoperative): pain with FADIR — flexion, adduction, internal rotation — as the cam/pincer lesion impinges on the acetabular rim. Capsular deficiency (postoperative): pain and apprehension with **hip extension and external rotation** — the opposite direction. The iliofemoral ligament is the strongest hip capsular ligament and the primary restraint to ER and extension; if it is not repaired or is overtensioned/undertensioned after capsulotomy, the patient develops gross instability in extension/ER. Additional findings: positive dial test, loss of recoil with log roll. Capsular deficiency is one of the most common causes of FAI revision surgery (along with under-resection, over-resection, and unaddressed dysplasia). Treatment: capsular repair or reconstruction with autograft or allograft. Boards rule: pain with extension + ER after hip arthroscopy = capsular deficiency, not residual FAI.

cards.front: What are the key features of medial meniscal root tears?
cards.back: Medial meniscal root tears disrupt the posterior root attachment of the medial meniscus, rendering it functionally absent — without the root, the meniscus cannot maintain hoop stress and extrudes medially (>3mm extrusion on MRI = functionally absent meniscus). Consequence: rapid acceleration of tibiofemoral contact pressures and cartilage wear, mimicking a total meniscectomy biomechanically. Demographics: middle-aged to older females (distinct from traumatic tears in young males). Mechanism: loaded knee flexion (e.g., going up stairs, squatting) with a felt "pop" on the medial side. McMurray's sign positive medially. MRI findings: radial tear into the root, vertical defect (ghost sign — absence of meniscus at the root), meniscal extrusion >3mm. Treatment: root repair (transtibial pull-through) if joint space is relatively preserved; TKA if significant arthritis. Repair technique: transtibial suture anchor pull-through — NOT vertical mattress suture (which is for longitudinal body tears). Healing rates up to 96% with repair in appropriately selected patients.

cards.front: How does bone scan distinguish shin splints (medial tibial stress syndrome) from tibial stress fracture?
cards.back: Shin splints: bone scan shows diffuse, longitudinal uptake along the posterior tibial cortex — broad and linear, reflecting periosteal stress reaction over a long segment. Tibial stress fracture: bone scan shows focal, discrete uptake — more localized and intense at the fracture site, positive in all three phases (three-phase bone scan positive in all phases for stress fracture; shin splints may only show delayed-phase uptake). Clinical overlap: both present with activity-related pain, but bone scan pattern distinguishes them. Pain duration after activity is NOT a reliable distinguishing feature — that is too variable. Boards rule: diffuse longitudinal posterior cortex uptake = shin splints; focal/discrete uptake = stress fracture.

cards.front: What is a longitudinal epiphyseal bracket (LEB / delta phalanx / bracket epiphysis), and when should it be treated?
cards.back: LEB (longitudinal epiphyseal bracket) = anomalous C-shaped secondary ossification center that extends longitudinally along the diaphysis instead of transversely — prevents normal transverse bone growth, causing progressive shortening and angular deformity. In the foot: first metatarsal LEB → hallux varus. In the hand: middle phalanx LEB → clinodactyly. Bilateral involvement in ~75%. Treatment: **early surgical excision (physiolysis) before ossification** — ideally in the first year of life. The pre-ossified bracket is cartilaginous and can be resected with fat or PMMA interposition to allow normal growth to resume. Waiting until ossification occurs forfeits the opportunity for growth correction and leaves only angular osteotomy as salvage. Boards trap: the correct answer is early excision before ossification, NOT observation until ossification occurs.

cards.front: What is the PRIMARY biomechanical function of Type II collagen in articular cartilage, and how does it differ from proteoglycans?
cards.back: Type II collagen → **tensile strength**. The collagen fibril network provides structural restraint that limits excessive swelling and prevents failure under tension. In the superficial zone, fibers run parallel to the articular surface and bear ~20% of applied load in tension. Degradation of the collagen network causes surface fibrillation and progressive degeneration. Proteoglycans (aggrecan) → **compressive resistance**. Negatively charged glycosaminoglycan chains generate osmotic swelling pressure by attracting water — this is what resists compressive loads. Boards trap: Type II collagen does NOT resist compression and does NOT distribute force to subchondral bone — that is the proteoglycan/water role. The two components have strictly separate mechanical roles: collagen = tension, proteoglycans = compression.

cards.front: When is NPWT vs. hyperbaric oxygen therapy indicated for chronic diabetic foot ulcers?
cards.back: NPWT (negative pressure wound therapy): indicated when perfusion is adequate (palpable pulses), no active infection, no osteomyelitis, and standard wound care has failed. Promotes granulation tissue, reduces edema, removes exudate, improves local blood flow → faster wound contraction and healing. RCT and meta-analysis evidence supports reduced ulcer size and time to closure vs. moist dressings alone. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy: indicated for ischemic wounds or osteomyelitis refractory to standard treatment — it addresses tissue hypoxia by delivering high-concentration O₂ under pressure. Not the answer when perfusion is intact. Distinguishing feature in the question: palpable pedal pulses = adequate perfusion = NPWT, not HBO. If the question described absent pulses, ischemia, or osteomyelitis → HBO becomes the answer.

cards.front: What is Baxter's nerve, what is its anatomic course, and how does entrapment present?
cards.back: Baxter's nerve = first branch of the lateral plantar nerve. Course: runs deep to the abductor hallucis → then between the quadratus plantae and flexor digitorum brevis → medial to the calcaneus → innervates the abductor digiti quinti. Entrapment occurs between the abductor hallucis fascia and the quadratus plantae, or just anterior to the medial calcaneal tuberosity. Presentation: sharp, burning medial-plantar heel pain, worst at end of day (distinguishes from plantar fasciitis, which is worst with first steps in the morning); abductor digiti quinti atrophy with chronic compression. Treatment: conservative (rest, PT) for 3 months; surgical decompression if refractory. Boards pearl: Baxter's nerve runs deep to the abductor hallucis and FDB, superficial to the quadratus plantae — this anatomic relationship is the testable fact.

cards.front: What is the proven benefit of multidisciplinary team evaluation in myelomeningocele patients undergoing spine surgery?
cards.back: Reduced postoperative complications. Multidisciplinary evaluation (orthopaedics, neurosurgery, urology, rehabilitation, nutrition, social work) optimizes modifiable preoperative risk factors: nutritional status, neurogenic bladder/bowel function, skin integrity (pressure sore risk), and shunt function. In myelomeningocele, postoperative complications disproportionately arise from non-orthopaedic factors — malnutrition, wound breakdown over insensate skin, UTI from unmanaged neurogenic bladder, shunt malfunction. Coordinating these preoperatively reduces infection, wound dehiscence, prolonged hospitalization, and unplanned readmissions. What MDT does NOT do: it does not fundamentally change long-term neurologic function or deformity progression. Boards rule: when asked about the benefit of multidisciplinary care in medically complex pediatric patients, the answer is perioperative complication reduction, not long-term deformity or psychosocial outcomes.

cards.front: What are the operative indications for tarsal navicular fractures?
cards.back: Nondisplaced fractures and stress reactions → NWB cast immobilization 6–8 weeks. Operative indications: (1) avulsion fractures involving >25% of the articular surface; (2) tuberosity fractures with >5mm diastasis or a large intra-articular fragment; (3) displaced or intra-articular body fractures. A displaced navicular body fracture = ORIF with lag screw fixation — displacement alone is sufficient indication regardless of the clinical exam finding. The navicular has a tenuous blood supply (watershed zone at the central third), making anatomic reduction and stable fixation critical to prevent AVN and nonunion. For navicular stress fractures (NSF): operative management has a 97.9% success rate vs 78.1% for conservative, with lower refracture rates — surgery is favored in athletes.

cards.front: What excision margins are required for common soft tissue tumors, and what is the oncologic behavior of solitary fibrous tumor?
cards.back: Excision margin framework: Intralesional (within pseudocapsule) → highest recurrence, select benign lesions only. Marginal (along reactive zone/capsule) → benign or low-risk tumors: intramuscular myxoma, lipoma, ganglion. Wide (cuff of normal tissue) → most malignant sarcomas and locally aggressive tumors: myxofibrosarcoma, solitary fibrous tumor, desmoid tumor (desmoid is locally aggressive/infiltrative — wide excision, high recurrence). Radical (entire anatomic compartment) → highly aggressive tumors when limb salvage margins cannot be achieved. Solitary fibrous tumor: classified as a fibroblastic tumor of intermediate malignant potential — wide excision is required because it has unpredictable biologic behavior, a risk of local recurrence, and rare metastatic potential despite often appearing well-circumscribed. Intramuscular myxoma: benign, hypocellular myxoid stroma, no mitotic activity, no metastatic potential → marginal excision is curative with very low recurrence.

cards.front: What are the nonunion risk factors for Type II odontoid fractures, and when is C1-C2 posterior fusion preferred over anterior odontoid screw fixation?
cards.back: Type II odontoid fractures (through the waist) are in a watershed zone with high nonunion risk. Risk factors for nonunion: age >65, delayed surgical intervention (>7 days → 48-fold higher nonunion risk), active smoking, osteoporosis, displacement, and angulation ≥11°. Treatment is stratified by nonunion risk: low risk → rigid collar; elevated risk → surgical fixation. Surgical options: anterior odontoid screw fixation (preserves C1-C2 rotation, union ~77–84%) vs. posterior C1-C2 fusion (higher union rates, sacrifices ~50% of cervical rotation). Posterior C1-C2 fusion is preferred in elderly patients with high nonunion risk — specifically when emphysema/barrel chest precludes safe anterior approach, delayed presentation, or when multiple risk factors are present. Boards rule: elderly + smoker + delayed presentation + emphysema = high nonunion risk = C1-C2 posterior fusion, NOT anterior odontoid screw. Displacement alone does not determine surgical approach — the overall risk profile does.

cards.front: What gait parameter shows the greatest difference between TAA and ankle arthrodesis during level walking?
cards.back: Peak plantarflexion power generation. TAA preserves sagittal plane ankle motion and allows the triceps surae to generate push-off power during terminal stance — this is significantly greater in TAA vs. arthrodesis patients. TAA provides ~43% greater sagittal ankle ROM (21.1° vs 14.7°) and significantly greater peak plantarflexion power generation. Arthrodesis patients compensate with increased hip ROM and altered ground reaction forces to make up for the lost ankle push-off. Walking speed and cadence are more similar between the two groups — both procedures result in comparable patient-reported outcomes despite the biomechanical differences. Boards rule: TAA vs. arthrodesis = plantarflexion power generation is the key differentiator, not walking speed.

cards.front: How is tibialis anterior tendon rupture managed based on acuity and retraction?
cards.back: Acute rupture (<6 weeks), sufficient tendon length/quality: primary end-to-end repair. Chronic rupture (>6 weeks) OR significant retraction OR poor tendon quality: autograft reconstruction (semitendinosus, gracilis). The retraction in this case (4 cm) and chronicity (months of symptoms) both preclude primary repair — the tendon ends cannot be approximated without tension. EHL transfer is an option for chronic ruptures but autograft reconstruction provides superior outcomes when tendon defect is significant. Conservative management (AFO) reserved for elderly, low-demand patients willing to accept persistent dorsiflexion weakness. Boards framework: acute + good tissue = repair; chronic + retraction/degeneration = autograft reconstruction.

cards.front: When do intraoperative fractures most commonly occur during TKA, and what is the most common fracture site?
cards.back: Most common fracture site: medial femoral condyle. Most common stage: bony preparation (39%), followed by trialing (33%). Bony preparation carries the highest risk because of technical errors associated with incomplete intercondylar notch cuts, particularly when using posterior-stabilized (PS) prostheses — incomplete notch cuts create a stress riser on the medial condyle. Risk factors: osteoporosis, female sex (80% of cases), advanced age, RA, steroid use, metabolic bone disease, and use of PS prostheses. Overall intraoperative fracture rate in TKA is low (<2%), but 21% of intraoperative fractures ultimately require revision surgery. Boards rule: medial femoral condyle fracture during TKA = bony preparation stage, not component implantation.

cards.front: What distinguishes telangiectatic osteosarcoma from conventional osteosarcoma in terms of imaging, histology, chemosensitivity, and prognosis?
cards.back: Telangiectatic osteosarcoma is a high-grade osteosarcoma subtype (4–11% of cases) with several distinguishing features: Imaging: purely lytic, expansile lesion with cortical destruction and a wide zone of transition — can mimic ABC. MRI shows multiple fluid-fluid levels (hemorrhagic cystic spaces) — same as ABC. CT is better than MRI for detecting subtle matrix mineralization that distinguishes it from ABC. Histology: dilated blood-filled cystic spaces lined by septa containing high-grade atypical/anaplastic stromal cells with fine lace-like osteoid — the malignant cells are in the septa, not the cyst fluid. Chemosensitivity: superior to conventional osteosarcoma — higher rates of good histologic response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Prognosis: survival is comparable to conventional osteosarcoma — histologic response to chemotherapy is the most important prognostic factor for both subtypes. Pathologic fracture does NOT preclude limb salvage. Boards trap: telangiectatic OSA responds BETTER to chemo than conventional OSA, but survival is equivalent — the key prognostic factor is histologic response, not the subtype itself.

cards.front: Are hemivertebrae a failure of formation or segmentation, and how does segmentation status affect progression risk?
cards.back: Hemivertebrae are a **failure of formation** — complete failure of one half of a vertebra to develop, producing a partial vertebra that creates an asymmetric growth force. This is distinct from failures of **segmentation** (unilateral or bilateral bars, where adjacent vertebrae are tethered together). Segmentation status of a hemivertebra determines progression risk: **fully segmented** hemivertebrae (normal disc spaces above AND below) have unrestricted growth potential on the affected side → highest risk of progression. **Non-segmented or incarcerated** hemivertebrae (fused to adjacent vertebrae above and below) have limited independent growth → lower progression risk. Boards rule: hemivertebra = formation failure; fully segmented = highest progression risk; surgical intervention delayed until documented progression after 2–3 years of age.

cards.front: What is the primary biomechanical consequence of lateral column shortening from cuboid malunion?
cards.back: Disruption of the midtarsal joint locking mechanism, impairing the rigid lever arm needed for effective push-off. The cuboid is the keystone of the lateral column. During gait, the midtarsal joint (Chopart's joint — talonavicular + calcaneocuboid) normally locks in supination at push-off, converting the foot into a rigid lever arm that allows the triceps surae to generate propulsive force. Lateral column shortening disrupts the geometric relationship required for this locking, producing a persistently mobile midfoot that cannot generate an effective push-off — explaining the clinical complaint of midfoot pain and push-off weakness. The lateral column length is the critical surgical restoration target in cuboid fracture malunion.

cards.front: What vascular pattern is most commonly associated with congenital talipes equinovarus (clubfoot)?
cards.back: Hypoplastic or absent anterior tibial artery with diminished/absent dorsalis pedis flow, and the posterior tibial artery becomes the dominant inflow to the foot. This is the opposite of what you might intuitively expect — the anterior tibial and dorsalis pedis are deficient, not hypertrophic. Incidence: dorsalis pedis most frequently absent (~21.5%), anterior tibial artery most frequently hypoplastic (~18.3%); 61% of feet have dominant posterior tibial supply. This vascular pattern is present regardless of clubfoot etiology (idiopathic vs. syndromic). Clinical relevance: routine Doppler ultrasound before operative intervention is recommended, as anterior tibial/dorsalis pedis deficiency affects surgical planning and wound healing risk.

cards.front: What are the opponensplasty options for thumb opposition reconstruction, and what makes the EIP (Burkhalter) transfer advantageous?
cards.back: Opponensplasty restores the three components of thumb opposition lost with median nerve palsy: palmar abduction, flexion, and pronation. Common donor tendons: EIP (extensor indicis proprius), FDS ring finger, palmaris longus, abductor digiti minimi (Huber). The **Burkhalter EIP transfer** (extensor indicis proprius) is advantageous because: (1) restores all three components of opposition — abduction, flexion, AND pronation; (2) minimal donor site morbidity — index finger maintains independent extension through the EDC; (3) adequate excursion and favorable line of pull. The FDS ring finger transfer also restores opposition effectively but sacrifices ring finger flexion — a real functional trade-off, not a minor one. Opponensplasty techniques are NOT equivalent — donor selection affects which components of opposition are restored and what morbidity is created. EIP is the boards-favored answer for comprehensive opposition with least morbidity.

cards.front: What is the bilaminar insertion anatomy of the pectoralis major, and which fibers form the posterior layer?
cards.back: The pectoralis major inserts on the proximal humerus just lateral to the bicipital groove as a bilaminar tendon with anterior and posterior layers. The **anterior layer** receives contributions from the clavicular head and the superior three to five sternal segments. The **posterior layer** receives contributions exclusively from the **inferior two to three sternal segments**. The inferior sternal fibers have the shortest fiber bundle length and the largest lateral pennation angle, placing them at a mechanical disadvantage during bench press — predisposing the posterior layer to fail first in a caudal-to-cranial pattern. Surgical implication: a tear with an intact anterior layer but complete posterior layer avulsion = inferior sternocostal fibers torn. The clavicular head contributes to the anterior layer only — its integrity can mask a complete sternal head tear on exam.

cards.front: Which fixation method is least expensive for PIP joint arthrodesis in hammertoe correction, and why?
cards.back: K-wire (Kirschner wire) fixation. K-wires are inexpensive, widely available, and require no proprietary instrumentation. Although K-wires require removal, this is done in clinic — not the OR — so no additional surgical facility or anesthesia cost is incurred. Total cost of care still strongly favors K-wires over headless compression screws, bioabsorbable implants, shape-memory alloy devices, or titanium intramedullary devices, all of which require proprietary systems and increase implant cost without consistently superior union rates or functional outcomes.

cards.front: What ligaments does the Broström-Gould reconstruction address, and what are the clinical exam findings that indicate each?
cards.back: The Broström-Gould reconstructs the **ATFL** (anterior talofibular ligament) and **CFL** (calcaneofibular ligament) — the two primary lateral ankle stabilizers. Clinical correlation: anterior drawer test (ankle in plantarflexion) evaluates ATFL laxity; talar tilt test (ankle dorsiflexed) evaluates CFL. Excessive inversion >15° on talar tilt = both ATFL and CFL injured. The PTFL (posterior talofibular ligament) is the strongest lateral ligament and is injured only in complete dislocations — it is not addressed by Broström-Gould. Injury sequence: ATFL first (most common, plantarflexion-inversion), then CFL (combined inversion), then PTFL (severe).

cards.front: Why is routine ECG screening not recommended for asymptomatic athletes at preparticipation exams?
cards.back: Low disease prevalence drives a high false-positive rate, making it cost-ineffective — not because ECG has poor sensitivity or specificity. ECG actually has reasonably good sensitivity (~70%) and specificity (~84%) for cardiac conditions causing sudden cardiac death (SCD). The problem is that SCD-related conditions are rare in athletes; when prevalence is very low, even a highly specific test generates far more false positives than true positives (Bayes' theorem). The downstream workup of false positives accounts for up to 98% of total screening costs. Boards rule: when asked why routine ECG screening is not recommended, the answer is low disease prevalence + false-positive burden — NOT poor test performance. Standard history and physical exam remain the mainstay unless red flags are present.

cards.front: What is the proven advantage of robotic-arm-assisted TKA (RATKA) over conventional jig-based TKA?
cards.back: Decreased iatrogenic periarticular soft-tissue injury. The robotic system uses a predefined active cutting field with automatic switch-off if the saw blade veers outside the intended zone — this protects the collateral ligaments, PCL, and extensor mechanism during bone preparation. Additional demonstrated advantages: improved component positioning accuracy and reduced bone trauma. What RATKA does NOT have proven evidence for: decreased long-term implant revision rate, decreased infection rate, or decreased operative time (operative time is typically longer with robotic systems). Boards rule: when asked about robotic TKA advantages, the answer is soft-tissue protection and component positioning accuracy — NOT long-term survivorship, which requires data not yet available.

cards.front: What structure serves as the primary scaffold for new myofiber formation during skeletal muscle healing?
cards.back: The basal lamina. After muscle injury, macrophages phagocytose the necrotic myofiber contents but selectively spare the basal lamina — leaving behind preserved cylindrical tubes. These basal lamina cylinders guide satellite cell migration and ensure regenerating myofibers form in proper alignment. The basal lamina is the critical scaffold for muscle regeneration; without it, satellite cells cannot organize properly, increasing the risk of disorganized fibrosis. Boards trap: the perimysium surrounds muscle fascicles but is not the scaffold for individual myofiber regeneration — that role belongs to the basal lamina surrounding each myofiber.

cards.front: What are the clinical features of pediatric trigger thumb, and where is Notta's node located?
cards.back: Pediatric trigger thumb = FPL tendon thickening that prevents normal gliding through the A1 pulley → IP joint locked in flexion, unable to actively or passively extend. Primary finding: **fixed flexion deformity at the thumb IP joint** — inability to extend is the defining presentation. Notta's node (thickening of the FPL tendon) is palpable at the **volar aspect of the MCP joint** (proximal to the A1 pulley), NOT distal to the IP joint. Bilateral in ~25%. Sensation is preserved. Natural history: ~32% resolve spontaneously by 5 years; each degree of IP flexion contracture reduces spontaneous resolution by 3%; >30° contracture warrants early surgical consideration. Treatment: extension splinting (nonoperative) or A1 pulley release.

cards.front: What connective tissue structure must be intact to allow full peripheral nerve recovery after axonal disruption (Wallerian degeneration)?
cards.back:
The endoneurium. Fibrillations and positive sharp waves on EMG confirm axonal disruption and Wallerian degeneration — this is at minimum a Sunderland 2nd-degree injury. Full recovery is still possible in 2nd-degree injuries because the endoneurium is intact, providing the connective tissue scaffold for regenerating axons to follow their original course to target end organs. Sunderland classification and connective tissue disruption:
- **1st degree**: no axonal disruption, all connective tissue intact → full recovery (neurapraxia/demyelination)
- **2nd degree**: axon disrupted, **endoneurium intact** → full recovery (axonal regeneration guided by intact tube)
- **3rd degree**: axon + endoneurium disrupted, perineurium intact → variable/incomplete recovery (regenerating axons may misguide)
- **4th degree**: axon + endoneurium + perineurium disrupted, epineurium intact → poor spontaneous recovery, usually requires surgery
- **5th degree**: complete transection → no recovery without surgical repair

Boards trap: epineurium is the outermost sheath and the last to fail — its integrity does NOT guarantee good recovery. Endoneurium is the critical structure for axonal guidance and full regeneration.

cards.front: What are the histologic and physiologic findings within a bone marrow lesion (BML) compared to normal bone?
cards.back: BMLs are characterized by elevated intraosseous pressure (~97% higher than normal) and **reduced** perfusion — the opposite of increased perfusion. The resulting focal ischemia impairs bone metabolism, producing: irregular and disorganized bone mineralization pattern, lower mean matrix mineralization, and elevated osteoid volume. On MRI: hyperintense on T2/PD fat-suppressed sequences (bone marrow edema pattern). Boards trap: BMLs cause REDUCED subchondral perfusion (venous congestion, compartment-syndrome-like physiology) — not increased. Increased perfusion would imply hyperemia; the pathology here is ischemia. Treatment: NSAIDs, activity modification, bisphosphonates; surgical options include core decompression, subchondroplasty, or arthroplasty.

cards.front: What are the orthopaedic and systemic features of Neurofibromatosis Type 1 (NF1)?
cards.back: NF1 = mutation in the neurofibromin gene on chromosome 17 → neurofibromin normally acts as a tumor suppressor by negatively regulating the Ras signaling pathway → loss of function → unchecked Ras activity → tumor formation. Systemic features: café-au-lait spots (≥6, >5 mm prepubertal/>15 mm postpubertal), axillary/inguinal freckling, neurofibromas, Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas — NOT cataracts), optic gliomas, hemihypertrophy. Orthopaedic features: anterolateral tibial bowing (congenital pseudarthrosis of the tibia), dystrophic scoliosis (short, sharp, segmented curve with kyphosis, vertebral scalloping, rib penciling, enlarged foramina), hemihypertrophy, and plexiform neurofibromas. Boards trap: Lisch nodules and optic gliomas are NF1 eye findings — NOT cataracts (cataracts = NF2 or other). Ectopic lens = Marfan's (superior) or homocystinuria (inferior). NF2 = chromosome 22, bilateral acoustic neuromas, cataracts, meningiomas — completely different gene and phenotype.

cards.front: When should a tibial tubercle osteotomy (TTO) be added to MPFL reconstruction for recurrent patellar instability?
cards.back:
When significant bony risk factors for recurrent instability are present, MPFL alone is insufficient — concomitant osteotomy is required. Bony risk factors that drive TTO:
- **Patella alta** (Caton-Deschamps >1.3, Insall-Salvati >1.2): distalizing TTO. Alta dramatically increases recurrence risk after soft tissue reconstruction alone, even if TT-TG is borderline normal.
- **Elevated TT-TG distance** (>20 mm): anteromedialization TTO (Fulkerson). TT-TG 15–20 mm is a gray zone; >20 mm is a clear indication.
- **Trochlear dysplasia** (Dejour B/D): may require trochleoplasty in addition to or instead of TTO.
- **Femoral anteversion** (>20° abnormal): femoral de-rotational osteotomy — but 12° is normal, so this is not the answer in a patient with normal anteversion.

In this case: Caton-Deschamps ~1.8 (significant alta) + TT-TG 19 mm (borderline) + minimal trochlear dysplasia + normal femoral anteversion → the patella alta is the operative bony abnormality → distalizing TTO with MPFL reconstruction. The TT-TG alone does not trigger TTO here, but the alta does.

cards.front: What is the definitive treatment for a recurrent proximal tibiofibular joint ganglion cyst?
cards.back: Proximal fibula hemiresection (proximal fibulectomy) with excision of the cyst. The cyst arises from the proximal tibiofibular joint via an articular connection — simple excision or aspiration leaves the synovial fluid source intact, producing high recurrence rates (aspiration 82%, simple excision 27%). Hemiresection eliminates the joint source and achieves 8% recurrence; arthrodesis achieves 0% but sacrifices joint motion. These cysts commonly compress the common peroneal nerve (foot drop in up to 28%). Boards rule: recurrent proximal tibiofibular joint ganglion = proximal fibulectomy, not repeat aspiration, not simple excision alone.

cards.front: In a pediatric Monteggia-equivalent injury (radial head dislocation with ulnar plastic deformation/bowing), what is the most important factor determining surgical vs. closed treatment?
cards.back: Time elapsed between injury and definitive treatment. Each day of delay increases the odds of requiring surgical intervention by 12.6%. At 14 days (as in this case), the likelihood of successful closed reduction is substantially reduced and surgery is usually required. Missed diagnosis occurs in up to 72% of cases initially — these children are often sent home with a "no fracture" read. The critical rule: always draw the radiocapitellar line on any pediatric elbow film to confirm the radial head points to the capitellum. Early treatment (closed reduction and casting) achieves excellent outcomes regardless of fracture pattern; delay converts a simple closed case into a complex reconstruction.

cards.front: What are the risk factors for concussion, and how does sex affect concussion risk in contact sports?
cards.back: The most significant risk factor for concussion is a prior history of concussion. Additional risk factors: age <18, contact sports (football, soccer, basketball), and female sex. In sports with physical contact, female athletes are at higher risk than male athletes — this is the boards-testable fact. In soccer specifically, defenders and midfielders have the highest incidence; goalies have the highest *relative* risk after adjusting for exposure. Ranking framework: prior concussion > no prior concussion, and female > male when prior concussion history is equal. Therefore: female with prior concussion > male with prior concussion > male without prior concussion.

cards.front: On the obturator oblique (iliac teardrop) view for S2 alar-iliac screw placement, what structure forms the floor of the corridor?
cards.back: The sciatic notch. The obturator oblique view (also called the outlet obturator oblique or iliac teardrop view) visualizes a safe corridor of bone from the PSIS to the AIIS — the same corridor used for S2 alar-iliac screws in spinopelvic fixation and LC-2 screws in acetabular surgery. The floor of this corridor on the fluoroscopic image is the sciatic notch. Violation of the floor = screw penetrates the sciatic notch → risk of sciatic nerve injury. This is distinct from the roof (lateral cortex of the iliac wing). Boards trap: the sciatic notch is the floor, not a medial or lateral wall structure — it sits at the inferior border of the corridor.

cards.front: What is the Levine and Edwards classification of Hangman's fractures, and what is the treatment for each type?
cards.back:
Hangman's fracture = bilateral C2 pars interarticularis fractures (traumatic spondylolisthesis of the axis). Classified by the Levine and Edwards system:
- **Type 1**: extension-axial load mechanism → non-angulated, displacement <3 mm → rigid cervical collar. Neurologically intact in the vast majority (wide canal at C2).
- **Type 2**: extension/axial load followed by flexion and compression → >3 mm horizontal displacement AND angulation of the proximal fragment → halo immobilization (or surgery if unstable).
- **Type 2A**: flexion/extension mechanism → horizontal fracture lines, minimal translation but significant angulation → halo immobilization. Key distinction from Type 2: do NOT apply traction (risks distraction and cord injury).
- **Type 3**: flexion-distraction → facet dislocation at C2–C3 → surgical stabilization required.

Boards trap: Type 1 mechanism is extension-axial load (not flexion-distraction), and treatment is a rigid collar (not halo or surgery).

cards.front: What is the mechanism, toxicity profile, and key clinical pearls for glycopeptides?
cards.back: Prototype: vancomycin. Mechanism: inhibits cell wall synthesis by binding D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of peptidoglycan precursors → blocks transpeptidation. Drug of choice for MRSA. Toxicities: nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity (both potentiated by concurrent aminoglycosides). Red man syndrome: not a true allergy — direct mast cell degranulation from rapid infusion → flushing, erythema, pruritus of face/neck/chest. Treatment: slow the infusion rate ± antihistamines. Not IgE-mediated; patient is not penicillin-cross-reactive. Trough monitoring required to avoid toxicity and ensure efficacy.

cards.front: Which antibiotics inhibit protein synthesis, and which ribosomal subunit does each target?
cards.back:
Mnemonic — "Lettuce Mayo Cheese And Tomato" (LMCAT): Linezolid, Macrolides, Clindamycin, Aminoglycosides, Tetracyclines.
- 50S inhibitors: Linezolid (prevents 70S initiation complex formation), Macrolides, Clindamycin.
- 30S inhibitors: Aminoglycosides (bactericidal), Tetracyclines (bacteriostatic).
Note: Linezolid is classified as a 50S inhibitor — it binds the 50S subunit to block assembly of the 70S initiation complex, not the 70S ribosome directly.
Orthopaedic relevance: Clindamycin has the best bone penetration of any antibiotic — first-line oral option for osteomyelitis and septic arthritis when the organism is susceptible. Linezolid also has good bone penetration and is used for MRSA infections when vancomycin cannot be used.

cards.front: What enzyme is deficient in each of these autosomal recessive conditions, and what is the key orthopaedic feature?
cards.back:
- **Diastrophic dysplasia**: sulfate transporter defect (SLC26A2/DTDST) → impaired proteoglycan sulfation → severe short-limbed dwarfism, hitchhiker thumb, cauliflower ear.
- **Hurler's (MPS I)**: alpha-L-iduronidase → accumulates heparan + dermatan sulfate; corneal clouding, kyphosis, claw hand.
- **Sanfilippo (MPS III)**: heparan sulfamidase (type A) or other enzymes by subtype → heparan sulfate accumulation; severe neurologic involvement, milder skeletal disease.
- **Morquio (MPS IV)**: galactose-6-sulfatase (type A) or beta-galactosidase (type B) → keratan sulfate accumulation; odontoid hypoplasia → atlantoaxial instability (screen before anesthesia/surgery).
- **Gaucher's**: glucocerebrosidase → glucocerebroside in macrophages; Erlenmeyer flask femur, avascular necrosis, bone crises. Most common lysosomal storage disease.
- **Osteopetrosis**: carbonic anhydrase II deficiency → osteoclasts cannot acidify resorption lacuna → bone not resorbed → "marble bone," fractures despite dense bone, obliterated marrow space.
- **Homocystinuria**: cystathionine beta-synthase → homocysteine accumulation; Marfanoid habitus but lens dislocates INFERIORLY (vs. superiorly in Marfan's), intellectual disability, thromboembolism risk.

cards.front: What is the general inheritance pattern for structural protein defects vs. enzymatic defects?
cards.back: Structural protein defects → typically autosomal dominant. One defective allele produces abnormal protein that disrupts the normal protein's function (dominant negative effect). Examples: osteogenesis imperfecta (Type I collagen defect), Marfan syndrome (fibrillin), achondroplasia (FGFR3). Enzymatic defects → typically autosomal recessive. One functional allele is usually sufficient for adequate enzyme activity (haploinsufficiency threshold not reached). Examples: lysosomal storage diseases (Gaucher's, Hurler's), most mucopolysaccharidoses, phenylketonuria. Exception: Hunter's syndrome (MPS II) is X-linked recessive — iduronate-2-sulfatase deficiency. Distinguished from Hurler's (MPS I, AR, alpha-L-iduronidase deficiency) by absence of corneal clouding and X-linked inheritance.

cards.front: Which antibiotics inhibit nucleic acid synthesis, and by what mechanism?
cards.back:
Mnemonic — "Quit Making RNA": Quinolones, Metronidazole, Rifampin.
- Quinolones (fluoroquinolones): inhibit DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV → prevent DNA supercoiling/replication.
- Metronidazole: generates free radicals that directly damage DNA → bactericidal; active against anaerobes and protozoa.
- Rifampin: inhibits bacterial RNA polymerase → blocks transcription. Used for TB, Staph coverage, and surgical prophylaxis in penicillin-allergic patients.

cards.front: What are the key features of fibrous dysplasia — pathogenesis, histology, deformity, and associated syndromes?
cards.back:
Pathogenesis: activating mutation of GNAS1 (encodes Gs-alpha protein) → constitutive G-protein signaling → elevated cAMP → abnormal osteoblast differentiation → fibrous replacement of bone. Mutation is post-zygotic → mosaicism → extent of disease depends on timing of mutation in development (explains monostotic vs. polyostotic forms). Histology: "alphabet soup" or "Chinese letters" — irregular curved trabeculae of woven bone in fibrous stroma, NO osteoblastic rimming. Key histologic differentiator: osteofibrous dysplasia HAS rimming osteoblasts around trabeculae; fibrous dysplasia does NOT. (Ossifying fibroma also has rimming osteoblasts — similar to osteofibrous dysplasia.) Deformity: shepherd's crook — progressive varus of proximal femur from weakened bone under load. Associated syndromes:
- McCune-Albright: polyostotic fibrous dysplasia + café-au-lait spots (irregular "coast of Maine" borders) + precocious puberty ± other endocrinopathies (same GNAS1 mutation).
- Mazabraud syndrome: polyostotic fibrous dysplasia + intramuscular myxomas.

cards.front: What are the four main molecular drivers of skeletal dysplasia and what conditions does each cause?
cards.back:
- **FGFR3** (fibroblast growth factor receptor 3): gain-of-function mutations inhibit chondrocyte proliferation in the **proliferative zone** of the physis → deficient endochondral ossification → achondroplasia (most common skeletal dysplasia, AD), hypochondroplasia, thanatophoric dysplasia (lethal). Rhizomelic shortening (proximal > distal limb).
- **COMP** (cartilage oligomeric matrix protein): structural cartilage matrix protein. Pseudoachondroplasia (COMP) = spine involved; classic deformity = windswept knees (valgus one side, varus other); diagnosed by electron microscopy showing lamellar/tubular COMP deposits in the RER of chondrocytes. MED (COMP or COL9/Type IX collagen) = no spine involvement, epiphyses and joints only. Key differentiator: spine affected → pseudoachondroplasia; spine spared → MED.
- **Collagen**: Type II mutations → spondyloepiphyseal dysplasia (SED), Kniest dysplasia, Stickler syndrome. Type X mutations → Schmid metaphyseal chondrodysplasia.
- **PTHrP / PTH1R**: PTHrP regulates growth plate chondrocyte differentiation via its receptor PTH1R. Activating PTH1R mutation → Jansen's metaphyseal chondrodysplasia. Inactivating mutation → Blomstrand chondrodysplasia.

cards.front: What is the embryonic origin of bone, and what are the key transcription factors driving MSC lineage commitment?
cards.back:
Most bone derives from mesoderm (paraxial mesoderm → axial skeleton; lateral plate mesoderm → appendicular skeleton). Exception: craniofacial bones derive from neural crest cells (ectoderm-derived). MSC lineage commitment:
- RUNX2 + Osterix (OSX/SP7) → osteoblast
- SOX-9 (+ Pax-1 for vertebral/IVD) → chondrocyte
- PPARγ → adipocyte
Clinical relevance: corticosteroids upregulate PPARγ, shifting MSCs toward adipogenesis at the expense of osteoblasts — contributing to steroid-induced osteonecrosis and osteoporosis.

cards.front: Describe the zones of articular cartilage from deep to superficial, including composition and cell characteristics
cards.back:
From deep to superficial:
1. Calcified zone — anchors cartilage to subchondral bone; Type X collagen; tidemark separates it from the deep zone.
2. Deep (radial) zone — ~30% of thickness; collagen fibers perpendicular to the joint surface; contains the tidemark; Type X collagen; chondrocytes in vertical columns.
3. Middle (transitional) zone — collagen fibers oblique/random; highest proteoglycan concentration; best suited to resist compressive forces; rounded chondrocytes.
4. Superficial (tangential) zone — collagen fibers parallel to surface; highest water content; highest collagen concentration; lowest proteoglycan content; flattened chondrocytes; only zone with progenitor/stem cells; resists shear.

cards.front: What are the key collagen types in cartilage and bone, and where is each found?
cards.back: In cartilage: Type II is the dominant structural collagen of hyaline/articular cartilage — provides tensile strength. Type X is produced by hypertrophic chondrocytes and found in three specific locations: (1) the calcified cartilage zone (tidemark) of articular cartilage, (2) the hypertrophic zone of the growth plate, and (3) hypertrophic chondrocytes of fracture callus. All three represent sites of endochondral ossification or calcification. In bone and dense connective tissue: Type I is the most abundant collagen in the body — found in bone, tendons, ligaments, skin, and fibrocartilage (meniscus). Mnemonic: Type I = bone (1 bone), Type II = cartilage (2 c's in cartilage), Type X = calcified/hypertrophic zone only.

cards.front: Describe collagen structure, its synthesis requirements, and mechanical properties
cards.back: Collagen chains are built from repeating Gly-X-Y sequences where X is proline and Y is hydroxyproline (or hydroxylysine). Proline and lysine are hydroxylated post-translationally by prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase — both require vitamin C (ascorbic acid) as cofactor. Three alpha chains wind into a triple helix, producing a stable structure with high tensile strength and stiffness. Cross-linking between fibrils (via lysyl oxidase, copper-dependent) further increases tensile strength. Vitamin C deficiency → scurvy → impaired hydroxylation → defective collagen → poor wound healing. Copper deficiency → impaired lysyl oxidase → defective cross-linking → weak collagen.

cards.front: Describe the elastic zone of the stress-strain curve, and rank common orthopaedic materials by stiffness (Young's modulus)
cards.back:
The elastic zone is the initial linear portion of the stress-strain curve. No bonds are broken; material returns to original shape when stress is removed. The slope = Young's modulus of elasticity (stiffness). Stiffness ranking from highest (stiffest) to lowest (most compliant):
1. Ceramic
2. Cobalt chrome
3. Stainless steel
4. Titanium
5. Cortical bone
6. PMMA
7. Cancellous bone
8. Tendon
9. Cartilage
Material properties from the stress-strain curve: Elastic zone (linear portion) → defines stiffness (slope = Young's modulus) and elasticity (returns to original shape). Plastic zone → defines brittleness vs. ductility. Brittle material: minimal or absent plastic zone — fractures near the yield point with little deformation (e.g., ceramics, cortical bone in torsion). Ductile material: long plastic zone — deforms significantly before fracturing (e.g., CoCr, stainless steel). Clinical relevance: ceramic femoral heads are brittle → catastrophic fracture risk if dropped. Metal implants are ductile → bend before breaking.
Fatigue failure: repeated cyclic loading causes failure at a stress level below the material's ultimate strength. The greater the applied stress, the fewer cycles required to fail (S-N curve: stress vs. number of cycles to failure). Endurance limit: some materials (e.g., stainless steel) have a threshold stress below which they will never fatigue fail regardless of cycle count. Titanium and cortical bone have NO true endurance limit — given enough cycles, they will fail at any stress. Clinical relevance: stress fractures are fatigue failure of bone; implant breakage (e.g., rod fracture in spine, nail failure) is fatigue failure of metal — both require repeated sub-ultimate loading.
Plastic zone and failure: beyond the yield point, the curve becomes nonlinear — the plastic region. Bonds are being broken. Material will not return to original shape when unloaded (permanent deformation). Ultimate strength = the peak of the curve = maximum stress the material can withstand before failure begins. Important: ultimate strength is NOT the breaking point. After ultimate strength, stress actually decreases as strain continues to increase — the curve descends to the fracture point. This descending portion reflects necking/microstructural failure before final fracture.
Clinical relevance: stress shielding occurs when an implant is much stiffer than bone (e.g., CoCr plate) — the implant carries most of the load, bone sees less stress, remodels and resorbs (Wolff's law). Titanium's closer modulus to bone reduces stress shielding vs. CoCr/SS.

cards.front: Define stress and strain, and state their relationship
cards.back: Stress = force divided by the cross-sectional area over which it acts (σ = F/A). Units: Pascals (Pa) or N/m². Strain = deformation divided by original length (ε = ΔL/L) — dimensionless. Relationship: stress and strain are related by the elastic modulus (Young's modulus): E = σ/ε. Stiffer materials have higher Young's modulus (require more stress to produce a given strain).

cards.front: What are the three key biomechanical properties of articular cartilage?
cards.back:
- Anisotropic: mechanical properties vary depending on the direction of applied force (due to the zonal orientation of collagen fibers — the split-line pattern). Contrast with isotropic = same in all directions.
- Biphasic: behaves as both a solid phase (collagen/proteoglycan matrix) and a liquid phase (~80% interstitial water). Under load, fluid pressurization within the matrix bears most of the load acutely; over time fluid exudes and the solid matrix bears more. Basis of the Mow biphasic model.
- Viscoelastic: time-dependent mechanical behavior. Two manifestations:
  - Creep: load held constant → deformation increases over time ("material creeps away"). Example: disc height loss throughout the day under constant body weight.
  - Stress relaxation: deformation held constant → stress (force) decreases over time ("material relaxes when pinned"). Example: serial clubfoot casting — soft tissues relax under fixed correction, allowing the next cast to advance further.
  - Both arise from fluid redistributing through the matrix over time. Cartilage is stiffer at higher loading rates.

cards.front: What drives osteoclast activation, and how is it regulated pharmacologically?
cards.back: Osteoclasts resorb bone via the RANK/RANKL axis. Osteoblasts and stromal cells express RANKL → binds RANK receptor on both osteoclast precursors (driving differentiation) and mature osteoclasts (activating resorption). RANK is expressed at both stages. Regulation: osteoprotegerin (OPG) is a decoy receptor produced by osteoblasts that sequesters RANKL and prevents RANK binding → inhibits osteoclast formation. OPG is the body's endogenous equivalent of denosumab. Denosumab (Prolia/Xgeva) is a monoclonal antibody against RANKL — same mechanism as OPG. Clinical uses: osteoporosis (Prolia), bone metastases and giant cell tumor (Xgeva). GCT stromal cells overexpress RANKL, driving the osteoclast-rich giant cell population — denosumab shrinks GCTs by cutting off that signal. Osteoclast inhibitors summary: OPG/denosumab → block RANKL → prevent osteoclast formation. Calcitonin (from thyroid C cells) → directly inhibits osteoclast activity via calcitonin receptors. Bisphosphonates → osteoclast apoptosis via mevalonate pathway inhibition. Core concept: bone resorption is always a balance between RANKL (pro-resorption, turns osteoclasts ON) and calcitonin (anti-resorption, turns osteoclasts OFF). Disease states tip this balance — metastatic bone disease, hyperparathyroidism, and inflammatory arthritis all shift it toward RANKL dominance.

cards.front: What are the key biochemical markers of bone formation and resorption?
cards.back:
Best marker of bone formation: alkaline phosphatase (ALP), specifically bone-specific ALP (BSAP), produced by osteoblasts. Other formation markers: osteocalcin, P1NP (most sensitive). Bone resorption markers: NTX, CTX, TRAP.
Elevated ALP — any condition with active osteoblasts: rickets, osteomalacia, Paget's disease, hyperparathyroidism (osteitis fibrosa cystica), healing fractures, osteoblastic metastases.
Low ALP — hypophosphatasia: rare genetic deficiency of tissue-nonspecific ALP. Presents clinically like rickets (bowing, fractures, rachitic changes on X-ray) but ALP is LOW — the key distinguishing lab. Bisphosphonates are contraindicated in hypophosphatasia.

cards.front: What is osteoinduction and what is its molecular mechanism?
cards.back: Osteoinduction is the process by which undifferentiated progenitor cells are stimulated to differentiate into osteoblasts. Primary mediators: BMPs (especially BMP-2, -4, -7). Signaling: BMPs bind BMPR-I/II (serine-threonine kinase receptors) → phosphorylate Smad 1/5/8 → complex translocates to nucleus → activates RUNX2 and OSX → osteoblast differentiation. The three graft properties: osteoinduction (BMP/Smad/RUNX2 signaling), osteoconduction (scaffold for ingrowth), osteogenesis (live osteoblasts forming bone directly). Fresh autograft has all three; allograft has osteoconduction + some osteoinduction, no osteogenesis.

cards.front: What is the CPAK classification and what does it capture?
cards.back: CPAK (Combined Plane Alignment of the Knee) is a 9-type classification system that characterizes a patient's constitutional (native, pre-arthritic) knee phenotype. It is based on two independent variables: (1) arithmetic HKA (aHKA) — the combined varus/valgus contribution of the femur and tibia to overall limb alignment (varus, neutral, or valgus); and (2) joint line obliquity (JLO) — the angle and direction of the native joint line (apex distal, neutral, or apex proximal). The 3 × 3 matrix produces 9 phenotypes. Purpose in TKA: rather than correcting all knees to a neutral mechanical axis, CPAK-guided planning attempts to restore the patient's individual constitutional alignment — the alignment their knee had before arthritis developed.

cards.front: For varus vs. valgus knee malalignment, which bone is typically the source of deformity, and what osteotomy corrects it?
cards.back: Varus alignment: the tibia is usually the culprit (low mPTA). Corrective procedure: valgus-producing HTO — fix the tibial problem at the tibia. Valgus alignment: the femur is usually the culprit (abnormal mLDFA). Corrective procedure: distal femoral osteotomy (DFO) — fix the femoral problem at the femur. This bone-of-origin framework also informs TKA planning via CPAK: understanding which bone drives the deformity guides component positioning decisions in kinematically aligned techniques.

cards.front: What is the most common complication of medial opening wedge HTO?
cards.back: Patella baja (infrapatellar contracture). Opening the medial tibial cortex distally effectively lengthens the proximal tibia, causing the patellar tendon to become relatively short relative to the new tibial length — the patella descends. This is clinically significant if subsequent TKA is planned: patella baja complicates exposure, increases the risk of patellar tendon avulsion during TKA, and makes patellar component positioning more difficult.

cards.front: What structural feature distinguishes the PAO from other pelvic osteotomies?
cards.back: The posterior column is preserved intact. The four PAO cuts (superior pubic ramus, ischium, retroacetabular surface, ilium) liberate the acetabular fragment while leaving the posterior column uncut — providing immediate stability, maintaining acetabular blood supply, and preserving the abductor musculature. This is the defining anatomic advantage of the PAO over triple osteotomies, which cut all three columns and rely on external fixation or plate fixation for stability.

cards.front: What version abnormalities characterize hip dysplasia?
cards.back: Excess femoral anteversion AND excess acetabular anteversion — both components are anteverted. This combined anteversion reduces anterior coverage of the femoral head, contributes to instability, and explains the characteristic anterior labral injury pattern in dysplastic hips. It also guides THA planning: in dysplastic patients, the surgeon must account for the pre-existing excess anteversion at both the cup and stem to avoid inadvertently creating a combined anteversion that predisposes to anterior dislocation.

cards.front: What is the radiation dose for heterotopic ossification prophylaxis in THA?
cards.back: 700 rads (7 Gy), given as a single fraction within 24–72 hours of surgery (preoperative or early postoperative). Used as an alternative to NSAIDs (indomethacin) for HO prophylaxis, particularly in patients who cannot tolerate NSAIDs.

cards.front: What are the four types of corrosion relevant to orthopaedic implants?
cards.back: (1) Passivation — not true corrosion but its opposite: formation of a protective oxide layer on the metal surface that resists further corrosion. Titanium is the classic example; its native TiO₂ layer makes it highly corrosion-resistant. (2) Fretting corrosion — small repetitive cyclic motion between two surfaces disrupts the passive oxide layer, exposing bare metal that re-oxidizes repeatedly → generates metal debris. Occurs at ANY modular junction (head-neck, neck-stem, stem-sleeve). (3) Crevice corrosion — tight crevices (e.g., under a screw head, at a modular taper) create a local microenvironment with low oxygen tension and low pH → accelerates oxidative metal dissolution within the crevice. (4) Galvanic corrosion — electrochemical reaction when two dissimilar metals are in contact in an electrolyte (body fluids); the less noble metal acts as the anode and corrodes preferentially. Example: mixing a cobalt-chrome head with a titanium stem at the taper.
