cards.front: What cup position in metal-on-metal THA is associated with elevated serum metal ion levels?
cards.back: Cup abduction >55°. Excessive inclination causes edge loading of the acetabular component — the femoral head contacts the rim rather than being fully seated in the cup, concentrating contact stress at a small area and dramatically increasing metal ion generation. Target inclination for MoM bearings is 40° ± 10° (stricter than conventional bearings). Elevated cobalt/chromium ions are associated with adverse local tissue reactions (ALTR), pseudotumors, and systemic toxicity.

cards.front: What is adhesive wear and why is it the most important wear mechanism in osteolysis?
cards.back: Adhesive wear occurs when molecular bonds form between two articulating surfaces during contact. Motion requires breaking these bonds — and the weaker material fails preferentially, transferring material from one surface to the other and generating submicron polyethylene particles. Adhesive wear is the primary driver of osteolysis because it produces the small (<1 µm) particles that macrophages engulf, triggering the RANKL-mediated inflammatory cascade that resorbs periprosthetic bone.

cards.front: What are the two mechanisms of abrasive wear in joint arthroplasty?
cards.back: Two-body abrasive wear: occurs when the two articulating surfaces have different hardness — the harder surface scratches the softer one (e.g., a scratched femoral head gouging the polyethylene). Third-body abrasive wear: a particle (bone cement fragment, metal debris, bone chip) becomes trapped between two articulating surfaces and scratches both — the trapped third body acts as an abrasive intermediary regardless of the relative hardness of the two bearing surfaces.

cards.front: What is the formula for volumetric wear debris in THA, and what is its clinical implication?
cards.back: V = π × r² × w, where r = femoral head radius and w = linear wear (penetration depth). Head size does NOT affect linear wear rate (penetration per unit sliding distance) — but because volumetric debris scales with r², larger heads produce more total debris for the same linear wear. This is the biomechanical basis for why large-head metal-on-metal bearings generated catastrophically high debris volumes — the r² term amplified volumetric output even though the linear wear rate was low.

cards.front: What are the optimal pore parameters for bony ingrowth into a porous-coated implant?
cards.back: Remember 50/50 — 50% porosity, 50 microns pore size. These parameters maximize surface area for osseointegration while providing sufficient interconnected pore channels for vascular and bony ingrowth.

cards.front: Why is UHMWPE irradiated, and what is the downside?
cards.back: Radiation cross-linking is done to improve volumetric wear resistance — cross-links between polymer chains reduce the amount of wear debris generated, lowering the risk of osteolysis. The downside is reduced mechanical strength: cross-linking disrupts the polyethylene's crystalline structure and decreases fatigue resistance, making the material more susceptible to fracture and delamination under cyclic loading.

cards.front: How do remelting and annealing differ as post-irradiation treatments for cross-linked polyethylene, and what is the tradeoff?
cards.back: Both methods are used after radiation cross-linking of UHMWPE to reduce free radicals (which cause oxidative degradation). Remelting heats the material above its melting point (~150°C) → eliminates virtually all free radicals → excellent oxidation resistance. Downside: melting disrupts the crystalline structure → reduced crystallinity → lower fatigue strength and hardness → greater risk of mechanical failure (fracture, delamination). Annealing heats below the melting point → reduces but does NOT eliminate free radicals → preserves crystalline structure → better fatigue strength and mechanical properties. Downside: residual free radicals remain → ongoing oxidation risk long-term. Tradeoff: remelting = better oxidation resistance, worse mechanical properties. Annealing = better mechanical properties, incomplete free radical elimination.

cards.front: What is the sequential release order for improving femoral exposure in the anterior approach to THA?
cards.back: When femoral exposure is difficult in the direct anterior approach, releases proceed in this order: (1) superior capsule and conjoint tendon — first line; (2) piriformis — second; (3) obturator externus — last resort. The obturator externus is released last because it is a major stabilizer of the femoral head and contributes to posterior hip stability; releasing it increases dislocation risk and is only done when the prior releases are insufficient.

cards.front: What is the characteristic plain radiograph finding of SPONK?
cards.back: Sclerotic halo — a rim of sclerosis surrounding the subchondral lesion on the medial femoral condyle. This represents the reactive bone response to the subchondral insufficiency fracture and is the boards-testable plain film sign of SPONK. Early disease (Ahlback stage I) may have a normal X-ray; the sclerotic halo appears as the lesion evolves.

cards.front: What is the preoperative glycemic target for arthroplasty, and why use fructosamine over HbA1c?
cards.back: Fructosamine <292 μmol/L. Fructosamine reflects the 21-day average blood glucose (vs. HbA1c, which reflects ~3 months), making it a more actionable preoperative metric — it captures recent glycemic control in the weeks immediately before surgery and responds to short-term optimization efforts. It is a modifiable risk factor: delaying surgery until fructosamine is <292 reduces the risk of PJI and wound complications.

cards.front: When multiple prior incisions are present around the knee, which should be used for TKA?
cards.back: The most lateral incision. Anterior knee skin is supplied predominantly from the medial side via the medial genicular vessels. Making a new medial or midline incision when a prior lateral incision exists creates a skin bridge with compromised perfusion — the lateral edge of that bridge is fed by the medial supply, which the new incision would interrupt. Using the most lateral existing incision preserves the medial blood supply to the intervening skin and avoids wound necrosis.

cards.front: What is the target for posterior condylar offset in TKA femoral component sizing?
cards.back: Posterior condylar offset (PCO) should be at least 80% of the lateral cortical width. PCO — the distance the posterior femoral condyles project behind the posterior cortical line — is a determinant of knee flexion after TKA. Undersizing the femoral component reduces PCO, tightens the flexion gap, and limits postoperative flexion. Restoring or slightly exceeding native PCO maximizes flexion arc.

cards.front: What distinguishes a hinged TKA implant from a VVC (varus-valgus constrained) implant?
cards.back: A hinged implant has a fixed extension stop that prevents hyperextension — VVC does not. Both provide significant varus-valgus stability via a deep intercondylar post-and-box mechanism, but only the hinged design physically limits extension. Indicated when the posterior capsule and collateral ligaments are incompetent and the patient cannot achieve active extension control, such as in severe instability, extensor mechanism disruption, or significant bone loss requiring megaprosthesis.

cards.front: What is patella clunk syndrome and what are its risk factors?
cards.back: Patella clunk is a complication of posterior-stabilized (PS) TKA in which a fibrous nodule forms at the proximal pole of the patella or quadriceps tendon junction and catches on the anterior edge of the femoral intercondylar box during extension, producing an audible/palpable clunk. Risk factors: wide femoral box (more space → more fibrous tissue proliferation) and tall femoral box (higher anterior edge → nodule engages more forcefully during extension). Treatment: arthroscopic or open excision of the fibrous nodule.

cards.front: What is the prerequisite for extensor mechanism reconstruction with Marlex mesh in TKA?
cards.back: Adequate bone stock on the tibial side. Marlex mesh (monofilament polypropylene) is used for extensor mechanism reconstruction when the patellar tendon or quadriceps tendon is irreparably disrupted. The distal fixation relies on the tibial tubercle — if bone is deficient there, the repair has no anchor and will fail. Good tibial bone is a requirement before this technique is viable.

cards.front: What is the vascular risk of lateral retinacular release in TKA?
cards.back: Patellar AVN. The lateral superior genicular artery is the dominant contributor to the patellar anastomotic ring and must be preserved during lateral retinacular release. Sacrificing it — particularly when combined with a medial parapatellar arthrotomy — can devascularize the patella, leading to AVN and subsequent fracture or component failure.

cards.front: What is the significance of femoral notching in TKA and how is it managed?
cards.back: Femoral notching occurs when the anterior femoral cut extends into the anterior femoral cortex. Notching ≥3 mm below the anterior cortex creates a stress riser and significantly increases the risk of supracondylar periprosthetic femur fracture. Management: bypass the notch with a stemmed femoral component, which transfers load past the stress riser and restores construct integrity.

cards.front: What is the mechanism of tranexamic acid (TXA) and its key contraindication?
cards.back: TXA is a lysine analog — it binds the lysine-binding sites on plasminogen, blocking plasmin formation and inhibiting fibrinolysis. Key contraindication: seizure disorder. At higher doses TXA crosses the blood-brain barrier and competitively antagonizes glycine receptors, lowering the seizure threshold.

cards.front: What is the maximum acceptable joint line alteration in TKA?
cards.back: 8 mm. Elevating or lowering the joint line beyond 8 mm leads to mid-flexion instability, patellofemoral dysfunction (patella baja or alta relative to the new joint line), and flexion gap imbalance. Restoring the native joint line is a primary goal of both primary and revision TKA.

cards.front: In what clinical scenario is antibiotic-impregnated cement indicated in TKA/THA?
cards.back: Higher-risk patients only — not routine use. Risk factors that may warrant antibiotic-impregnated cement include immunosuppression, diabetes, obesity, prior infection, revision surgery, or other host factors that elevate PJI risk. Routine use in primary low-risk arthroplasty is not supported.

cards.front: Which medications should be stopped 1 week before elective orthopaedic surgery?
cards.back: Antiplatelets (clopidogrel, etc.), aspirin, factor Xa inhibitors (rivaroxaban, apixaban), warfarin, and NSAIDs — all held 1 week prior to surgery.

cards.front: Which lupus medications should be continued perioperatively before THA/TKA, and why?
cards.back: Per ACR/AAHKS 2017/2021/2022 guidelines, SLE-specific medications are continued when recommended by rheumatology: Mycophenolate, Azathioprine, Cyclosporine, Tacrolimus, Rituximab, Belimumab, Anifrolumab, and Voclosporin. Rationale: the risk of organ damage from a lupus flare (nephritis, carditis, CNS involvement) far outweighs the risk of orthopaedic complications such as wound healing or infection. These drugs are not held for the same reasons as RA biologics — discontinuation risks a potentially life-threatening flare.

cards.front: Which antirheumatic drugs should be continued through THA/TKA, per ACR/AAHKS guidelines?
cards.back: Per ACR/AAHKS 2017/2021/2022 guidelines, the following DMARDs are continued through surgery (do NOT hold): Apremilast (Otezla — PDE4 inhibitor), Doxycycline, Hydroxychloroquine (Plaquenil), Leflunomide (Arava), Methotrexate (Rasuvo), and Sulfasalazine (Azulfidine). The last four — Hydroxychloroquine, Leflunomide, Methotrexate, Sulfasalazine — are grouped as CTQ (Continue Through Surgery) on the OITE. Methotrexate in particular was historically held perioperatively, but current guidelines support continuation. Biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL inhibitors, JAK inhibitors) are held — withhold for one dosing cycle before surgery and restart once wound healing is confirmed.

cards.front: What is the ideal indication for patellofemoral arthroplasty (PFA)?
cards.back: Isolated lateral facet patellofemoral arthritis. PFA is best suited for patients with disease confined to the lateral patellofemoral compartment, with a preserved medial and tibiofemoral joint. Extension of disease to the medial patellofemoral facet or tibiofemoral compartments is associated with worse outcomes and higher conversion rates to TKA.

cards.front: How does tibial bearing extrusion differ between fixed and mobile bearing UKA, and how is each managed?
cards.back: Fixed bearing UKA: extrusion results from a tight flexion gap. The bearing is impinged in flexion, cantilevers (lifts off the tibial tray anteriorly), and is extruded — "spits out." Mobile bearing UKA: extrusion results from a loose flexion gap. Without sufficient flexion-gap tension to constrain the bearing, it slips out of the tray. Management: revise to a thicker bearing to tension the flexion gap and restore constraint. The distinction matters intraoperatively — tight flexion gap (fixed bearing risk) vs. loose flexion gap (mobile bearing risk) — and drives opposite gap-balancing strategies.

cards.front: Which phase of throwing most stresses the rotator cuff, and what is happening biomechanically?
cards.back: The deceleration (follow-through) phase. After ball release, the posterior rotator cuff — primarily infraspinatus and teres minor — contracts eccentrically to decelerate the rapidly internally rotating arm. This phase generates the highest distraction forces on the glenohumeral joint. Chronic overload during deceleration is a major contributor to posterior cuff pathology and internal impingement in overhead athletes. The phases in order: wind-up → early cocking → late cocking (max ER) → acceleration → deceleration → follow-through.

cards.front: What is the relationship between the humeral head and the pectoralis major tendon, and why does it matter?
cards.back: The highest point of the humeral head sits approximately 5.6 cm above the superior border of the pectoralis major tendon insertion. This landmark is used intraoperatively during shoulder arthroplasty to set correct humeral component height and restore native anatomy.

cards.front: Where does the popliteofibular ligament (PFL) attach, and what is its function?
cards.back: The PFL attaches to the fibular styloid (posteromedial aspect of the fibular head). It runs from the popliteus musculotendinous junction to the fibular styloid. It is a primary static restraint to external tibial rotation and a key component of the posterolateral corner (PLC), alongside the LCL and popliteus tendon. Avulsion of the fibular styloid on MRI or plain film ("arcuate sign") indicates PLC injury.

cards.front: Where does the anterolateral ligament (ALL) attach on the tibia, and what is its function?
cards.back: The ALL tibial attachment is midway between Gerdy's tubercle and the fibular head. Its femoral attachment is proximal and posterior to the lateral epicondyle (near but distinct from the LCL). The ALL is a primary restraint to internal tibial rotation, particularly at 30° of flexion. It is the anatomic structure responsible for the pivot shift phenomenon — ALL deficiency in ACL-injured knees is associated with a high-grade pivot shift. ALL reconstruction is sometimes added to ACL reconstruction in cases of persistent rotatory instability.

cards.front: What is the anatomic relationship between the popliteus tendon and the LCL?
cards.back: Mnemonic — popliteus is **DAD** relative to the LCL: **D**eep, **A**nterior, **D**istal. The popliteus tendon runs deep to the LCL; its femoral attachment is anterior and distal to the LCL femoral attachment by ~18.5 mm. Layer order lateral to medial: LCL → popliteus tendon → joint capsule. The 18.5 mm separation necessitates two separate tunnels for anatomic PLC reconstruction.

cards.front: What are the femoral and fibular attachment points of the LCL?
cards.back: Femoral attachment: proximal and posterior to the lateral epicondyle. Fibular attachment: anterior to the midpoint of the fibular head. These landmarks are used for anatomic LCL reconstruction and differentiate the LCL from the popliteofibular ligament, which attaches more posteriorly on the fibular head.

cards.front: Describe the Slocum test and what each component tests
cards.back: The Slocum test is a variant of the anterior drawer performed with the tibia rotated. Two components: (1) Tibia externally rotated (~15°) → tests anteromedial rotatory instability (AMRI); implicates the MCL, posteromedial capsule, and ACL. (2) Tibia internally rotated (~30°) → tests anterolateral rotatory instability (ALRI); implicates the ACL and posterolateral structures. A positive test = increased anterior tibial translation compared to neutral.

cards.front: What is the SCAT used for, and which version is boards-relevant?
cards.back: The SCAT (Sport Concussion Assessment Tool) is the standardized sideline tool for concussion evaluation in athletes. The SCAT-4 is the boards-relevant version per Miller's. It assesses: symptom checklist, cognitive screen (orientation, immediate memory, concentration), neurological screen, and balance testing (modified BESS). Used acutely on the sideline and in follow-up. An athlete who screens positive is removed from play immediately — return to play follows a stepwise protocol.

cards.front: What are the phases of adhesive capsulitis and what characterizes each?
cards.back:
Three phases:
- **Inflammatory (painful) phase** — early; predominant symptom is pain, including posterior shoulder pain; ROM may be relatively preserved. Synovitis and capsular inflammation drive this phase.
- **Frozen (stiffening) phase** — mid; ROM loss predominates as pain begins to subside; the capsule contracts and fibroses. External rotation is typically lost first and most severely.
- **Thawing (resolution) phase** — late; gradual spontaneous ROM recovery over months to years.

Pathology: fibroblasts and myofibroblasts deposit **type III collagen** in the capsule and rotator interval, causing contracture — similar to Dupuytren's disease. Treatment in the inflammatory phase focuses on pain control; manipulation under anesthesia or capsular release is reserved for the frozen phase after anti-inflammatory treatment fails.

cards.front: During which throwing phase is the elbow UCL most vulnerable?
cards.back: Late cocking. Valgus stress on the medial elbow peaks during late cocking and early acceleration, when the elbow is near 90–120° of flexion and the forearm is maximally lagging behind the accelerating arm. The UCL resists this valgus torque, and repetitive high loads lead to chronic attenuation, partial tears, or complete rupture. The medial olecranon also impinges on the posteromedial olecranon fossa during this phase (valgus extension overload), producing posteromedial osteophytes.

cards.front: What is the T-sign on elbow MRI arthrogram?
cards.back: The T-sign is contrast extravasation under the ulnar attachment of the UCL on MRI arthrogram, forming a "T" shape at the distal insertion. It is pathognomonic for a UCL tear at the ulnar (distal) attachment. Contrast leaks between the UCL and the sublime tubercle of the ulna, tracking along the undersurface of the ligament and creating the characteristic T configuration. Indicates a distal UCL avulsion — relevant to surgical planning, as distal tears may be repaired vs. reconstructed depending on tissue quality.

cards.front: What are the functional roles of the anterior and posterior bands of the elbow UCL anterior bundle?
cards.back: The elbow UCL (medial collateral ligament) anterior bundle has two bands with distinct biomechanical roles. The **anterior band** is the primary valgus restraint from 0–90° of flexion — it is the primary stabilizer throughout the functional arc of throwing and the structure reconstructed in Tommy John surgery. The **posterior band** becomes the primary restraint at higher degrees of flexion (>90°), as the anterior band slackens. In practical terms, the anterior band is the clinically critical structure; isolated posterior band injury is less functionally limiting.

cards.front: What is batter's shoulder?
cards.back: Posterior glenohumeral instability in the lead shoulder of a baseball batter, caused by a single traumatic event — typically a check swing or full swing and miss, in which the lead shoulder is driven into forced internal rotation and horizontal adduction, loading the posterior capsule and labrum. Distinct from throwing injuries, which are repetitive microtrauma. The lead shoulder (front shoulder relative to the pitcher) bears the eccentric deceleration load when the swing is abruptly stopped. Results in posterior labral tear or posterior capsular injury. Treated as posterior instability.

cards.front: What rotational motion finding increases injury risk in baseball pitchers?
cards.back: A gain of less than 5° of external rotation in the throwing shoulder compared to the non-throwing shoulder is a risk factor for injury. In normal adaptive remodeling, the throwing shoulder develops increased ER (from posterior capsule stretching and humeral retroversion) to accommodate the demands of late cocking. Failure to gain adequate ER — less than 5° compared to the contralateral side — indicates insufficient adaptive laxity. Combined with posterior capsule contracture (GIRD), this creates excessive stress on the posterior-superior labrum and cuff during the late cocking phase, predisposing to internal impingement, SLAP tears, and cuff injury.

cards.front: What is internal impingement in the throwing athlete?
cards.back: Internal impingement is the repetitive contact between the articular surface of the posterior-superior rotator cuff (posterior supraspinatus and anterior infraspinatus) and the posterior-superior glenoid labrum that occurs in the late cocking phase — maximum abduction and external rotation. In this position, the greater tuberosity rotates posteriorly and the cuff undersurface contacts the posterior-superior glenoid rim. In throwers, this repetitive contact causes partial-thickness articular-surface cuff tears (posterior supra/anterior infra), posterior-superior labral fraying or SLAP tears, and Bennett lesion formation. Distinguished from subacromial (external) impingement, which involves the cuff bursal surface contacting the acromion. Internal impingement is a normal physiologic contact that becomes pathologic with the repetitive, high-velocity demands of overhead throwing.

cards.front: What is a Bennett lesion, and what are the proposed mechanisms?
cards.back: A Bennett lesion is a posterior-inferior glenoid exostosis (reactive bone formation) seen in overhead throwers. It occurs at the posterior capsule / posterior band of the IGHL attachment on the glenoid. Two proposed mechanisms: (1) **Internal impingement** — repetitive contact between the posterior-superior cuff and glenoid in late cocking (max ER/ABD) transmits stress to the posterior capsule attachment, stimulating periosteal bone formation; (2) **Traction** — eccentric deceleration loads pull on the posterior capsule/IGHL insertion, producing a traction exostosis. Often associated with posterior labral tears and internal impingement findings. Frequently asymptomatic and incidental on MRI or axillary radiograph in throwers.

cards.front: Which throwing phases are associated with specific shoulder pathology?
cards.back:
- **Early cocking**: deltoid muscle forces peak — shoulder abduction and elevation loads are highest during this phase.
- **Late cocking / early acceleration**: maximum external rotation → internal impingement (posterior-superior labrum contacts the undersurface of the posterior rotator cuff) and SLAP tears (peel-back mechanism: biceps anchor levers the posterior-superior labrum off the glenoid in max ER/ABD).
- **Deceleration**: eccentric loads on the posterior supraspinatus and anterior infraspinatus — these fibers decelerate the rapidly internally rotating arm and are at risk for partial-thickness articular-surface tears. Posterior capsule contracture (GIRD — glenohumeral internal rotation deficit) also develops from repetitive eccentric stress in this phase.

cards.front: How is subscapularis failure after anatomic TSA managed?
cards.back: Timing determines management. Acute failure (<6 weeks): repair the subscapularis — the tendon has not yet retracted or atrophied and is still repairable. Chronic failure (>6 weeks): convert to reverse shoulder arthroplasty (RSA). The chronically failed subscap has retracted, scarred, and atrophied beyond reliable repair; RSA does not require an intact subscapularis for stability or function, making it the appropriate salvage.

cards.front: What is the recommended position for glenohumeral arthrodesis?
cards.back: 20° abduction, 30° forward flexion, 40° internal rotation. This position optimizes hand-to-mouth function and allows the hand to reach the midline of the body. Scapulothoracic motion compensates for the fixed GH joint to provide functional reach. Indications include brachial plexus palsy, irreparable deltoid/cuff paralysis, failed arthroplasty with bone loss, or chronic infection.

cards.front: What is the normal acromiohumeral interval (AHI) and what does a decreased interval indicate?
cards.back: Normal AHI is 8–10 mm on AP shoulder radiograph. A decreased AHI (<7 mm) indicates superior migration of the humeral head and is associated with a massive rotator cuff tear — the cuff can no longer maintain the humeral head centered in the glenoid. An AHI <6 mm is considered the threshold for proximal humeral migration consistent with an irreparable massive tear, and is one indicator pointing toward reverse shoulder arthroplasty over repair.

cards.front: What is the management of multidirectional instability (MDI)?
cards.back: MDI is instability in more than one direction, typically inferior plus anterior and/or posterior. Classic patient: swimmer or gymnast with generalized ligamentous laxity. First-line treatment is nonoperative — structured rehabilitation targeting rotator cuff strengthening and periscapular muscle strengthening to dynamically stabilize the joint. The majority improve with rehab alone. Surgery is reserved for failure of at least 6 months of dedicated rehab. Surgical procedure: inferior capsular shift (capsulorrhaphy) — a circumferential tightening of the entire capsule, not just one direction. Addressing only the anterior or posterior capsule in MDI leads to failure by shifting instability to the untreated direction.

cards.front: What is the glenoid track, and how is it calculated?
cards.back:
The glenoid track is the zone of the posterior humeral head that contacts the anterior glenoid during shoulder abduction and external rotation — the at-risk zone for Hill-Sachs engagement. Formula: **Glenoid track width = 0.83D − d**, where D = AP diameter of the inferior glenoid and d = width of any anterior glenoid bone loss (bony Bankart defect). 0.83 reflects that the humeral head contacts ~83% of the glenoid width during range of motion.

**On-track Hill-Sachs**: the lesion falls within the glenoid track → will not engage → soft tissue Bankart repair alone may suffice.
**Off-track Hill-Sachs**: the lesion extends medial to the glenoid track → will engage in ER/ABD → requires remplissage (fills the Hill-Sachs with infraspinatus) or Latarjet (extends the glenoid track anteriorly with the coracoid).

Glenoid bone loss widens the off-track zone (decreases d subtracted, so smaller track). This is the key framework for deciding between soft tissue repair alone vs. bony augmentation.

cards.front: What accessory portals are used for Bankart repair, and what does each access?
cards.back:
- **7 o'clock portal**: posteroinferior accessory portal; used for posterior Bankart repair and posterior-inferior labral work. Provides access to the inferior glenoid from the posterior side.
- **5 o'clock portal (low anterior / trans-subscapularis)**: anteroinferior accessory portal placed through the subscapularis tendon; used for anterior Bankart repair, particularly for placing the most inferior anterior anchors (5 o'clock position on the glenoid face). The trans-subscapularis trajectory gives the correct angle for inferior anchor placement that cannot be achieved from standard anterior portals without compromising anchor direction.

cards.front: What is the Wilmington portal and when is it used?
cards.back: The Wilmington portal is a trans-cuff accessory portal used for SLAP repair. It is placed through the rotator cuff (between the supraspinatus and infraspinatus) to provide a direct, perpendicular angle for anchor insertion into the posterior-superior glenoid — an angle that cannot be achieved from standard posterior or anterior portals without risking anchor malposition. It is the preferred portal for posterior SLAP anchor placement. The small cuff defect created is generally well tolerated.

cards.front: What are the Putti-Platt and Magnuson-Stack procedures, and why are they no longer preferred?
cards.back:
Both are historical open procedures for anterior glenohumeral instability, largely replaced by anatomic Bankart repair.

**Putti-Platt**: the subscapularis tendon and anterior capsule are divided and overlapped (imbricated/reef'd) — the lateral stump is sutured to the glenoid rim and the medial stump is sutured over it, tightening the anterior soft tissues. Creates a mechanical checkrein but is non-anatomic.

**Magnuson-Stack**: the subscapularis tendon is transferred laterally from the lesser tuberosity to the greater tuberosity (or the bicipital groove), creating a sling effect against anterior translation.

Both procedures restrict external rotation — the primary complication and reason they fell out of favor. Loss of ER leads to altered glenohumeral kinematics, accelerated arthrosis (instability arthropathy), and functional limitation. Both are high-yield as historical/eponymous procedures on the boards.

cards.front: What is an ALPSA lesion and why does it carry a high recurrence risk?
cards.back: ALPSA (Anterior Labroligamentous Periosteal Sleeve Avulsion) is a variant of the Bankart lesion in which the anterior labrum and attached periosteum avulse together and strip medially along the scapular neck, healing in a medialized position rather than detaching as a free edge. Unlike a classic Bankart (free labral detachment), the ALPSA heals — but in the wrong place — creating a deficient anterior bumper that does not restore normal labral height or glenohumeral joint volume. This medialized, scarred-down tissue provides poor restraint against anterior translation, explaining the high recurrence rate. ALPSA is commonly seen in chronic/recurrent dislocators. Surgical repair must mobilize and advance the medialized sleeve back to the glenoid rim.

cards.front: What is a type 2 retear in double-row rotator cuff repair?
cards.back: In double-row repair constructs, retears are classified by failure location. Type 1 = lateral row failure (at the footprint). Type 2 = medial row failure — the repair fails at the musculotendinous junction, where the medial row anchors are placed. Type 2 is the more clinically significant failure pattern; the tissue at the musculotendinous junction is weaker and more prone to pullout than the footprint. Type 2 retears have a worse prognosis for healing and are associated with advanced tendon degeneration.

cards.front: What is the tangent sign on MRI, and what does it indicate?
cards.back: The tangent sign is assessed on T1 oblique sagittal MRI. Draw a line tangent to the superior border of the scapular spine and the coracoid process. **Positive tangent sign**: the supraspinatus muscle belly does NOT rise above this line — indicates significant supraspinatus atrophy. **Negative**: muscle bulges above the line — normal bulk. A positive tangent sign predicts poor outcomes after rotator cuff repair and is a marker of potentially irreparable tear with advanced muscle atrophy. Used to assess reparability in massive RCT.

cards.front: Which physical exam tests assess the subscapularis, and what do they each isolate?
cards.back:
- **Lift-off test** (Gerber): arm behind the back, patient lifts hand off the lumbar spine against resistance — tests the **upper subscapularis**. Requires sufficient internal rotation to get the arm behind the back; not possible if limited motion.
- **Belly press test**: elbow forward, patient presses palm into abdomen — tests the **lower subscapularis**. More useful when lift-off cannot be performed due to limited IR.
- **Bear hug test**: patient places hand on contralateral shoulder, examiner tries to pull it off — sensitivity ~60% for subscapularis tears. Less specific than lift-off/belly press but can be done in more positions.

Subscapularis is the primary internal rotator and anterior stabilizer of the glenohumeral joint; tears are commonly missed — always test specifically.

cards.front: What is the difference between shoulder pseudoparesis and pseudoparalysis?
cards.back:
Both occur with massive rotator cuff tears and intact deltoid/axillary nerve function — the distinction is degree of active elevation loss.

**Pseudoparesis**: patient can actively elevate above 90° but with significant pain and weakness. The rotator cuff force couple is partially intact; some overhead function is preserved.

**Pseudoparalysis**: patient cannot actively elevate above 90° despite an intact deltoid. The rotator cuff can no longer provide a stable fulcrum for the deltoid to act against — the humeral head migrates superiorly and the arm cannot be raised. Deltoid strength is present (distinguish from axillary nerve palsy or deltoid rupture). Associated with massive, often irreparable tears.

**Clinical significance**: pseudoparalysis indicates loss of the rotator cuff force couple and is a marker of severity. In the setting of irreparable massive RCT, pseudoparalysis is a primary indication for reverse shoulder arthroplasty (RSA), which recruits the deltoid as the primary elevator by mediating and distalizing the center of rotation.

cards.front: What are the key boards facts about SPONK (Spontaneous Osteonecrosis of the Knee)?
cards.back:
**Mechanism**: subchondral insufficiency fracture — not true avascular necrosis. The subchondral bone fails under repetitive load, causing overlying cartilage collapse.

**Location**: medial femoral condyle (weight-bearing zone) in the vast majority.

**Demographics**: older patients, classically postmenopausal women. Acute onset medial knee pain with no antecedent trauma.

**Imaging**: MRI is diagnostic — bone marrow edema + subchondral low-signal crescent or flattening. X-ray is often normal early (Ahlback stage I).

**Ahlback staging**: I = normal XR (MRI diagnosis); II = subchondral flattening; III = subchondral radiolucency; IV = articular collapse; V = joint space narrowing (secondary OA).

**Prognosis**: lesion width <50% of MFC width → favorable for nonop healing. Larger lesions collapse.

**Treatment**:
- Small/early: protected weight bearing, NSAIDs; bisphosphonates controversial
- Large or failed nonop: UKA (if isolated medial compartment, adequate bone stock) or TKA
- HTO is NOT the answer for SPONK (unlike medial OA with varus malalignment)

**Distinguish from secondary osteonecrosis**: secondary = younger patients, bilateral, multiple sites (femoral head), associated with steroids, alcohol, sickle cell disease, radiation, Gaucher's.

cards.front: What size threshold triggers osteochondral allograft (OCA) for chondral/OCD lesions on the boards?
cards.back: Lesions >2×2 cm (>4 cm²) = osteochondral allograft. For boards purposes, this is the cutoff — anything above 2×2 cm is too large for OATS autograft (limited plug size and donor site) or MACI alone when there is significant bone involvement, and OCA is the answer. Below this threshold, smaller lesions can be addressed with marrow stimulation, OATS, or MACI depending on depth and patient factors.

cards.front: Where is osteochondritis dissecans (OCD) of the knee most common, and what is the prognosis by location?
cards.back: The posterolateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle is the most common location — accounts for ~70% of knee OCD lesions. It also carries the best prognosis, particularly in skeletally immature patients, where healing with nonoperative management (activity restriction, unloading) is common. Atypical locations (lateral femoral condyle, patella, trochlea) are less common and generally have a worse prognosis. Skeletal maturity is the strongest prognostic factor overall — open physes heal; closed physes do not.

cards.front: Where is numbness most likely after tibial tubercle osteotomy or anterior knee surgery?
cards.back: The inferoLATERAL aspect of the knee. The infrapatellar branch of the saphenous nerve crosses the operative field of anterior knee approaches and TTO incisions; injury to this branch produces sensory loss in the inferomedial to inferolateral patellar region, with the inferolateral distribution being the most commonly reported area of postoperative numbness. Counsel patients preoperatively that this is expected and often permanent.

cards.front: What are the Dejour types of trochlear dysplasia, and which require trochleoplasty?
cards.back:
Dejour classification of trochlear dysplasia (based on lateral radiograph and axial MRI/CT):
- **Type A**: shallow trochlea, crossing sign on lateral X-ray; mild dysplasia
- **Type B**: flat or convex trochlea, crossing sign + supratrochlear spur; severe
- **Type C**: asymmetric trochlea (hypoplastic medial facet), double contour sign; severe
- **Type D**: asymmetric with cliff pattern (abrupt step-off between facets), crossing sign + double contour + supratrochlear spur; most severe

Types B and D have the most significant bony abnormality (flat/convex groove with spur or cliff deformity) and are best managed with trochleoplasty — surgical deepening and reshaping of the trochlear groove. Types A and C may be managed with soft-tissue procedures (MPFL reconstruction, TTT) alone. Trochleoplasty corrects the underlying bony cause of instability rather than compensating for it.

cards.front: How do the Insall-Salvati and Caton-Deschamps ratios differ, and when do you use each?
cards.back:
**Insall-Salvati**: patellar tendon length ÷ greatest diagonal patella length. Normal ~1.0 (0.8–1.2). Alta >1.2, baja <0.8. Limitation: depends on patellar tendon length — unreliable after tibial tubercle osteotomy, patellar tendon surgery, or any procedure altering tendon length. Best for native, unoperated knees.

**Caton-Deschamps (CD)**: distance from inferior patellar pole to anterosuperior tibial plateau ÷ articular surface length of patella. Normal 0.6–1.3. Alta >1.3, baja <0.6. Advantage: measures where the articular surface of the patella sits relative to the tibial plateau — not affected by patellar tendon length. Preferred after TKA, tibial tubercle osteotomy, or when tendon length has been altered.

Rule: Insall-Salvati for native knees; Caton-Deschamps when there has been prior surgery that changes tendon anatomy.

cards.front: What is the primary stabilizer against valgus and internal rotation at 0–30° of knee flexion?
cards.back: The posterior oblique ligament (POL), a key component of the posteromedial corner (PMC). It is the primary static restraint to internal tibial rotation and valgus stress at low flexion angles (0–30°). The superficial MCL is the primary valgus stabilizer across a broader range of flexion. POL injury is implicated in combined posteromedial corner and ACL injuries.

cards.front: What is the screw-home mechanism of the knee and which muscle unlocks it?
cards.back: As the knee reaches full extension, the tibia externally rotates ~10° relative to the femur — driven by the larger medial femoral condyle rolling and gliding further than the lateral. This "locks" the knee in extension, providing passive stability without muscular effort. To initiate flexion, the popliteus internally rotates the tibia (or externally rotates the femur in closed chain) to unlock the mechanism. The popliteus is often called the "key to the knee" for this reason. Innervated by the tibial nerve.

cards.front: What is the blood supply of the ACL?
cards.back: The middle geniculate artery (branch of the popliteal artery), which pierces the posterior capsule to supply the ACL via the synovial envelope surrounding the ligament. This limited, indirect supply contributes to the poor intrinsic healing capacity of the ACL after rupture.

cards.front: How does fibrocartilage compare biomechanically to hyaline cartilage?
cards.back: Fibrocartilage is less elastic than hyaline cartilage. Hyaline cartilage (type II collagen, high proteoglycan content) has superior compressive stiffness and resilience, distributing load more effectively. Fibrocartilage (type I collagen dominant) is stiffer, less compliant, and less able to withstand repetitive compressive loading — which is why marrow stimulation (microfracture) produces an inferior, less durable repair tissue compared to procedures that restore hyaline-like cartilage (MACI, OCA).

cards.front: What osteotomy is indicated for varus vs. valgus knee malalignment, and for pathologic posterior tibial slope?
cards.back:
- **Varus malalignment** (medial compartment overload): medial opening wedge HTO or lateral closing wedge HTO. Shifts load from medial to lateral compartment.
- **Valgus malalignment** (lateral compartment overload): lateral opening wedge DFO or medial closing wedge DFO. Distal femoral osteotomy is preferred for valgus because most valgus deformity originates at the distal femur.
- **Pathologic posterior tibial slope** (e.g., ACL graft failure risk, chronic PCL insufficiency with anterior tibial sag): anterior closing wedge HTO — reduces slope, decreases anterior tibial translation force on the ACL graft or reduces posterior sag in PCL deficiency.

cards.front: What is the protocol if an osteochondral graft is dropped and contaminated intraoperatively?
cards.back: Irrigate with 10% povidone-iodine solution followed by normal saline wash. This decontaminates the graft while preserving chondrocyte viability. Do not discard — the graft can be salvaged with this protocol. Prolonged exposure to povidone-iodine without saline washout is chondrotoxic, so the saline rinse is required.

cards.front: How are cartilage restoration techniques selected based on lesion size and depth?
cards.back:
Decision is driven by lesion size, depth, and whether bone is involved.

**Palliative** (not restorative): debridement, lavage — temporary symptom relief only.

**Marrow stimulation** (microfracture): small, contained lesions. Produces fibrocartilage (type I collagen), not hyaline cartilage — results are not durable long-term. Appropriate for low-demand patients or as a bridge.

**Restorative** (hyaline-like cartilage):
- *MACI (Matrix-Induced Autologous Chondrocyte Implantation)*: two-stage — biopsy/harvest first, then chondrocyte culture and implantation on a collagen scaffold. Best for larger chondral lesions without significant bone loss.
- *Minced cartilage*: autograft or allograft cartilage fragments placed into the defect; single-stage; emerging technique.

**Reconstructive** (structural tissue transfer):
- *OATS (Osteochondral Autograft Transfer System)*: autograft osteochondral plug(s); best for small lesions 1–2 cm; limited by donor site morbidity and plug size.
- *OCA (Osteochondral Allograft)*: fresh allograft for larger, deeper lesions; handles bone loss; can address bipolar (kissing) lesions — a relative contraindication to OATS/MACI. Requires allograft availability and viability timing.

Rule: small + superficial → less invasive (marrow stimulation, OATS); large + deep + bone involvement → OCA.

cards.front: What meniscal tear pattern is associated with parameniscal cysts?
cards.back: Parameniscal cysts are associated with horizontal cleavage tears. The tear creates a cleavage plane parallel to the tibial plateau through which synovial fluid is pumped peripherally with joint loading, forming a cyst at the meniscal periphery. Lateral meniscal cysts are more common than medial. Treatment addresses both the cyst and the underlying tear — arthroscopic repair or partial meniscectomy of the horizontal tear typically causes the cyst to resolve; isolated cyst excision without addressing the tear has a high recurrence rate.

cards.front: What is the composition and collagen architecture of the meniscus?
cards.back: The meniscus is composed of fibroelastic cartilage (type I collagen predominates, with elastin fibers). Collagen architecture is organized in two layers: the surface layer has randomly oriented collagen fibers, which resist compressive and shear forces at the articular surface. The deep layer has two orientations: circumferential (longitudinal) fibers running parallel to the meniscal rim — these are the primary load-bearing fibers that convert axial compression into hoop stress (circumferential tension); and radial (tie) fibers oriented perpendicular to the circumferential fibers, which resist longitudinal splitting and provide structural cohesion. This architecture explains why vertical longitudinal tears (bucket-handle) disrupt the hoop stress mechanism while radial tears completely eliminate it.

cards.front: What is the blood supply of the meniscus and how does it determine healing potential?
cards.back: The medial and lateral inferior genicular arteries (branches of the popliteal artery) supply the peripheral meniscus. Only the outer 10–30% (peripheral third) is vascularized. This creates three zones: red-red zone (outer third, vascularized, best healing), red-white zone (middle third, transitional), and white-white zone (inner third, avascular, no intrinsic healing capacity). Zone determines repair vs. resection: tears in the red-red zone can be repaired; white-white zone tears are not repairable.

cards.front: What is spear tackler's spine, and why is it an absolute contraindication to contact sports?
cards.back: Spear tackler's spine is an acquired cervical stenosis syndrome defined by four criteria: (1) developmental or acquired cervical spinal stenosis, (2) loss of normal cervical lordosis (straightening or kyphosis), (3) radiographic evidence of pre-existing trauma (e.g., end plate changes, interspace narrowing), and (4) a history of using the head as a primary point of contact in tackling. It is an absolute contraindication to return to contact sports because axial loading with the neck in flexion eliminates the spine's normal energy-dissipating lordotic curve, concentrating force at a stenotic segment and dramatically increasing the risk of catastrophic cord injury.

cards.front: How should an avulsed tooth be managed on the sideline?
cards.back: Store the tooth in milk (preferred) or the athlete's buccal sulcus (saliva). Do not let it dry out — exposure to air causes irreversible periodontal ligament cell death. Do not scrub the root. Reimplant as soon as possible; viability drops sharply after 30 minutes of extraoral time. This is a dental emergency — transport immediately.

cards.front: What is the alpha angle, and what value indicates cam-type FAI?
cards.back: The alpha angle is measured on a lateral hip radiograph (modified Dunn view preferred) or MRI as the angle between the femoral neck axis and a line from the femoral head center to the point where the head loses its spherical contour anterolaterally. Normal: <50°. >55° = cam deformity. The 50–55° range is a gray zone. Cam morphology creates a non-spherical prominence at the head-neck junction that abuts the acetabular rim in flexion, causing labral and cartilage injury.

cards.front: What is the standard radiographic workup for FAI, and what does each view add?
cards.back: Four views: (1) AP pelvis — arthritis (Tönnis grade), dysplasia, crossover sign, posterior wall sign; (2) True lateral — general morphology; (3) Modified Dunn (45° or 90° flexion lateral) — alpha angle measurement, best view for cam lesion; (4) False profile — anterior coverage, ACEA measurement. MRI/MRA evaluates labrum and cartilage (anterior-superior labral tear is most common in FAI); CT defines bony morphology for surgical planning.

cards.front: What are the key hip radiographic angles in FAI, their normal values, and what each measures?
cards.back:
- **Alpha angle**: normal <50°; >55° = cam deformity (head-neck asphericity)
- **LCEA (lateral center-edge angle / angle of Wiberg)**: normal 25–40°; >40° = overcoverage (pincer); <25° = dysplasia
- **ACEA (anterior center-edge angle)**: measured on false profile; normal >20°; <20° = anterior undercoverage/dysplasia
- **Acetabular index (Tönnis roof angle)**: normal >0°; elevated = dysplasia/insufficient roof coverage

cards.front: What radiographic signs indicate cam vs. pincer FAI morphology?
cards.back: Cam: pistol grip deformity (loss of normal head-neck offset on AP view, giving a "pistol grip" silhouette); elevated alpha angle >55°. Pincer: crossover sign (anterior acetabular wall crosses medial to the posterior wall on AP view = acetabular retroversion/focal overcoverage); posterior wall sign (femoral head center lies lateral to the posterior acetabular rim = global overcoverage or posterior deficiency). Mixed morphology is most common clinically.

cards.front: What are the hip arthroscopy portals, their uses, and the nerve/vessel at risk at each?
cards.back:
- **Anterolateral**: primary viewing portal; risk — superior gluteal nerve
- **Anterior**: accesses central compartment; risks — lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (LFCN), lateral femoral circumflex artery (LFCA)
- **Distal anterolateral (DALA)**: accesses peripheral compartment; risk — lateral femoral circumflex artery
- **Mid-anterior**: accessory working portal
- **Posterolateral**: posterior joint access; risk — sciatic nerve (risk increases with hip external rotation, which draws the sciatic nerve anteriorly)
Most common overall complication of hip arthroscopy: traction neuropraxia (pudendal nerve from perineal post, lateral femoral cutaneous nerve).

cards.front: What position is most vulnerable for instability after hip arthroscopy, and why?
cards.back: Extension combined with external rotation. This position maximally stresses the anterior capsule — which is routinely incised (anterior capsulotomy) during hip arthroscopy for access. If the capsule is not repaired, the hip lacks its primary anterior static restraint in extension/ER and is at risk for microinstability or frank iatrogenic instability. Capsular closure after hip arthroscopy is important, particularly in patients with underlying hyperlaxity or borderline dysplasia. Microinstability post-scope is underrecognized and presents as persistent anterior hip pain and a sense of giving way.

cards.front: What does a persistently high alpha angle after FAI surgery indicate?
cards.back: Inadequate cam resection. Residual cam morphology is the most common cause of failed FAI surgery and persistent/recurrent symptoms. The alpha angle should be reduced to <50° postoperatively. Intraoperative fluoroscopy with dynamic assessment is used to confirm adequate resection before closure.

cards.front: When is a grade III MCL injury managed operatively vs. nonoperatively?
cards.back: Most grade III MCL injuries — whether proximal (femoral) or distal (tibial) — are managed nonoperatively with bracing and rehab. Isolated grade III tears generally heal well without surgery. The exception is a Stener-type lesion: interposition of the MCL within the joint (the ligament becomes folded into or trapped in the joint space), which prevents healing and requires operative extraction and repair. This is analogous to the thumb UCL Stener lesion (UCL displaced superficial to the adductor aponeurosis). On MRI, look for MCL folded or displaced into the joint rather than lying in its normal medial plane — this finding mandates surgery. Surgical approach by scenario: distal Stener lesion → primary repair. Acute unstable distal grade III (without joint interposition but grossly unstable) → repair plus reconstruct. Proximal tears generally heal nonop; distal tears have worse healing biology and a lower threshold for surgery.

cards.front: What are the three layers of the medial knee?
cards.back:
(Warren & Marshall classification)
- **Layer 1**: Sartorius and the deep crural fascia (investing fascia of the leg)
- **Layer 2**: Superficial MCL, posterior oblique ligament (POL), semimembranosus expansions, MPFL (medial patellofemoral ligament — primary restraint to lateral patellar translation, runs from medial femoral epicondyle to superomedial patella)
- **Layer 3**: Deep MCL (medial capsular ligament) and posteromedial capsule
The MCL complex = layers 2 + 3. The MPFL sits in layer 2 and is the structure reconstructed for patellar instability.

cards.front: What are the three layers of the lateral knee?
cards.back:
(Seebacher classification)
- **Layer 1**: IT band (anteriorly) and biceps femoris long head (posteriorly)
- **Layer 2**: Quadriceps retinaculum (anteriorly); patellofemoral ligaments
- **Layer 3**: Joint capsule — superficial stratum contains the LCL, fabellofibular ligament, and arcuate ligament; deep stratum contains the coronary (meniscotibial) ligaments. The popliteus tendon runs between layers 2 and 3.
The PLC (posterolateral corner) structures — LCL, popliteus tendon, PFL, arcuate ligament — are largely within layer 3.

cards.front: How do you interpret the dial test for posterolateral corner vs. PCL injury?
cards.back: The dial test measures external tibial rotation asymmetry (>10–15° side-to-side = positive). Asymmetry at 30° only = isolated PLC injury. Asymmetry at both 30° and 90° = combined PLC + PCL injury. Memory anchor: the PCL is tested at 90° of flexion (posterior drawer test position) — if the PCL is torn, you'll see increased ER at 90° in addition to 30°. If asymmetry disappears at 90°, the PCL is intact and the PLC is the sole culprit.

cards.front: How does varus stress testing at 0° vs. 30° localize lateral knee ligament injury?
cards.back: Varus laxity at 30° of flexion only = isolated LCL injury. The posterior capsule and cruciates provide secondary varus stability in full extension; at 30°, they are relaxed and the LCL is the primary restraint. Varus laxity at 0° (full extension) = LCL plus cruciate involvement (PCL most commonly, or combined PLC + PCL injury) — the cruciates must also be compromised for laxity to be present in full extension. This mirrors the MCL logic: laxity at 30° only = isolated collateral; laxity at 0° = collateral + cruciate.

cards.front: What is the natural history of PCL insufficiency?
cards.back: PCL deficiency leads to progressive medial compartment and patellofemoral compartment DJD over time. The mechanism: posterior tibial sag increases contact pressure in the medial compartment and alters patellofemoral kinematics, accelerating cartilage wear in both locations. Isolated PCL tears in low-demand patients are often initially managed nonoperatively, but the long-term arthritic sequelae — particularly medial and PF compartment arthrosis — provide the rationale for reconstruction in active patients and in combined ligamentous injuries.

cards.front: What is the mechanical basis of the pivot shift test?
cards.back: In the ACL-deficient knee, the anterolateral tibial plateau subluxates anteriorly at low flexion angles (the IT band acts as an extensor in early flexion, driving anterior tibial translation). As the knee flexes to ~30–40°, the IT band shifts posterior to the knee axis and becomes a flexor — this change in force vector pulls the tibial plateau posteriorly, causing a palpable reduction (the "clunk"). The pivot shift is therefore a subluxation in extension and a reduction with flexion. It implicates the ACL as the primary restraint and the ALL/anterolateral capsule as secondary restraints — a high-grade pivot shift suggests combined ACL + ALL/PLC insufficiency.

cards.front: What are the indications and limitations of ACL primary repair?
cards.back: Candidate lesion: proximal-third (femoral-sided) tear, acute injury. The ligament retains enough tissue integrity and vascular supply proximally to heal with suture repair (the Bridge-Enhanced ACL Repair / BEAR technique uses a collagen scaffold). Primary repair is not indicated for mid-substance or distal tears, which lack healing potential. Key limitation: high re-tear rates, particularly in pediatric and adolescent patients — this population should be counseled carefully, and many surgeons still prefer reconstruction in skeletally immature patients with high activity demands. Long-term data vs. reconstruction are still emerging.

cards.front: When can an athlete return to sport after ACL reconstruction?
cards.back: Minimum 9 months. Data show re-tear rates drop significantly when return to sport is delayed to ≥9 months — each additional month before 9 months is associated with a ~51% reduction in re-tear risk (Grindem et al). Criteria-based clearance (strength symmetry, hop testing, psychological readiness) should be met in addition to time. Returning before 9 months markedly increases re-injury risk regardless of functional test results.

cards.front: What minimum graft diameter is required for ACL reconstruction, and why does it matter?
cards.back: Graft diameter must be >8 mm. Grafts ≤8 mm have significantly higher re-tear rates. This is particularly relevant for hamstring autografts (gracilis + semitendinosus), where final graft size is variable and patient-dependent — a small hamstring harvest may yield an inadequate construct. If the anticipated graft diameter is <8 mm, consider an alternative graft source (BPTB, quad tendon, allograft augmentation). Graft diameter is one of the strongest modifiable predictors of ACL reconstruction failure.

cards.front: What is the Lachmann grading system for ACL laxity?
cards.back: Graded by translation and endpoint quality. Translation: Grade 1 = <5 mm, Grade 2 = 5–10 mm, Grade 3 = >10 mm. Endpoint: A = firm, B = soft (absent endpoint). Most ACL tears are grade IIB (5–10 mm translation, soft endpoint). A firm endpoint (grade A) suggests a partial tear or intact secondary restraints; a soft endpoint (grade B) indicates complete disruption of the ACL. Grade and endpoint together guide clinical decision-making.

cards.front: Why is a collar contraindicated for occipitocervical instability, and what is the correct management?
cards.back: Occipitocervical instability (atlanto-occipital dissociation, AOD) represents complete disruption of the ligamentous and capsular connections between the skull base and C1–C2 — it is the most unstable injury in the cervical spine. A rigid collar provides no meaningful stability at this level and creates a false sense of security. **Traction is also contraindicated** — distraction at the occipitoatlantal junction risks acute neurologic deterioration and death. A halo vest is insufficient and may apply distracting forces across the unstable segment. **Definitive treatment: urgent posterior occipitocervical fusion** (occiput to C2 or lower). Until the OR, keep the patient supine and minimize all cervical movement. Boards rule: AOD → no collar, no traction, no halo. Operate.

cards.front: Why are C1 lateral mass screws placed unicortically, and what is at risk with bicortical purchase?
cards.back: C1 lateral mass screws (Harms technique) must be **unicortical only**. The **internal carotid artery** runs immediately anterior to the C1 lateral mass as it ascends toward the carotid canal in the skull base. Bicortical purchase drives the screw through the anterior cortex of the lateral mass directly into the internal carotid — a potentially fatal vascular injury. Additional at-risk structures at C1: the **vertebral artery** runs in the sulcus arteriosus on the superior surface of the posterior C1 arch (at risk with posterior arch screws or laterally misplaced lateral mass screws), and the **hypoglossal nerve (CN XII)** exits near the anterior-inferior C1 lateral mass. Boards rule: C1 = unicortical lateral mass screws only. Bicortical = internal carotid at risk.

cards.front: What is the safe zone for occipital plate screw placement and what structure is at risk above the EOP?
cards.back: Occipital screws must be placed **at or below the external occipital protuberance (EOP)** — never above it. The midline occipital keel below the EOP is the thickest region of the occiput (up to 15mm), providing the best bone stock for bicortical screw purchase. Above the EOP, the bone is significantly thinner and the **superior sagittal sinus** runs in the midline — violation risks catastrophic venous sinus injury, massive hemorrhage, and air embolism. Laterally, bone thins further (3–5mm) and the transverse sinuses are at risk. Surgical rule: plate position = at or below EOP, screws clustered in the midline keel for maximum purchase and sinus avoidance.

cards.front: What are the ADI thresholds for atlantoaxial instability and when is surgery indicated?
cards.back: The atlanto-dens interval (ADI) is measured on lateral radiograph from the posterior cortex of the anterior C1 arch to the anterior cortex of the odontoid. Normal: ≤3mm in adults, ≤5mm in children. Threshold significance: **ADI >3mm** = transverse atlantal ligament (TAL) injury. **ADI >5mm** = TAL + alar ligament disruption. **ADI >10mm** = all stabilizing ligaments disrupted (TAL + alar + apical) → **surgical indication** (C1-C2 posterior fusion). The >10mm threshold is the hard surgical cutoff — at this degree of instability the cord has no passive ligamentous protection. Context: most commonly applied in rheumatoid arthritis (pannus erodes the TAL and odontoid) and Down syndrome. Boards rule: ADI >10mm = operate regardless of symptoms. Pairs with SAC <8mm as the two radiographic triggers for C1-C2 fusion.

cards.front: What changes occur in disc degeneration, and how do they compare to OA and normal aging?
cards.back: Disc degeneration cascade — **Decrease**: water, proteoglycans, GAGs, chondroitin sulfate, absolute cell number, nutritional transport, matrix synthesis, disc height, pH. **Increase**: keratan sulfate, collagen diameter, non-collagen glycoprotein, degenerative enzyme activity, IL-6, MMPs, nitric oxide, PGE2, fibroblast-like cells. Key discriminators vs. OA and normal aging: (1) **Water**: ↓ in disc degen and normal aging; ↑ in early OA — collagen network breakdown removes the restraint on proteoglycan-driven swelling, so water rushes in early OA before proteoglycan loss dominates. (2) **Cell number**: ↓ in disc degen and normal aging; ↑ initially in OA (chondrocyte cloning/clustering as repair response), then ↓ in late OA. (3) **Matrix synthesis**: ↓ in disc degen and aging; ↑ initially in OA (reparative attempt). (4) **Fibroblast-like cells**: ↑ in disc degen only — not a feature of OA or aging. (5) **KS:CS ratio**: ↑ in all three — not discriminating. (6) **Cytokines**: IL-6 drives disc degen; IL-1 and TNF-α are the dominant OA cytokines. Boards trap: water ↑ in early OA is the most tested distinction.

cards.front: What is the Space Available for the Cord (SAC), how is it measured, and what threshold indicates surgical intervention?
cards.back: The SAC is the distance from the posterior aspect of the odontoid (or posterior C2 body) to the anterior cortex of the posterior ring of C1, measured on lateral radiograph or CT at the C1–C2 level. It represents the remaining space for the spinal cord after the odontoid occupies its portion of the canal. Normal canal at C1 ≈17mm; odontoid occupies ~5mm. Steele's Rule of Thirds: at the atlas level, the space is equally divided among the odontoid (⅓), the cord (⅓), and a "safe" reserve (⅓). **SAC <13mm = at risk; SAC <8mm = surgical indication** — C1-C2 posterior fusion. Contexts: Down syndrome (atlantoaxial instability from transverse ligament laxity), rheumatoid arthritis (cranial settling with pannus), os odontoideum, JIA. Boards rule: screen Down syndrome patients with flexion-extension radiographs before contact sports or anesthesia; SAC <8mm → fuse regardless of symptoms.

cards.front: What factors predict outcome after surgical decompression for cervical myelopathy, and which is least predictive?
cards.back: The strongest predictors of postoperative neurologic recovery are: (1) **preoperative mJOA score — most important factor**; better preoperative function = better postoperative outcome. (2) Duration of symptoms — longer duration before decompression = worse outcome. (3) MRI cord signal change — T2 hyperintensity (cord edema/gliosis) worsens prognosis; T1 hypointensity (myelomalacia) = worst prognosis. **Age is the least predictive factor** — elderly patients recover as well as younger patients when corrected for other variables. Boards hierarchy: preoperative JOA > duration > cord signal > age (least predictive). Trap: age alone should not dissuade from surgery.

cards.front: Why is C3 a critical level in cervical laminoplasty, and what is the consequence of disrupting it?
cards.back: The semispinalis cervicis muscle originates from the C3–C5 laminae and inserts on the C2 spinous process — it is the primary cervical extensor and the main stabilizer of the C2–C3 segment. In traditional C3–C7 laminoplasty, detaching or disrupting the semispinalis cervicis at C3 severs its functional connection to C2, eliminating the primary extensor moment arm at the C2–C3 junction → progressive post-laminoplasty kyphosis and significant axial neck pain. This is why C3-sparing laminoplasty techniques were developed: either (1) performing laminoplasty from C4–C7 and doing a separate C3 laminectomy with muscular re-attachment, or (2) preserving the C3 lamina entirely and relying on indirect decompression at that level. Nuance: if the compression is maximal at C3–C4, you cannot spare C3 — the trade-off between adequate decompression and muscular preservation must be weighed explicitly. Post-laminoplasty axial neck pain (up to 60% of patients) is largely driven by this muscle detachment. Boards rule: laminoplasty complication = axial pain + kyphosis → disruption of semispinalis at C3 is the mechanism. C3-sparing = preserve the C2 extensor anchor.

cards.front: What is the mJOA score, its severity thresholds, and when is surgery indicated for cervical myelopathy?
cards.back: The modified Japanese Orthopaedic Association (mJOA) scale grades cervical myelopathy severity out of 18 (18 = normal). Severity thresholds: mild = 15–17, moderate = 12–14, severe = ≤11. **Surgical indication: mJOA ≤14.** The clinically critical window is the moderate range (12–14) — these patients retain enough neurologic reserve to recover meaningfully with decompression, but are deteriorating. Patients in this range should be caught and operated before dropping into the severe category, where outcomes are significantly worse. Mild myelopathy (15–17) without progressive worsening can be observed; surgery is appropriate if progression is documented. Boards rule: mJOA ≤14 → operate. The 12–14 window is the highest-yield surgical group — best functional recovery with lowest risk of continued deterioration.

cards.front: What are the five flexor tendon zones of the hand, and why is Zone II clinically significant?
cards.back: **Zone I** — distal to the FDS insertion, FDP tendon only, bounded distally by the FDP insertion into the distal phalanx. **Zone II** ("Bunnell's no man's land") — from the FDS insertion to the distal palmar crease (A1 pulley); both FDP and FDS run together through a tight fibro-osseous sheath, so a laceration here involves two tendons in a confined space prone to adhesion. **Zone III** — distal palmar crease to the flexor retinaculum; lumbrical origins are here. **Zone IV** — under the flexor retinaculum (carpal tunnel); tendon lacerations at this level put the median nerve at risk. **Zone V** — proximal to the retinaculum to the musculotendinous junction in the forearm. Boards rule: Zone II = worst prognosis for tendon repair (two tendons, one tight sheath, high adhesion risk) — this is why "no man's land" was historically avoided for primary repair before modern core-suture technique and early controlled active motion protocols (see WALANT, entry [3] in hand_wrist_incorrects.md) improved outcomes.

cards.front: What is the organizing rule for extensor tendon zones, and what classic injury maps to each?
cards.back: Kleinert/Verdan zones I–VIII run distal to proximal over the dorsum; Doyle added zone IX at the forearm musculotendinous junction. **Odd zones overlie joints, even zones overlie bone/shafts.** Zone I (DIP) = mallet finger (terminal tendon avulsion). Zone II (middle phalanx). Zone III (PIP) = boutonniere deformity (central slip disruption — see entry below). Zone IV (proximal phalanx). Zone V (MCP) = sagittal band rupture ("boxer's knuckle," extensor subluxation) and "fight bite" human-bite injuries over the knuckle. Zone VI (metacarpals). Zone VII (wrist, under the extensor retinaculum) — adhesions are common here because the tendons are tethered under the retinaculum. Zone VIII (distal forearm, musculotendinous junctions). Zone IX (proximal forearm muscle bellies). Boards rule: odd = joint, even = bone; Zone III → boutonniere, Zone V → sagittal band/fight bite.

cards.front: How do pronator syndrome, anterior interosseous nerve (AIN) syndrome, and carpal tunnel syndrome differ by compression site and exam findings?
cards.back: All three are median neuropathies at different levels. **Pronator syndrome** — compression proximal to the AIN branch point, at sites including the ligament of Struthers (only if a supracondylar process is present), the lacertus fibrosus, between the two heads of pronator teres, or at the FDS arch. Presents with volar forearm pain plus sensory loss that **includes the thenar eminence**, because the palmar cutaneous branch is compressed along with the main trunk. **AIN syndrome** — pure motor branch, compressed in the same general region (deep head of pronator teres, FDS arch); no sensory loss at all, presents as weakness of FPL, FDP to index/long, and pronator quadratus — classic finding is inability to form the "OK sign" (makes a triangle instead of a pinch). **Carpal tunnel syndrome** — compression at the wrist under the transverse carpal ligament; sensory loss in the thumb/index/long/radial half of ring, plus thenar motor loss (APB, opponens), but **thenar eminence sensation is spared** because the palmar cutaneous branch exits proximal to the tunnel. Boards rule: thenar sensation preserved = CTS; thenar sensation lost = pronator syndrome; motor loss with zero sensory change = AIN.

cards.front: What are the sites of ulnar nerve compression at the elbow versus the wrist, and how are Guyon's canal zones organized?
cards.back: **Cubital tunnel syndrome** (elbow) — compression sites proximal to distal: the arcade of Struthers (fascial band from the medial intermuscular septum to the medial head of triceps, ~8cm proximal to the medial epicondyle — a different structure from the median nerve's ligament of Struthers), the medial intermuscular septum, the retrocondylar groove, Osborne's ligament (the cubital tunnel retinaculum roofing the tunnel between the two FCU heads), and the deep flexor-pronator aponeurosis distally. **Guyon's canal syndrome** (wrist) is organized into three zones by which branch is affected: **Zone 1** (proximal to the bifurcation) — combined motor + sensory loss. **Zone 2** (around the deep motor branch, classically from a hook of hamate fracture or a ganglion) — motor loss only (intrinsics). **Zone 3** (around the superficial sensory branch) — sensory loss only. Boards rule: elbow = Struthers/Osborne's/FCU aponeurosis; wrist = Guyon's zones 1/2/3 — remember zone 2 is motor (hook of hamate), zone 3 is sensory.

cards.front: How do radial tunnel syndrome (PIN compression) and Wartenberg syndrome differ in nerve branch, presentation, and compression site?
cards.back: **Radial tunnel/PIN syndrome** — compression of the posterior interosseous nerve (the deep motor branch), most commonly at the arcade of Frohse (fibrous proximal edge of the supinator), also possibly the fibrous edge of ECRB or the leash of Henry (radial recurrent vessels). Pure motor: finger and wrist extensor weakness, with the wrist extending into radial deviation because ECRL (which branches off before the PIN split) is spared; **no sensory loss**, since the PIN is motor-only distal to its branch point. The pain-only variant without motor deficit (radial tunnel syndrome proper) is often mistaken for lateral epicondylitis — tenderness sits 3-4 fingerbreadths distal to the lateral epicondyle over the mobile wad, worsened by resisted supination or the middle-finger extension test. **Wartenberg syndrome** — compression of the superficial radial nerve (sensory only) as it emerges from under brachioradialis in the distal forearm, often from a tight watch/handcuff/cast or crossing with the APL/EPB tendons (an association with De Quervain's release). Presents with dorsoradial hand/thumb numbness only, **no motor loss**. Boards rule: PIN = motor only (mimics lateral epicondylitis); Wartenberg = sensory only (mimics De Quervain's).

cards.front: What supplies blood to the scaphoid, and why does fracture location predict avascular necrosis risk?
cards.back: The dorsal carpal branch of the radial artery enters the scaphoid through the dorsal ridge and supplies roughly 70-80% of the bone, **including the entire proximal pole, via retrograde flow** — blood enters distally at the waist/dorsal ridge and flows backward to nourish the proximal fragment. A separate volar branch (from the superficial palmar arch) supplies only the distal pole/tuberosity. Because the proximal pole depends entirely on retrograde flow through the waist, a fracture at or proximal to the waist can cut off its only blood supply. AVN risk by location: **proximal pole fractures — up to 100%**; waist fractures (~65% of all scaphoid fractures) — intermediate risk; distal pole/tuberosity fractures (~10%) — low risk (~30-33%), since the distal fragment keeps its own separate supply. Boards rule: more proximal fracture location = higher AVN risk, because of the single dominant, retrograde dorsal supply.

cards.front: How do proximal fragment vascularity and humpback deformity together determine the bone graft choice for scaphoid nonunion?
cards.back:
A four-cell decision matrix based on two independent variables — **is the proximal fragment vascular (no AVN) or avascular (AVN)?** and **is there a humpback deformity (volar angulation/flexion collapse with DISI)?**
- **Vascular, no humpback** → standard ORIF ± non-structural cancellous autograft; a vascularized graft is not required because the bone is already alive.
- **Vascular, with humpback** → the problem is shape, not blood supply — correct it with a **structural wedge graft from the iliac crest** (non-vascularized is sufficient since the fragment is viable).
- **AVN, no humpback** → the problem is blood supply, not shape — a **pedicled vascularized graft, classically the 1,2-intercompartmental supraretinacular artery (1,2 ICSRA) graft** from the distal radius, is the workhorse.
- **AVN and humpback** → both problems exist simultaneously — this is the indication for a **free vascularized medial femoral condyle (MFC) graft**, which provides a large corticoperiosteal/corticocancellous segment on its own arterial pedicle (descending genicular artery) for microvascular anastomosis, correcting the deformity while revascularizing the fragment in one construct.
Boards rule: match the graft to the deficit — vascularity problem → vascularized pedicle graft; structural problem → structural graft; both problems → free vascularized graft (MFC), not a local pedicled option.

cards.front: Why is the 4+5 extensor compartmental artery (ECA) graft favored over the 1,2 ICSRA graft for Kienbock's disease?
cards.back: Both are dorsal, distal-radius-based vascularized bone graft options — 1,2 ICSRA was originally described for scaphoid nonunion, and both have been adapted for lunate revascularization in Kienbock's disease. The **4+5 ECA graft has a longer, larger-caliber pedicle** than the 1,2 ICSRA graft. The 1,2 ICSRA's pedicle is short and radially based — well suited to reach the nearby scaphoid — while the 4+5 ECA's longer, more ulnar-sided pedicle gives the reach and rotational arc needed to comfortably reach the lunate, which sits more central/ulnar in the proximal carpal row. The longer pedicle also allows capsulotomy and lunate exposure without tension on, or risk to, the vascular pedicle. Boards rule: 1,2 ICSRA → scaphoid (short pedicle, radial-sided target); 4+5 ECA → lunate/Kienbock's (longer pedicle, reaches the more central/ulnar target). Pairs with Lichtman staging (hand_wrist_incorrects.md entry [7]) — vascularized grafting is a treatment option at Kienbock's stages II-IIIA.

cards.front: Why does the TFCC's foveal insertion matter more than its ulnar styloid insertion for DRUJ stability, and how does the Palmer classification organize TFCC tears?
cards.back: The TFCC has two components on its ulnar side: **deep fibers insert onto the fovea** (a depression at the base of the ulnar styloid), while **superficial fibers insert onto the tip/body of the ulnar styloid** itself. The deep, foveal component is the primary stabilizer of the distal radioulnar joint (DRUJ) — this is the basis of Atzei's "iceberg" concept, where the superficial TFCC visible on routine radiocarpal arthroscopy is only the tip, and the functionally dominant foveal (deep) portion can only be assessed via DRUJ arthroscopy or direct probing. A tear involving the deep/foveal fibers produces true DRUJ instability regardless of whether the superficial rim is intact, and repair requires **foveal reattachment**, not simple rim debridement. **Palmer classification** — Class 1 (traumatic): 1A, central tear of the TFC proper away from any attachment, usually not repaired if DRUJ-stable; 1B, peripheral ulnar avulsion off the fovea/styloid ± ulnar styloid fracture — the subtype most likely to destabilize the DRUJ; 1C, distal avulsion off the volar ulnocarpal ligaments (ulnolunate/ulnotriquetral); 1D, radial-sided avulsion ± sigmoid notch fracture. Class 2 (degenerative): progressive ulnocarpal impaction spectrum associated with positive ulnar variance. Boards rule: the deep/foveal fibers are the ones that matter for DRUJ stability; Class 1B tears are the ones most associated with instability and most often need foveal repair rather than debridement.

cards.front: What is the maximum safe resection during radial styloidectomy, and what structure does exceeding it destabilize?
cards.back: Resection should not exceed **4mm**. The radioscaphocapitate (RSC) ligament originates just 4mm from the tip of the radial styloid (the long radiolunate ligament originates further proximal, around 10mm). Because the RSC ligament is the primary volar restraint against ulnar translation of the carpus, resecting beyond 4mm strips its origin and produces iatrogenic carpal ulnar translocation — trading a treatable radial-sided impingement problem for a much harder carpal instability problem. Biomechanical testing shows a significant jump in radiocarpal translation once resection reaches or exceeds 6mm.
